### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-10
> Guys, andLinux already works on Windows using a custom kernel and some special configuration. AND, it's running Edgy. 

We ARE running edgy, we don't need a custom kernel, no special configuration (currently), just a custom initrd that's already complete. It already installs fine on Windows with this implementation. We don't want to have it actually working ON Windows; colinux and vmware do that fine. Andlinux is based on colinux; it runs under windows; we don't aim to do that, just install under windows. Completely different objectives.

> I've gotten Gnome, KDE, Fluxbox, and XFCE fully operational aside from some configuration issues with startup scripts

Us too. Gnome works out of the box, kde, fluxbox, and xfce can be installed with a simple apt-get, and they all work flawlessly, no issues whatsoever

> and the fact that it only runs in root at the moment

That is a HUGE problem. Do you realize how many issues there are with running as root? Not just security, but with the system itself; many apps refuse to run as root. And if you don't have proper filesystem permissions implemented (because you are running as root) that means a LOT of stuff is going to get broken, unless you hack away at it

> I run it constantly, and performance is very good

Performance is very good here too; no noticeable difference from standard ubuntu. By the way, you're using colinux (as the page in [http://wiki.gp2x.org/wiki/AndLinux](http://wiki.gp2x.org/wiki/AndLinux) says), so by definition, performance will suffer, because you're running 2 operating systems, while here, the only performance degradation would be from using a loopmounted filesystem, which only has a very slight, unnoticeable impact on performance

> Perhaps along with the installation stuff you guys are working on that this can become an extension of it.

Uh, I don't think that would be useful to you or us. You apparently don't understand what we're trying to do. We're trying to get ubuntu installed under windows, not running under windows. Completely different objective, completely different technology used. We're not emulating with colinux; we're running the real deal, only without partitioning or burning a cd. I'd say we'd best stay separate; we aim for completely different objectives.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-10
> I was thinking to change the installation process as follows:

    * have a cywing file to execute most of the installation tasks
    * use cygwin to create an empty image file
    * from cygwin loopmount the ISO file and the image file
    * copy the relevant files from the ISO file to the image file (similar to standard ubuntu installer, probably it is even possible to use the standard ubuntu installer directly)
    * execute relevant scripts/apply patches/set settings (similar to standard ubunu installer)
    * copy relevant windows files into image (settings/my documents/emails/contacts...)
    * copy kernel/initrd to windows folder


This way the image file is created in a custom way on-the-fly and you only have to download the standard ISO image.

That would add an awful lot of space to the download size, and an awful lot of time to the installation time. Not a good idea, I think, and why bother with all the effort of doing all that if a preconfigured disk image works fine? If bandwidth is truly the issue, we can just continue to rely on bittorrent; more people downloading means more people seeding. I'm looking into places for good http hosting, so the bandwidth issue will (hopefully) soon be resolved. We shouldn't be wasting our efforts on trying to create a disk image under cygwin, when it's faster and better as it currently is, with everything preconfigured. Also, Windows can't write to a loopmounted ext3 partition. EXT2IFS and EXT2FSD won't help; they only write to real partitions, not disk images. We'd have to write the code for this ourselves, a lot of extra work and hassle. This would introduce a whole lot of issues, increase installation time, make everything more complex, and not have any practical benefits apart from conserving bandwidth, which can be solved by using bittorrent as we already are, and finding another host for http downloads. Not a good idea, I think.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-11
> you would solve the security problem since there would be no need to pass around a text file with the password, it would be easier to import files/settings

Those 2 issues can already be easily solved, using the existing method

The security issue: create a file in /etc/rcS.d/S99security in the disk image

```
rm /ntfs/ubuntu/config.ini
rm /etc/rcS.d/S99security
```

chmod +x /etc/rcS.d/S99security

in other words, it'll just remove the file with the password on the first boot, and since you're going to be booting from ubuntu right after installing (or else why would you install), it would just remove the password-containing file (right after adding the user using a separate script), so there would be no security issue

The import setting from windows: create a file in /etc/rcS.d/S99import in the disk image

Have the installer import and convert the files, then store them in C:\ubuntu\import\evolution and C:\ubuntu\import\firefox

```
mv /ntfs/ubuntu/import/evolution ~/.evolution
mv /ntfs/ubuntu/import/firefox ~/.mozilla
rm /etc/rcS.d/S9import
```

chmod +x /etc/rcS.d/S99import

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-11
Hello1024, use this menu.lst instead, and don't rename the files. why? we can symlink /boot to C:\ubuntu, allowing for kernel upgrades, and even full-fledged dist-upgrades to work under this system, but if it's just called "kernel" or "initrd", apt will fail when upgrading the kernel or dist-upgrading

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-12
> I am concernced you'll end up reinventing d-i... I think that using a standard ISO image/netinstall + automated d-i is a better approach.

As I was saying, windows can't write to the loopmounted ext3 disk image, so this is simply not an option. As for CoLinux or netinstall, that would just defeat the entire purpose by making installation even harder. Everything needs to be preconfigured. We won't be reinventing the wheel; all we have to do is just run the same scripts ubunu uses in its installer, only run them on the first boot instead of within the installer. I just need to hunt for the shell scripts it runs, and add them to /etc/rcS.d, then remove them after they run. It won't be a revolutionary change; in fact, once the system boots for the first time, all the user should be left with is a standard ubuntu, with no changes whatsoever.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-13
The aim is that this installation should be risk-free, and therefore we don't want to change any partitions.  No PC's have any unpartitioned space on when new from an OEM, and if anyone has previously repartitioned the system, it implies they are enough of a power user to use anoher install method.

Remember we are actually using an ext3 file system, just loopmounted inside whatever filesystem is already existing (which in this case is ntfs) - that way you get all the benefits of a linux filesystem, without having to repartition or fiddle with installing boot loaders to the MBR, both of which are risky things.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-14
**THIS THREAD IS A CONTINUATION OF THE THREAD AT [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=305109](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=305109)**

We have a development version of a Windows based installer for Ubuntu which is designed to be:
[LIST]
[*]very simple to use
[*]provide a no-risk installation
[*]no repartitioning the hard disk
[*]no changing the windows boot loader
[*]Easy uninstallation.
[*]Low risk
[*]Doesn't use a virtual machine
[*]Doesn't significantly reduce performance of the resulting installation
[*]Resulting installation supports everything a normal install does.
[*]Fast install
[/LIST]

[CENTER]*************************[/CENTER]

[COLOR="Red"]**NOTE THAT THE VERSION BELOW HAS LOTS OF BUGS AT THE MOMENT, AND WE RECCOMEND YOU WAIT UNTIL THE NEXT VERSION BEFORE TRYING IT OUT**[/COLOR]

**Currently there is a prototype of the installer. see [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype) for download and install instructions.**

See [HERE]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype#head-413f9c7ad73a27ec42a4a99bae9c18f42c085a1e") for troubleshooting suggestions.  Note that the developers are busy working on the next version, and most likely will not be here to give support for the current release.

[CENTER]*************************[/CENTER]

The aim of this installer is to provide an easier way for a Windows user to install Ubuntu without having to know how to burn a cd iso, set the bios to boot from cd, repartition the disks, set up a multiboot system, etc.  It will not replace any of the current Ubuntu installation options, and will not require that windows is installed prior to the installation of Ubuntu.

The installer works by creating a disk image of a pre-installed ubuntu system on the hard disk (downloaded with a bittorrent downloader integrated into the installer, or a standard http download when we find mirrors), and then installing GRUB for windows, which can be chain loaded by the existing boot loader, and which then loads the linux kerner and initrd from the ntfs partition.  The initrd is modified to support mounting the image file mentioned above as a root file system, and then continuing the boot process like a normal installation.

This does not use a virtual machine to run linux on, so the performance of the resulting system will be similar to the performance of any other linux installation.  The system will use ext3 in the image file, so users will get all the benefits of a linux filesystem.

**_Current progress_**
A prototype has been made (see [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype)) which works (WITH LOTS OF UNRESOLVED BUGS), however there are still a few unresolved issues:

GUI Installer:
[LIST]
[*]UPNP
[*]Advanced/Expert install
[*]Langage support
[*]slideshow during install
[*]Disk space checks required
[*]Support for installing on a secondary drive
[*]Supporting installation of boot loader where the boot drive isn't c:\
[/LIST]

First boot:
[LIST]
[*]Importing settings from config.ini file
[/LIST]

For more details, refer to the following links:

Launchpad entry here:  [https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/windows-installer](https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/windows-installer) 
Specification here:  [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe) 
Comments here:  [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Comments](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Comments) 
Prototype here:  [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype)

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-14
> Full write access to loopmounted partition

Remove that, that's done...

> Hardware Detection

Hardware detection (xorg and network) coming up, I'm uploading a new release, I'll have the link soon. If you're too impatient, just run these commands on the first boot through a shell script:

```
dpkg-reconfigure -fnoninteractive xserver-xorg
dpkg-reconfigure dhcp3-client
/etc/init.d/networking restart
```

Not possible; we'll need to use a swap file instead. That's included in the new release I'm uploading at this instant

### Post by rai4shu2 on 2007-01-14
Maybe this is a silly question, but wouldn't it make more sense distribution-wise to use the squashfs image in the casper folder of the desktop CD? It seems to me that all you'd need are a few patches here and there.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-14
> Maybe this is a silly question, but wouldn't it make more sense distribution-wise to use the squashfs image in the casper folder of the desktop CD? It seems to me that all you'd need are a few patches here and there.

That's what the original (current) release uses, but the main issue is that the bootup scripts are filled with unionfs and squashfs junk, so the issue is that on shutdown, nothing is saved. For this reason, the release I'm uploading now is based off a standard ubuntu install, with a few added scripts for hardware detection and booting off a loopmounted filessytem. Also, we can't use squashfs, because it's a read-only filesystem.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-15
Ok, new release, folks

[http://st0rage.org/~ubuntu/](http://st0rage.org/~ubuntu/)

new features:

swap working
now based off a real hard drive install, so changes are actually saved
xorg autoconfiguration
network autoconfiguration
switched from ext2/3 to reiserfs, due to decreased chance of corruption
split /etc and /home from / for easier updates and more flexibility

Hello1024 should have the installer ready sometime soon, but for you early adopters:

download all files, unzip them, for the ones ending in .001, .002, .003 just catonate before decompressing

cat ubuntu.img.gz.001 ubuntu.img.gz.002 ubuntu.img.gz.003 >ubuntu.img.gz

in windows, 7-zip should do the job, just right-click .001, extract here

place etc.img home.img initrd.img* vmlinuz* swap.img ubuntu.img in C:\ubuntu

place menu.lst grub grldr in C:\

add this to C:\boot.ini

C:\grldr="Ubuntu"

part 2 and 3 of the disk image are still uploading, but feel free to download part 1 for now... the others should be uploaded by midnight pacific time

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-15
There are two ways it works:
Either the small installer downloads the content
Or, the large installer just runs itself, and self-extracts the content.

The small installer is this one:
[http://omattos.co.uk/setup-ubuntu.exe](http://omattos.co.uk/setup-ubuntu.exe)

and the large installer can be downloaded with this torrent:
[http://omattos.co.uk/ubuntu.torrent](http://omattos.co.uk/ubuntu.torrent)
(we don't yet have a normal http download for this, although one will be coming very soon)

Both installers visually work in exactly the same way, except with the large one you don't require an internet connection, and don't have to wait for the download.

Note that at the moment the large installer can't be run directly from the cd because it tries to make a temporary file in it's own directory - I'm working on a fix for that right now.  As a workaround, you can copy it from the cd to the desktop before running it. 

You might want to wait a few hours before downloading it, because tuxcantfly is just uploading a new release, which I'll compile into a new version of the installer.

### Post by petervk on 2007-01-15
This looks like a great idea. Kudo's to you for starting such a project.

The only concern I have is the file system, you said:
> switched from ext2/3 to reiserfs, due to decreased chance of corruption

ext3 is the standard fs for every major linux distribution. (Red Hat, Fedora, SUSE, Debian, and Ubuntu) I don't know all the technical reasons for the ext3 vs. Reiserfs debate, but my main interest is that this project should follow the mainline Ubuntu for most of its decisions to increase its reputation and adoption. (and their default choices as well) Then this project will become a true alternative install.

Another question, you use custom hd image files, is it possible to instead use the official Ubuntu live cd images, and extract the squashfs image contained within them? Their will have to be some changes made to the images, but this may be another route...

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-15
> ext3 is the standard fs for every major linux distribution. (Red Hat, Fedora, SUSE, Debian, and Ubuntu) I don't know all the technical reasons for the ext3 vs. Reiserfs debate, but my main interest is that this project should follow the mainline Ubuntu for most of its decisions to increase its reputation and adoption. I personally think that for this project to be really successful you will have to match the Ubuntu live cd install as closely as possible. (and their default choices as well) Then this project will become a true alternative install.

Ubuntu lets you install using reiserfs as well... ext3 isn't exactly the "standard"

I agree that we should be using ext3, but the major issue is that I'm having issues getting ext3 unmounted on reboot; it constantly gets itself corrupted, while reiserfs doesn't. If someone manages to get ext3 not to corrupt itself on reboot, I'll switch back, but until then, it'll have to be reiserfs

> Another question, you use custom hd image files, is it possible to instead use the official Ubuntu live cd images, and extract the squashfs image contained within them? Their will have to be some changes made to the images, but this may be another route...

That's what the original release uses, but the main issue is that the bootup scripts are filled with unionfs and squashfs junk, so the issue is that on shutdown, nothing is saved. For this reason, the newer release is based off a standard ubuntu install, with a few added scripts for hardware detection and booting off a loopmounted filessytem. Also, we can't use squashfs, because it's a read-only filesystem. Also, Windows can't read squashfs, and windows can't write to loopmounted ext3/reiserfs partitions, so we can't just have it extract the contents of the squashfs filesystem and dump it into a disk image.

### Post by UBfusion on 2007-01-15
I got the torrent, and copied it to my VM guest (XP SP2 x86). Host is x64 (E6300)

Ubuntu installation was extremely speedy and worked out of the box.

The initial splash screen and desktop is 1920x1440 and needed dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg. After this step, desktop is 1024x768, but initial splash screen (before the login screen) is still 1920x1440. No big problem.

After logging in, the desktop experience is amazingly fast. I would say that the response and feel is almost as fast as the VM host's. Amazing.

Network within the VM seemed somewhat problematic. The virtual network card was recognized properly, but in network utilities there is no card configured. I did not manage to make network work.

Next I thought it would be wise to install vm-tools. Unfortunately I could not get neither CD or USB drives to mount in the VM. The errors complained about no "suid root". My guess is that this problem has to do with the forced use of the ubuntu/ubuntu account and the fixed name of the machine and possibly the chmod of some files.

I guess that the above glitches are virtualisation artifacts and peculiarities which won't appear when installing in a 'real' environment. All I wanted to see with my eyes was that the ubuntu installation part works in a sandbox environment and all I can say is that it really works much better than I anticipated! A big bravo to the team!

My priorities right now are getting Feisty to install properly on my box, but I'll be definitely be watching the whole effort, especially when it moves to Feisty.

My estimate that this installer thing is BIG and will make it in the front page of every news site on the planet and help spread the word to people afraid to mess with their system. It's the best thing after the invention of the live CD - install it, dual boot with it, and clean uninstall it - a real demonstration heaven. Next step is to make ubuntu coexist with windoze... This will be real revolution and it's not so far away as it seems!

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-15
> I don't know all the technical reasons for the ext3 vs. Reiserfs debate

Mainly, it's the online resizing ability of reiserfs. From [http://www.namesys.com/fs.html](http://www.namesys.com/fs.html)

```
resize

    used for on-line ReiserFS resizing. To grow a ReiserFS online, use

        mount -o remount,resize=NEW_BLOCKCOUNT /mnt
```

in other words, if you run out of space on your loopmounted partition, it can just expand itself, without even needing to reboot or anything. ext3, on the other hand, is much more difficult to expand/resize

### Post by Drakia on 2007-01-16
> **BOBSONATOR said:**
> 1. Wait, so i can download this on my windows computer that has a 80gb NFTS partition
2.  and it will edit that partition to lets say 70gbNFTS 10GB ext3?
3.  and will it install grub so i can select either windows or ubuntu to boot????

No, it creates a disc image on your current Windows partition (No repartitioning is done at all) then installs Grub for Windows onto the C: drive (It does not overwrite the current MBR) And then puts an entry into boot.ini (So your computer is still using the Windows boot manager) to load Linux using Grub

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-16
> Nice to hear this! If this driver is stable, why isn't it included in the linux kernel?

FUSE, filesystem in userspace

in other words, you simply can't include it in the kernel

I bypassed this limitation through an enormous hack on the initrd; basically stuffed all of fuse into the initrd, so it can mount ntfs using fuse on boot

### Post by ago on 2007-01-16
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> As I was saying, windows can't write to the loopmounted ext3 disk image, so this is simply not an option.

But Linux can, if you can't use cygwin you can surely access the image files rw when you reboot into Linux. That is how Topologilinux works and how your system works. When you reboot, the initrd uses losetup to create a loop device and formats the image file, then it mounts the root.img and loopmounts the livecd.iso, and finally it runs the installer using the files in the iso and targeting root.img. In the Ubuntu case, the main difference is that you also import preseed.cfg (generated by the nsis wizard) then you run the debian-installer with the preseed targeting the image file. 

There is no need whatsoever to create a custom images server-side, you can use instead a single custom initrd (and by the way, the hd-media initrd is a very good starting point for the purpose) and with it you can reuse ANY official ISO file and get an installation that is 100% identical to the official one (other than the initrd obviously). An official hd-media initrd and an official automatic installer are already available. This means that you only maintain the modifications of the initrd and the nsis files within your project, so less bandwidth, less work and a more robust setup using tried and tested technologies without reinventing the wheel. 

Moreover  the same installer will work out of the box for any Ubuntu flavor, any architecture and most versions, while in your case you have to generate and maintain a new image for each and every version. And for what? To gain a few minutes during installation? The approach above will take less than a standard LiveCD installation, no big deal.

> I just need to hunt for the shell scripts it runs, and add them to /etc/rcS.d, then remove them after they run.
What about using preseed instead, that was designed for automatic installations, and what about modifying the existing hd-media initrd image (already designed for hd-media installation) as opposed to modifying the whole ISO file?

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-16
[CENTER]*********************************************
**NEW INSTALLER VERSION RELEASED - SEE BELOW FOR LINKS**
*********************************************[/CENTER]

Hi,

I've finally got the installer working.  Here are the changes:

Full support for edubuntu, xubuntu, etc., although as there are no disk images for these yet they don't boot - they're good for testing the installer\uninstaller though as they're small downloads.

CRC checks on the download to check if the file download is incomplete.

Torrent downloader progress

Fixed some icons etc.

And most importantly the new version of tuxcantfly's disk images, which bring all the advantages he posted about.



To answer some questions:

Yes, there may be problems on windows XP x64 or Vista.  If someone on windows XP x64 could send me the boot files (the small hidden files in the root of c:), I might be able to figure a way to get it working there.

The disk space requirements are 7GB of free space at the moment.  It will use just over 6GB when installed.

Mrsilver:  Just above the [Operating Systems] line add a line that says timeout=30


Some notes on manuall installation:
  If you manually install, you'll have to also manually uninstall (reverse the manual install process)
  You won't break your computer as long as you only add a single line to the bottom of the boot.ini file - don't edit the lines that are already there unless you know what you're doing

To clarify the installer versions thing - the big install and the little install have exactly the same code, so there should be no difference in the results obtained from the two.


So, here's the links:

[CENTER]*********************************************
**NEW INSTALLER VERSION RELEASED**
*********************************************[/CENTER]

Large all-inclusive version:

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-16
Yes, you can manualy install with less disk space.

Go through the manual install procedure - put the grub, grldr and menu.lst files in c:\.  Put the other files in d:\ubuntu.  Edit the bottom of the menu.lst file to change all refrences of hda1 to hda2  and sda1 to sda2 (presuming d: is a partition on the same physical disk as c:).  Ask tuxcantfly if you need more help, because I'm still a bit unsure of how all the wizardry of his bit works - It looks like he's done a massive amount of development work since the old version.

Yep, I'll be putting a feature for alternate drives in the expert install section soon.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-16
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> As I was saying, windows can't write to the loopmounted ext3 disk image, so this is simply not an option.

But Linux can, if you can't use cygwin you can surely access the image files rw when you reboot into Linux. That is how Topologilinux works and how your system works. When you reboot, the initrd uses losetup to create a loop device and formats the image file, then it mounts the root.img and loopmounts the livecd.iso, and finally it runs the installer using the files in the iso and targeting root.img. In the Ubuntu case, the main difference is that you also import preseed.cfg (generated by the nsis wizard) then you run the debian-installer with the preseed targeting the image file. 

There is no need whatsoever to create a custom images server-side, you can use instead a single custom initrd (and by the way, the hd-media initrd is a very good starting point for the purpose) and with it you can reuse ANY official ISO file and get an installation that is 100% identical to the official one (other than the initrd obviously). An official hd-media initrd and an official automatic installer are already available. This means that you only maintain the modifications of the initrd and the nsis files within your project, so less bandwidth, less work and a more robust setup using tried and tested technologies without reinventing the wheel. 

Moreover  the same installer will work out of the box for any Ubuntu flavor, any architecture and most versions, while in your case you have to generate and maintain a new image for each and every version. And for what? To gain a few minutes during installation? The approach above will take less than a standard LiveCD installation, no big deal.

> I just need to hunt for the shell scripts it runs, and add them to /etc/rcS.d, then remove them after they run.
What about using preseed instead, that was designed for automatic installations, and what about modifying the existing hd-media initrd image (already designed for hd-media installation) as opposed to modifying the whole ISO file?

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-16
JRegan1990:
Hopefully if official developers see the benefits of this.

tuxcantfly:
I've had a few more ideas which might help solve a few things, if they're possible to implement on your side:

We add an extra kernel parameter to the menu.lst file, for example "firstbootautoconfig", which will then mean the initrd ignores the location it's given to find the ubuntu.img, and instead searches on all partitions of all disks till it finds it.  When it's found, it then modifies the menu.lst file to refrence the right place (or we could even just autodetect the location of ubuntu.img and the other files on EVERY boot, although that prevents you effectively having two installations on the same pc, and might add time to the startup time).

That saves us meddling with multiple entries for IDE or sata disks, and also allows it to be installed on any drive or partition from windows, and the windows installer doesn't need to know the partition number and physical disk number (which are both fairly hard to accuratly figure out from within windows).

---

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-16
My choice would be to stick with NSIS unless there's a good reason to change.  I wouldn't re-invent the wheel by trying to start a new installer from scratch, although I am having a few issues with NSIS's large file compression (filed a bug report), and if that can't be fixed then we'll have to figure a work around, which may involve changing to another installer.  At the moment, the installer uses a 2 stage extraction workaroud, which doubles the installation time, so it will be quite important for the final release that we get something better.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-16
> Btw is there 64-bit support?

If you mean it will work on an amd64 system, yes. If you mean it will boot a 64 bit ubuntu, not yet. Shouldn't be too much work to have it running native 64 bit ubuntu; just replace every binary in the disk image with a 64 bit version, but bandwidth and hosting are the issue. Also, is it really practical to provide an amd64 version? This is meant for inexperienced users, who certainly don't know how to cope with all the hacks and issues with using a real 64 bit ubuntu; flash hacks, video codec hacks, etc. The only benefit of using real 64 bit ubuntu is a slight performance boost; if you really want to max out performance, just install the normal way.

> I'm not sure if they has been brought to anyone's attention or if it has been fixed in the installer v3

Yes, it's been brought to our attention, and yes, it's been fixed

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-17
> How would i get rid of my current Ubuntu installation? its a normal dualboot and i want to switch to this. 

Why would you want to do that? This is simply meant for people who can't/won't dual boot; if you're already dual booting, you really shouldn't switch... also, there's probably a whole spew of issues with this installation method; not exactly sure about what; there hasn't been extensive testing, so it's likely filled with bugs. I'd say stick with your dual boot. If you insist on switching, though (don't say I didn't warn you):

boot up with a windows recovery disk

go into the recovery command line

fixmbr

exit

boot up a live cd

delete your existing ubuntu partition, and shrink your ntfs partition; ntfsresize in ntfsprogs should do the job

reboot into windows, install using the installer

### Post by ago on 2007-01-17
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Good point, great idea, only problem is, I have no idea how this ubuntu installer works, and don't know how to give it the necessary parameters to use the loopmounted filesystem as the root

I can help on that:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/6.10/ubuntu/installation-guide/i386/appendix-preseed.html](https://help.ubuntu.com/6.10/ubuntu/installation-guide/i386/appendix-preseed.html)

The way you pass the parameters is to create a preseed.cfg file. See the link for info on the formatting.

The way to create the preseed.cfg file is fairly simple. You start with a template file where you have placeholders for the variables eg:

d-i debian-installer/locale string %LOCALE%

Then after you run the nsis wizard, and collect the info, you run a string substitution on the template file, in the case above replacing %LOCALE% with 'en_US'. One of the parameters is going to be the device on which to install and you can use /dev/loop7 (where you losetup root.img), which can be preset in the template itself.

You place the preseed.cfg in the same folder containing the disk images. 

When you boot in Linux in installation mode, after loopmounting the root image and the iso file, you also copy the preseed.cfg file within Linux, you then run the standard debian-installer passing the pressed.cfg you generated and it will take care of the rest using the iso file as a source for the packages. The basic task is to merge hd-media initrd with the one you have, i.e. to add the loopmounting script to hd-media. You can find hd-media initrd here [http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/feisty/main/installer-i386/current/images/hd-media/](http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/feisty/main/installer-i386/current/images/hd-media/)

> Also, everything won't work unmodified; we'll still need to tweak the fstab
That should be taken care of by the debian-installer.

> some of the shutdown scripts
You can add custom scripts to preseed.cfg without changing the ISO image. see [https://help.ubuntu.com/6.10/ubuntu/installation-guide/i386/preseed-intro.html#preseed-hooks](https://help.ubuntu.com/6.10/ubuntu/installation-guide/i386/preseed-intro.html#preseed-hooks)

> but you have a point; if we do this, we'll conserve bandwidth, and this is a much lower-maintainence, long-term solution. Also, it'll increase our chances of becoming an officially supported ubuntu installation method, because currently, this is all just a giant hack. If you know how to do it, mind uploading it, or at least giving us the proper info?
I explained the process above, I hope it is exhaustive enough, feel free to ask any question. I can work on it tonight or tomorrow.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-17
> **Paloseco said:**
> I agree that using the same ISO for both normal installation and for this setup would be a good enhancement since users only need a cd or dvd. But for now the thing I consider most important is to get the installed/uninstaller running as flawlessly as possible. Using a direct download for all the files is also necessary since lot of users hate to download free software from P2P networks. And you cannot download it in universities or places where P2P are blocked or you are behind a firewall.

Using debian-installer as opposed to custom image+custom scripts is probably the best way to ensure flawless installation, since the installation will be basically identical to a standard one, so you benefit of all the testing going on upstream. That should also speed up development since lots of current issues have been already addressed in the debian-installer. That will also solve the download problem, since you will be using standard ISO files you can get such files from the official mirrors via ftp/http/or torrent, the installer itself will only contain: kernel, initrd, grldr and nsis script, which is peanuts. ### Post by ago on 2007-01-17
Well looking at the code, the above will not work since you need to pass the ROOT parameter to the kernel line (kernel /ubuntu/kernel root=/dev/XYZ ro quiet splash), which in turns is parsed by init and then used by ntfs-ng.  This means that the initrd must also be changed so that $ROOT points to the device on which initrd is located, which can be found by trial and error.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-17
> **Kristho said:**
> Ok... But where do I fint the menu.lst file when I'm sitting in Windows?

C:\ 
but be aware that at the moment you still need to know the parameter in root=/dev/XXX you can try different variants by replacing XXX with hda1, hda2, hdb1, sda1...  (letter is drive number, number is partition number, h/s is hd type)

title Ubuntu
find --set-root /ubuntu/kernel
kernel /ubuntu/kernel root=/dev/XXX ro quiet splash
initrd /ubuntu/initrd
boot

I will submit a patch later on to spare you the trouble

### Post by foxtyke on 2007-01-17
Okay, not sure which installer I got working but I got one of them going and here's what I had to do:

[LIST]
[*]Uncheck Read-Only on /ubuntu (which Windows reapplied but it saved me from having to do it to individual files within the folder)
[*]Uncheck Read-Only on boot.ini
[*]Press ESC a few times after selecting Ubuntu before boot came on the screen and selecting HDA1, otherwise hang at splash[/LIST]
Once I did that, I got into the actual environment and found I did need to adjust my monitor a bit and that for whatever reason, I can't mount my CD drives.

The point though is, I got in and almost everything worked (except for the mounting of drives and perhaps, lack of ndiswrapper and utils for enabling my WiFi card) which means that this project is on the right track.

I fear partioning my drives and loathe the idea of trying to setup dual-boot due to the amount of backing up I'd have to perform not to mention the time and effort of reinstalling everything again and this, this project is a near perfect solution to that.

I applaud the efforts of the developers and testers on this and look forward to future releases.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-17
ago:

Can I just check I understand what you've said so far, since a lot has been said since I last checked:

In the new plan for using d-i and doing the majority of the install on first boot from a cd image:

The user downloads an installer
The user selects various options (username etc.), which are used to put into a preseed cfg file on the ntfs drive.
The installer then downloads, or allows the user to provide, an iso image file, which is the same as the livecd(?).  The image is put in the same folder on the ntfs drive.
The installer creates an empty file to become the loopback image
The installer creates an empty file to become the swap file
The installer writes grub, grldr, and menu.lst to the root of the c: drive
The installer modifies c:\boot.ini to include the ubuntu entry
Then the system restarts to complete installation.

From there, ntldr loads grldr, which loads grub, which loads the kernel and a modified initramfs.  The init scripts in the initramfs then mount the ntfs partition, and the loopback image files, and the iso image, and then use the installer together with the preseed file to complete the installation (any ideas on timing?).  Then the iso is unmounted and deleted, and the boot process continues (or is a reboot required?), and shows the desktop.


If I've got the above correct, I think these would be nice features for the installer:
The option to download the iso image file, but then burn it to a cd, using the windows CD burning API.
The option to install to any physical drive and partition (inside the NTFS as an image file)
The option to install direct to a physical drive, rather than a loopback mount.
The option to do a repair install, to fix the boot entry in case it has been removed, say by a windows reinstall.


For the actual system itself, I think it would be a big benefit to find some way to have a dynamicly resizing loopback image, so that when I add a 10kb file to my ubuntu system, the loopback image gets 10kb bigger. (you may not want to support shrinking again for fragmentation reasons, but it's worth thinking about).  Whenever a program on ubuntu requests how much free space there is, it should be reported as the amount of space there is left on the ntfs partition that can be allocated to the loopback image.


Obviously all of my suggestions above are for a while after the initial release, but would be good targets to be aiming for.  For now, what parts of the system are you and tuxcantfly working on, and whick bits can I have a crack at?

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-17
> **Hello1024 said:**
> ago:
From there, ntldr loads grldr, which loads grub, which loads the kernel and a modified initramfs.  The init scripts in the initramfs then mount the ntfs partition, and the loopback image files, and the iso image, and then use the installer together with the preseed file to complete the installation (any ideas on timing?).
Spot on.

The timing of the installation should be comparable to a standard LiveCD installation, if anything it will be  less than that becuse the files are on HD. I would estimate ~20minutes, which should be more than an acceptable (far less than Vista).

 > Then the iso is unmounted and deleted, and the boot process continues (or is a reboot required?), and shows the desktop.
Once the image is built you can also chroot straight into it, since the kernel used is the same, therefore a reboot is not required. An additional and optional step might be to modify menu.lst in the windows partition, this is because you want to boot into setup-mode the first time and into default Ubuntu mode the second time and onward. You can also use grub savedefault command, but it is probably safer to modify menu.lst after a successful installation, so that the user does not accidentally reinstall over an existing system.

> The option to download the iso image file, but then burn it to a cd, using the windows CD burning API.
The option to install to any physical drive and partition (inside the NTFS as an image file)
The option to install direct to a physical drive, rather than a loopback mount.
The option to do a repair install, to fix the boot entry in case it has been removed, say by a windows reinstall.
[/B]
Yes having the official ISO opens a lot of other routes.  And using d-i also has a lot of other advantages.

> For the actual system itself, I think it would be a big benefit to find some way to have a dynamicly resizing loopback image, so that when I add a 10kb file to my ubuntu system, the loopback image gets 10kb bigger.
You need to be careful with fragmentation, that slows you down a lot, unzipping the image is already an hazard since the file is not necessarily contiguous, moreover in my experience with VMs, resizeable images are way slower.  I think that is a nice feature but it is also technically complex to do right, a quick and safer hack is to create a second larger image and copy the content from the old one, but that is obviously slower.

 > For now, what parts of the system are you and tuxcantfly working on, and whick bits can I have a crack at?
I will try to create a proof of concept modifying the hd-media initrd and using the d-i with a preseed.cfg. If that work the nsis must be changed so that the values are collected and replaced into the preseed.cfg.template. Also it would be nice to have some safety checks (user rights, hd space, blacklisted hardware...), that is something you can probably add to your nsis installer as a module. 

I also have a script ready to autodetect the windows root image from within Linux so there is no need to set that in menu.lst. 


Another thing that would help is to collect all the modifications to a standard initrd within a few files (as opposed to edit existing files in different places). It should be possible to have most of the modification encapsulated as an rcS.d script, this would be easier to maintain as the initrd gets modified upstream (and it can even be included in the default initrd).

PS what is a good way to chat / exchange files? 
PS2 Given the number of people trying that I think you should activate malone in launchpad

### Post by maccam94 on 2007-01-17
> **ububug said:**
> anyone? \:

Ok, hit ctrl + alt + F1 on your keyboard when you get that message.
Type in the user name, hit enter.
Type in the password, hit enter.
type each line exactly:
sudo su
typepasswordandhitenter
/etc/init.d/?dm stop
dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg
/etc/init.d/?dm start

At this point you should be presented with the login window. Good luck!

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-17
Yep - while the uninstaller should never fail, if for some reason it does fail, this is the procedure to fully remove all the changes it made:

remove c:\ubuntu
remove the c:\grub... line from the c:\boot.ini file
remove the c:\menu.lst file
remove the c:\grub file
remove the c:\grldr file

open control panel > add remove programs > select ubuntu linux > press remove > when it offers to remove the entry from the list, click yes.


If the install failed half way through, then just removing the c:\ubuntu folder is probably all you need to do, because the installer won't have made the other changes.

### Post by maccam94 on 2007-01-17
To the devs of this project:

Make sure that with every release you update the front page download links in this thread, so new users will be downloading the correct files when they find this.

On another note, it might be a good side project to create an installer that works without requiring a disc (the main stumbling blocks I can think of in Ubuntu installations are finnicky Windows installations that won't boot and failed NTFS shrinking attempts) :
1. defrags the ntfs partition
2. extracts a modified ubuntu installation iso to an image file on the ntfs partition (using similar methods that you use now)
3. reboots the computer into the installer
4. allows the user to select a size for the ubuntu install
5. The installer resizes the NTFS partition (which should work now that the partition has been defragged)
6. The rest of the installation proceeds as normal, however GRUB is not installed to the MBR.
7. When the computer reboots, Windows starts, the installer gets the grub image from the new partition, and configures the boot.ini file.
8. Lastly, the installer removes the temporary ubuntu cd installation image, and finishes.

Tell me what you think of it, and keep up the good work!

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-17
> **maccam94 said:**
> 
On another note, it might be a good side project to create an installer that works without requiring a disc
This is what this project is about. We are talking about ISOs not discs... I.E. you work with the ISO file without burning the disk.

 > 5. The installer resizes the NTFS partition (which should work now that the partition has been defragged)
The NTFS is not touched. Ubuntu is installed within a file residing on the NTFS drive. We might be able to use a real partition if freespace or some unused partition is detected. In the future it should be possible to create a dedicated partition ex-post (optional) and dump the content of the image file within that.

> 6. The rest of the installation proceeds as normal, however GRUB is not installed to the MBR.
The MBR is not modified, nor ntldr, simply a new boot option is added in boot.ini 

> 8. Lastly, the installer removes the temporary ubuntu cd installation image, and finishes.
I am in favor of leaving the iso files, they might be handy if you want to burn a CD later on or if you screw up something and need to reinstall ubuntu.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-17
> For the actual system itself, I think it would be a big benefit to find some way to have a dynamicly resizing loopback image

online partition growth is one of the main features of reiserfs; all you do is:
```

mount -o remount,resize=NEW_BLOCKCOUNT /
```

we might be able to get this automated with some shell script and daemon

of course, we'll have to go by increments of 100MB or so; remounting reiserfs every time the disk is written to will really slow down performance, but if it only happens every 100MB or so, it should barely be noticeable.

```
you may not want to support shrinking again for fragmentation reasons, but it's worth thinking about
```

shrinking-on-the-go is not a good idea; all that defragging and remounting will kill performance.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-17
> for whatever reason, I can't mount my CD drives.

Sounds like an issue with /etc/fstab. Add this line:
```

/dev/hdc        /media/cdrom0   udf,iso9660 user,noauto     0       0
```

substitute /dev/hdc with whatever your cd drive is

> I am unable to mount my external hd

Also sounds like an fstab issue. Add this line:

```
/dev/sdb1        /media/harddrive  ext3  user     0       0
```

Replace /media/harddrive with wherever you want it to be mounted. Replace ext3 with whatever your filesystem type is. Replace /dev/sdb1 with whatever device your external hard drive is; use sudo fdisk -l to find out

The fstab stuff should be fixed once we get this installing with d-i

> my lcd display says "Out of range, 74 KHtz \ 60 htz", or something like that.

Are you using the latest release? This should've been fixed in version 3. If it still doesn't work, try this:

```
Ctrl-Alt-F1
login: ubuntu
password:ubuntu
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-17
> The installer creates an empty file to become the loopback image
The installer creates an empty file to become the swap file

I think the initrd itself should be doing this on first boot, before launching d-i; it just mounts the ntfs partition with ntfs-3g, runs dd to create an empty file on the ntfs partition, and formats the file with mkfs.reiserfs and mkswap; I don't think windows can format loopmounted disk images as reiserfs or swap. Also, we'll likely be needing another custom initrd if we're using d-i, so that the disk images are created and formatted on first boot, and d-i is launched with one initrd, and the final system is booted with the other initrd

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-17
> My point was that it would create a normal dual-boot setup, avoiding initrd hacks and filesystem images on NTFS partitions.

Impossible, I think. You have to shrink your ntfs partition somehow to do that, and ntfs can't be shrunk while in windows, so you'd have to boot into a minimal ubuntu to shrink it, and if you shrink ntfs, you're at risk of data loss.

EDIT: Nevermind, good idea, could just leave the iso in C:\, boot with the hacked initrd (just the first time), and loopmount the iso and start d-i, which can shrink ntfs, then we'll have a standard install once done. Should be a lot easier that way, too... But still, we should keep a version with the filesystem images, in case someone's partition is too fragmented to be resized, or something... also, like this, we can't just simply uninstall; you have to delete the new partition and expand the ntfs partition; chance of file loss...

---

title Ubuntu
find --set-root /ubuntu/kernel
kernel /ubuntu/kernel root=/dev/hda1 ro quiet splash
initrd /ubuntu/initrd
boot

```

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-18
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> EDIT: Nevermind, good idea, could just leave the iso in C:\, boot with the hacked initrd (just the first time), and loopmount the iso and start d-i, which can shrink ntfs
I doubt you can shrink ntfs if it is mounted... The only way is to have parted within the initrd and boot (with preseed) into a memory device without mounting anything else. The only task in this boot configuration is to shrink ntfs. After the ntfs is shrunk you can mount it, loopmount the iso, and start the standard d-i with preseed.

In fact you could try the shrink approach first and if it fails use the loopmount approach. You are right that uninstallation is an issue, but none of the other OSs has good uninstallation procedures, so I would not be too concerned about that. We could have an option in the advanced configuration to choose the installation mode: 

1. safe (do not touch bootloader/filesystem, install within a file under C:\)
2. usefreespace (if enough free space is autodetected, this will change the bootloader)
3. standard (try to resize the filesystem and change bootloader and use safe mode if it is not possible)
4. cd (burn the cd and reboot with the cd inserted)

By default we should go for 2 if there is freespace and for 1 if there is no freespace.

The good news is that d-i (with parted) is already on the initrd (hd-media), the iso is used only to provide the packages...

---

### Post by Paloseco on 2007-01-18
> **Hello1024 said:**
> ago:

Can I just check I understand what you've said so far, since a lot has been said since I last checked:

In the new plan for using d-i and doing the majority of the install on first boot from a cd image:

The installer then downloads, or allows the user to provide, an iso image file, which is the same as the livecd(?).  The image is put in the same folder on the ntfs drive.
The installer modifies c:\boot.ini to include the ubuntu entry
Then the system restarts to complete installation.


* Making a backup of the boot.ini is not a bad idea. Whe could restore that ini file when ubuntu is uninstalled, but we have to trust that the user does not install any other operating system after ubuntu and a new entry is added...

*Why do you need to restart to complete installation? From my point of view the windows installer does everything and when you restart the ntldr shows the listed operating systems and you just select ubuntu and load the gdm login or start the desktop if you have auto-login enable. This goes next:

* Enable/disable autologin from windows installer so when you boot ubuntu first time you go directly to the desktop. Any of those behaviors can be changed later in the gnome control center or so.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-18
> **Paloseco said:**
> but we have to trust that the user does not install any other operating system after ubuntu and a new entry is added...
That is why deleting the ubuntu entry from boot.ini is probably a safer approach

> *Why do you need to restart to complete installation?
Because we need to modify linux partitions (loopmounted within a file or mounted) and doing that from windows is impossible. That is required even if you use a pre-built image. Moreover you only reboot once, after installation you go straight into the new OS without a second reboot, and you have to reboot once anyway...

> when you restart the ntldr shows the listed operating systems and you just select ubuntu and load the gdm login or start the desktop if you have auto-login enable.
This is what you will see, except that the first time you run ubuntu it should take about 20min to do the actual installation (which is fully automated at this stage). The windows stage (nsis) simply collects information and downloads all the required files, then it passes the ball to the actual installer which is run under Linux in automated mode. 

> Enable/disable autologin from windows installer so when you boot ubuntu first time you go directly to the desktop. Any of those behaviors can be changed later in the gnome control center or so.
That can be inserted as an advanced option

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-18
To test out the position on the hard drive or fragmented theories, you could just copy the kernel to a new file, delete the old file, and the rename the new file back to the name of the old file.  That will find a new physical location for the file, which hopefully won't be fragmented.

The primitave support for NTFS in grub may not be able to go past the 4GB 32 bit limit, or go past the 8.4GB limit int13 has on some BIOS's

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-18
Before we had one installer for all the editions of ubuntu.  With the new d-i installer, should we carry on with this?  My main worry is if I design the installer so it can download or use any version of any edition of the iso, then we might end up in a situation where the version of d-i in the initramfs (which comes with the installer) won't be able to install whatever edition of ubuntu the packages in the iso are for.  That opens the door to a massive number of untested combinations. We don't really want to get in a situation where an install can't complete because of missing packages or a version mismatch. 
 
I was alternatively considering having one installer per edition, and then that installer will then only install with that iso, and that way we eliminate version differences issues, however it relies on a new installer version for every release (which might not be a bad thing - it means we can update the installer and initramfs to fix bugs and know that the old version can't hang around long).

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-18
> **Hello1024 said:**
> My main worry is if I design the installer so it can download or use any version of any edition of the iso
You are absolutely right, version compatibility is tricky (possible but tricky). But within the same version, any Ubuntu flavor should be supported. Matching the version of the installer to the current version of ubuntu is a very good approach. This way we can use a single kernel/initrd shipped with each version of the installer. At most we might support latest version + latest LTS (LTS only available in one flavor).

---

### Post by maccam94 on 2007-01-18
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Impossible, I think. You have to shrink your ntfs partition somehow to do that, and ntfs can't be shrunk while in windows, so you'd have to boot into a minimal ubuntu to shrink it, and if you shrink ntfs, you're at risk of data loss.

EDIT: Nevermind, good idea, could just leave the iso in C:\, boot with the hacked initrd (just the first time), and loopmount the iso and start d-i, which can shrink ntfs, then we'll have a standard install once done. Should be a lot easier that way, too... But still, we should keep a version with the filesystem images, in case someone's partition is too fragmented to be resized, or something... also, like this, we can't just simply uninstall; you have to delete the new partition and expand the ntfs partition; chance of file loss...

If you go back to my original post, I said the installer could schedule a defrag of the ntfs partition, reboot, defrag, then when Windows starts again the installer can continue.

If you want, you could maybe use ntfs-3g to save a log file for the windows installer to read later, so it could then know what partition to delete, to expand the ntfs partition how much, and then let Windows run chkdsk to expand the ntfs filesystem. I still think all of this is kind of trivial, since it would basically be a fool proof way to install Ubuntu without overwriting the Windows MBR or requiring people to burn isos to cds.

In any case, I think we should be focussing on the loopback method at the moment, because it's safest, and if a user wants ubuntu on it's own partition they probably know enough about computers to burn an iso and run the livecd installer.  It would be a nice feature to add for advanced install though.

### Post by maccam94 on 2007-01-18
> **ago said:**
> I doubt you can shrink ntfs if it is mounted... The only way is to have parted within the initrd and boot (with preseed) into a memory device without mounting anything else. The only task in this boot configuration is to shrink ntfs. After the ntfs is shrunk you can mount it, loopmount the iso, and start the standard d-i with preseed.

In fact you could try the shrink approach first and if it fails use the loopmount approach. You are right that uninstallation is an issue, but none of the other OSs has good uninstallation procedures, so I would not be too concerned about that. We could have an option in the advanced configuration to choose the installation mode: 

1. safe (do not touch bootloader/filesystem, install within a file under C:\)
2. usefreespace (if enough free space is autodetected, this will change the bootloader)
3. standard (try to resize the filesystem and change bootloader and use safe mode if it is not possible)
4. cd (burn the cd and reboot with the cd inserted)

By default we should go for 2 if there is freespace and for 1 if there is no freespace.

The good news is that d-i (with parted) is already on the initrd (hd-media), the iso is used only to provide the packages...

Yeah, it would probably have to load a small ramdisk (which would have the necessary tools) to shrink the NTFS partition. I know that if option 3 were available, I would be extremely happy, because that would make doing safe Ubuntu installs at work sooooo mcuh easier.

**Radical change idea**
Come to think of it, there's an Ext2/3 driver for XP (google IFS ext2 xp). I think it's pretty good (not sure of its exact limitations). Could the installer detect the freed space made with the ramdisk, partition it into swap and root, format them, and then do an installation WHILE INSIDE WINDOWS? Just allow the user to choose what version of Ubuntu to install, as well as enter their login name and password? The installer could probably detect the language, keyboard, and time zone settings from XP. That would mean for a very simplified install process for the user: 1. Reboot your computer (defrag). 2. Choose how much of your hard drive to dedicate to Ubuntu (save choice, generate ramdisk image, edit boot.ini to include ramdisk). 3. Reboot (ramdisk does its magic). 4. What Username and Password would you like to use in Ubuntu? (record for installation). 5. Which version of Ubuntu would you like to install? (download choice). From then on a simple progress bar should do, the installer could detect the language, timezone, and keyboard settings on its own, and with the free space would be able to be automatically partitioned into the default 2xRAM swap, everything else ext3. The installer could then insert the detected values into the normal Ubuntu installer, and just let it go. Using the IFS driver, the entire system could just be installed in Windows, and when done the installer could just say "You will now be able to choose between starting Ubuntu and Windows after starting or restarting the computer. Enjoy!"

I think this would be the best possible way to easily switch people over from Windows to Ubuntu. Just an idea I had :-k

Keep up the good work you guys!

### Post by ago on 2007-01-18
> **maccam94 said:**
> Could the installer detect the freed space made with the ramdisk, partition it into swap and root, format them, and then do an installation WHILE INSIDE WINDOWS?
Nope becase in that case we would need to recode all debian-installer for windows, and that is way beyond what nsis can do. What do you gain anyway? The only difference would be that you have to wait before rebooting as opposed to wait after rebooting...

That is already happening, the windows part of the installer is the one in charge of collecting data and passing it to the linux installer, which as explained, is non-interactive.

### Post by winter_mute on 2007-01-18
I like this idea, because sometimes people just don't understand the liveCD concept.  It's honestly a bit confusing to explain - here's a CD with an OS on it.  You can run it from the CD, but it'll be a bit slow.  If you want, you can install it, and repartition the drive to save all your... what, you don't know what repartitioning is?  Anyways, in my experience liveCDs are good to give a quick overview of the system.  But on about half of the computers I've used them on, they're noticeably slower than a native install, which I've lost potential converters because they think that Linux is slower than XP.  I think that providing a quick and painless native install option would be nice because then I can just offer them this little EXE which they don't have to do all the confusing stuff, and I probably won't need to be there in person to help them set it up exactly with the repartitioning.

Just my two cents.  It's a very nifty project, and if I ever get a computer that I need to install Ubuntu on that doesn't have Ubuntu installed, I'll be damn well sure to test it out before I use a liveCD.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-18
> IT BOOTS WINDOWS NO MATTER WHAT.... HOW DO I GET TO THE GRUB OS SELECTION SCREEN?!

Edit C:\boot.ini, and increase the "timeout" value from 0 to 10, and make sure this is 
in the last line:
```

C:\grldr="Ubuntu"
```

> However, I've run into a problem. I ran the installer, and the PC rebooted. However, no change! It gave me the two usual options (Microsoft Windows XP Home and Microsoft Windows XP Home Recovery mode) but no Ubuntu! Nothing different. Any advice?

see above; make sure you have the C:\grldr="Ubuntu" line in C:\boot.ini

> I may give Kubuntu a try because I like KDE in my one experience of using it.

For now, just install plain ubuntu with the installer, then apt-get install kubuntu-desktop; the upcoming d-i based installer should allow you to install kubuntu directly

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-18
> So one installer per version, which can install any flavour of the version?

Actually, we can have one installer for all versions; just have the installer download a different initrd for each release, as in, one initrd for k/x/ubuntu 6.10, another one for k/x/ubuntu 6.06 LTS. The installer can be the same for all; just make sure the initrd is separately downloaded and not packaged in the installer.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-18
> How to get straight to Gnome after the ubuntu boot without
hiting the ESC key and selecting the hda option?

boot up ubuntu
open a terminal
sudo gedit /media/windows/menu.lst
find every instance of "sda1" and replace it with "hda1"
save, and reboot

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-18
> 2. How to print in Linux?

Look for that in the "absolute beginner" forum; they can explain it better

> 3. How to get online in Linux?

Firefox? If the issue is your network connection, try this:

sudo dpkg-reconfigure dhcp3-client
sudo /etc/init.d/networking restart

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-18
> Any idea when the option of expert/advanced install will be available as I need to change the install drive from C

I've never actually tried this myself, but it may work:

install using the manual install method, only extract the files to D:\ubuntu instead of C:\ubuntu. Keep grldr, menu.lst, grub, and boot.ini in C:\

open C:\menu.lst, and change every occurrence of sda1 to sda2, or if you're using a pata drive, to hda2. Also change (hd0,0) to (hd0,1). If it's 2 hard drives you have, not 1 hard drive with 2 partitions, change sda1 to sdb1 or hdb1, and change (hd0,0) to (hd1,0)

> will I have any probs as I'm already dual booting with XP and Vista and this will make it a tri-boot.

Probably not; just make sure you run the installer within xp, not vista; vista has some funky new bootloader that doesn't work with grldr

### Post by skadish on 2007-01-19
This whole idea of the Windows-based installer is so amazing that it might even be something that persuades people to "upgrade" to Ubuntu instead of Vista. This is because it removes a major stumbling block for Linux beginners, namely installation. Now, all they have to do is install it the Windows way, with which they are already comfortable.

However, there are two further stumbling blocks that a program like this should also take care of, namely: (1) getting online and (2) printing (and other external hardware).

Imagine: A program like this could *detect* hardware and internet from settings within Windows, and apply them to the initial Ubuntu boot. When you boot in Ubuntu for the first time, everything works! Now THAT is something that can convince the average computer-user that there is nothing to be afraid of in Linux. That is "Linux for human beings."

Please do *not* reply that internet connection and hardware are things that should be learned by going to the "absolute beginner" forum, or something like that. I tried that, and believe me, even though people tried to be helpful it was far too much effort and confusing, and in the end I did NOT install Ubuntu. I still haven't, but I still want to. That is why hearing about the Windows installer was very exciting news for me.

What will make people like me use Ubuntu is when it is not only a simple net download that installs from Windows, but also just **works**, without going to forums or anywhere else. Take Open Office and Firefox as examples.

Good luck! I think this may be the most important step Ubuntu can take in terms of reaching out.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-19
Hi,

Firstly, sorry I haven't been very active on the forums lately.

To answer some questions that have been asked in my absense:

  The "expert" mode for the installer will be done with the next testing release, although we don't know exactly when that'll be - it all depends on how things go with getting d-i to work correctly and install to the right place.

  The hardware issues a few people have described (printers, internet) should also be resolved with the next release, because we'll be doing the same hardware detection as the standard installer uses.

  The errors a few people are getting on bootup might be resolved by repeatedly tapping escape immediatly after selecting ubuntu from the boot menu, and then try another option from the menu.  HDA1 is most likely to be the one you want.

  If after installation, there appears to be no way to start ubuntu when you restart your computer, then do the following:

Click Start
Click Run
Type:
  notepad c:\boot.ini
Press OK
Make sure the timeout=30 line is there - if it's not 30, change the number to 10 or 30.
If the line isn't there at all, add the line timeout=30 to just above the [operating systems] line.
Save the file
Exit

  When you use your ubuntu system, for the moment do not install updates to the kernel - if you don't know what that means, just avoid updates for the moment.

  If you still have trouble booting the system, report the problem (include the exact error if there is one), but note that unless we know of an easy fix, we'll just reccomend that you wait till the next version, because that'll have very big changes that'll probably fix all the errors, and cause other ones instead (but we hope many fewer overall :-))

As to when the next version will be done, it depends, but I'll have the installer working hopefully sometime this weekend or in the next week, although we also need the d-i bit to be working as well before we can call it a release.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-19
> **Hello1024 said:**
> 
it all depends on how things go with getting d-i to work correctly and install to the right place.
Good news! I have a d-i proof of concept kind-of-working (back-end only). I will updload it tonight (only for devs). It autodetects the right devices from within linux, for end users this means no more problems with having to guess your hd numbers and hack menu.lst, moreover it will make it possible to install Ubuntu from other places than C:. On the backend side, it attaches the windows files to devices (as opposed to mounting them, which makes fstab and image formatting/partitiong easier, allowing to use images of custom size), it imports the iso and preseed.cfg and then it goes on with d-i. Since the installation folder is "signed", it will also avoid complications with multiple installations. And obviously it works with standard ISOs as opposed to custom ones.

The installer itself is not useful at this stage, since more work needs to be done on the preseed parameters and d-i configuration. But it shows that the technology is sound and everything is more or less in place. Moreover, the initrd is based on hd-media which is a good initrd for setup mode but not so good for normal mode (we'll see if it is better to use 2 initrds or merge them into a single one). I also have a couple of small scripts to automate the generation of the initrd image starting from a template initrd and embedding our modifications. 

Curiously enough, I am testing from a VM Linux partition, the downside is that I have not tested ntfs-3g (but I have installed the packages in the initrd), the upside is that the loopmount backend can also be used from with Linux and it is not windows-specific. That might not be that relevant for Linux users, but Mac users might be interested...

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-19
Hello1024, would you mind adding a link to the troubleshooting section to the front post, nicely bolded so people notice?

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype#head-413f9c7ad73a27ec42a4a99bae9c18f42c085a1e](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/install.exe/Prototype#head-413f9c7ad73a27ec42a4a99bae9c18f42c085a1e)

To users: Many questions are being asked here that have already been answered before. If you're having xserver errors, network errors, booting/bootloader errors, or the like, please read the troubleshooting section and do what it says before you post a question about booting errors; it may have already been answered

> When I restarted and chose UBUNTU as OS to boot, a blinking cursor kept staring at me

Try defragmenting; the bootloader might be fragmented. Also check your boot.ini to see if it has the necessary lines mentioned in the troubleshooting section. Also, what's the last message you get before the error? Does GRUB at least show up?

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-19
> However I now don't get the login screen and I can't open login window preferences, like before the update broke it. How can I fix this?

Currently, it's configured to automatically log in. Go to System -> Administration -> Login Window, enter the password "ubuntu", go to "Security", and uncheck "Enable Automatic Login"

> and is there any way I can hide kernel updates in the update manager so I don't break it again by accident? Even better - a kernel update?

Open synaptic, find "linux-image-2.6.17-10-generic", select it, click "Package", and select "Lock Version". It's actually not the kernel itself that's the problem; it's that it regenerates the initrd incorrectly whenever it upgrades the kernel. Also make sure to "Lock Version" uspash and usplash themes, because they also regenerate the initrd. We'll hopefully have this fixed by properly configurating the settings in /etc/initramfs-tools

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-19
> The installer worked great, but when I rebooted windows and chose to run Ubuntu a "Cannot mount selected partition" problem happened. Any ideas?

Try defragmenting your hard drive, and running chdsk from windows to fix any corruption. Also try booting with the HDA1 menu selection in GRUB
> 
it's funny to me that it looks after /ntfs/ubuntu/ubuntu.img, i moved those .img files in /ntfs/ubuntu and still i get the error 

No, it's NOT looking after C:\ntfs\ubuntu\ubuntu.img; all files are supposed to be in C:\ubuntu\; don't change the location. The reason that's what it says is that /ntfs is the temporary mountpoint for the initrd; it's not the physical location.

> mount: mounting /ntfs/ubuntu/ubuntu.img on /root failed: No such file or directory
mount: mounting /ntfs/ubuntu/etc.img on /root/etc failed: No such file or directory
mount: mounting /root/dev on /dev/.static/dev failed: No such file or directory
mount: mounting /sys on /root/sys failed: No such file or directory
mount: mounting /proc on /root/proc failed: No such file or directory

target filesystem doesn't have /sbin/init

It isn't properly mounting your ntfs partition. Did you install on a different partition from the first one? If so, try changing all instances of sda1 in C:\menu.lst to sda2. If you installed on a different hard drive from the first one, try changing sda1 to sdb1. If you're not using a sata or scsi hard drive, change sda1 to hda1. Maybe your ntfs drive is just too corrupted; try running chdsk from windows and defragmenting it

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-19
> I will updload it tonight (only for devs)

Umm... All this is licensed under the GPL. Isn't it required that the code and everything be accessible to anybody, devs and non-devs alike? Just post a link to the code (but discourage everybody from using your version), so the lawyers don't start threatening us. Use our cutlersoftware page for the hosting; it's the fastest and most reliable that we currently have access to.

> Imagine: A program like this could *detect* hardware and internet from settings within Windows, and apply them to the initial Ubuntu boot. When you boot in Ubuntu for the first time, everything works! Now THAT is something that can convince the average computer-user that there is nothing to be afraid of in Linux. That is "Linux for human beings."

Yep, that's the vision... Only it will take a heck of a lot of effort to get all that hardware detection from windows working... we'd need a central ubuntu hardware database, along with all the packages and configurations needed to get each bit of hardware working automatically. Given that we've only got 3 people working on this project right now (me, ago, Hello1024), and we aren't exactly pros in the area of windows-based hardware detection (at least me, not too sure about ago and Hello1024), this'll only be happening in the distant, distant future, if ever. Help from more experienced developers is always appreciated, though...

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-20
> Is there a way I can manually reset gdm settings and possibly users? (replacing a config file?)

sudo gedit /etc/gdm/gdm.conf

find "Automatic Login" and change it from "True" to "False"

to add/remove users, use adduser and deluser

### Post by Darth_tater on 2007-01-20

im sure somebody has alread posted this, but in case they haven't (or if it didnt get noticed) id just like to say this:

1) please add support for the standard ISO's. it is very difficult for me (~ 40 Kb DL speed) to download a large image again (this was released about 18 hrs after i finished the Kubuntu DL) even useing bittorrent.    
why cant the contents of a normal ISO be unpacked in the same way that this program uses an "already installed" image?  i understand that certain hardware detection/setup scripts have to be added, but that seems like it would be quite easy to copy the scripts after the initial installation.  also it would be very nice, if i could have the option to install applications before the reboot (like automatix that "patches" the install so its all ready on reboot)  or something similar (i can build windows install CD's with the applications i need already installed on them)

2) i can understand why it might be difficult to do this in Nullsoft's installer (they dontt have a plugin for ext3 FS. do they?), but... i would really like it if i *could* use grub as my boot loader and actually install to already created partitions

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-21
> GRLDR is compressed.

That doesn't sound good... Are you running off a compressed ntfs filesystem? I'm pretty sure that won't work

EDIT: Workaround until ntfs-3g gets support for ntfs compression: disable compression for these files:
menu.lst
grldr
grub

disable compression recursively for this folder:
ubuntu

instructions at [http://support.microsoft.com/kb/925308/](http://support.microsoft.com/kb/925308/)
