### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-21
Doug:
From windows, check for the existance of c:\ubuntu\ubuntu.img, and c:\ubuntu\swap.img

If they're not there, reinstall (tip, to avoid having to re-download the file, delete all the files in c:\ubuntu except setup-ubuntu-full.exe, and then reinstall and it won't need to re-download)

If they are there, try the trick of tapping the escape key immediatly after selecting ubuntu from the startup menu, and then try another choice.


betovarg:  Presuming that from windows in "My Computer" you see a single hard disk, and from Control panel > administrative tools > computer management > Disk Management you see a single hard disk with a single partition on called "system", then it should all work.  If you don't see that, I probably wouldn't bother trying until the next version, as it's unlikely to work for now.

knilles:  Probably best to wait until the next version, because at the moment nobody has had any success with booting from anything other than the first partition.  We're working on it as a priority for the next release though.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-22
Hello1234,

The script I have submitted a few days ago (S34guestmode), is not just for d-i, but it can easily be integrated into the current version of the installer. If the script is run after the devices are activated, it will find the right device and mount all the image files in there as /dev/loop. /etc/fstab is also simplified since you mount /dev/loop7 as /, /dev/loop6 as swap, /dev/loop5 as /home.

On your side all that is required is to generate a unique key in the /ubuntu folder (as a filename) and add its value in menu.lst (using the example provided). Tuxcantfly has to include losetup support  (by adding losetup executable to the initrd available in the mount-XYZ.deb) (and obviously ntfs-3g) and execute the script in the early stages of the init process. 

This in practice will take care of half of the problems reported in this thread (manual menu.lst drive selection and having the option to install /ubuntu to a different drive). I am still working in making d-i launch properly, but as mentioned you can use the script independently.

---

### Post by Nirro on 2007-01-22
Hey, developers.

I not so much of a developer, nor expert in the init process, but maybe my idea can help you.
If you have a problem that users upgrade their kernel or startup scripts and mess with the install, maybe you can use chroot.
I mean, the install will run it's own kernel and run the init-scripts (the minimal that is needed).
Than chroot to the main ubuntu system (which preferred to be with original kernel and scripts) and finish the boot.
Thats way, users that will upgrade the system, use it in the chrooted environment and don't damage the boot.

This method of chrooting after boot is used by the Morphix liveCD developers, and in that way they can take a regular unmodified Debian system, and make it a liveCD.


Don't hesitate to tell me, if I say nonsense...

Nirro.

---

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-22
> **Nirro said:**
> I mean, the install will run it's own kernel and run the init-scripts (the minimal that is needed).
Than chroot to the main ubuntu system (which preferred to be with original kernel and scripts) and finish the boot.
That is how it works already. The main difference of the two approaches proposed (current and d-i) is whether the first time you boot, you chroot into a pre-made image which needs some additional customization, or instead, the image is built on the fly by running the debian-installer and using an empty image as a disk device, after which you chroot into it. The main advantage of the first approach is that is faster, the main advantage of the second approach is that it is closer to a standard installation and you can use default ISOs with it.

---

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-22
> **ago said:**
> Hello1234,

The script I have submitted a few days ago (S34guestmode), is not just for d-i, but it can easily be integrated into the current version of the installer. If the script is run after the devices are activated, it will find the right device and mount all the image files in there as /dev/loop. /etc/fstab is also simplified since you mount /dev/loop7 as /, /dev/loop6 as swap, /dev/loop5 as /home.

On your side all that is required is to generate a unique key in the /ubuntu folder (as a filename) and add its value in menu.lst (using the example provided). Tuxcantfly has to include losetup support  (by adding losetup executable to the initrd available in the mount-XYZ.deb) (and obviously ntfs-3g) and execute the script in the early stages of the init process. 

This in practice will take care of half of the problems reported in this thread (manual menu.lst drive selection and having the option to install /ubuntu to a different drive). I am still working in making d-i launch properly, but as mentioned you can use the script independently.

From my point of view thats actually quite easy to do, but it all depends on how long you think it'll take to get d-i working, because as soon as d-i works it saves all the hassle of trying to move large disk images around.

My current testing version of the installer allows for installation to a loopback partition on any drive, as well as allowing the use of a local iso image, or any other data source.  It allows extra customisation to the boot.ini (default boot, timeout etc.), and has advanced options to fix the boot.ini if another application/windows reinstall removes ubuntu's entry.  It also allows moving of the grldr, kernel, initrd, and a few other files in case they are fragmented, or otherwise running windows defragment.  The read-only problem on the boot.ini file has now been fixed.  All this is done as far as the UI is concerned, and quite a lot of the NSIS scripting complete, but most of the bits that will need to go into the preseed.cfg file aren't done yet, as some parts I'm not sure how to do in the preseed file (eg. NTFS shrink), and other bits I either havn't got round to or didn't have any easy way to test.  Obviously, all of the above isn't ready for testing until the entire system is done - so no PM's asking for an early prieview version please.

One other thing, if we're wanting to get the installer integrated into the official ubuntu cd's, on the autorn as suggested above, we'll need to keep the file size down, in particular the initrd as that seems to be getting quite big now. (the rest of the installer is about 700K, grub is 700K, and the kernel is about 1MB)

---

Well it was my fault that windows would not boot. I used the automatic install that does the downloading but after I rebooted there was no option to select an OS. I figured that Grub wasn't set up right so I added a Windows entry to the menu.lst file. But when I rebooted I was stuck. Turns out I needed to do two things. First change the entries in Grub to hda(0,1) instead of hda(0,0) But as I found out that doesn't work... hopefully it can be fixed. :(  My second problem was that when I installed automatically the boot.ini file wasn't changed so I had edit that too.  After I made those changes I was able to make it to the Ubuntu boot screen but it was frozen. I tried the recovery mode and it returned an error I can't remember. So in the end I removed the program :(  Just have to wait for a new release.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-22

> From my point of view thats actually quite easy to do, but it all depends on how long you think it'll take to get d-i working, because as soon as d-i works it saves all the hassle of trying to move large disk images around.
I did not have time to code this w/e but I am trying to catch up tonight. Hopefully I should make a better version of my script by modyfying iso-scan.postinst. That should be able to correctly pass the iso file to the rest of d-i (probably ready by tonight).

If that works, then then I need to make sure that partman can work off /dev/loop7 (where I mount root.img), which I doubt will happen very quickly (since I think that the target must be in the list of partman's known devices, which does not include /dev/loop#) so I might have to hack partman as well.

After that the installer part should be ready and the only task left should be to create a normal-boot initrd or better, to have a switch so that the same initrd can be used for setup purposes and booting purposes.

That said, the initial autodetiction script should work as it is also with the current initrd. So it can be used right now to avoid having people asking support for /dev/hdz8... Also the menu.lst will not change much once we move to d-i, so I see no point in waiting for d-i in order to implement disk autodetection.

> My current testing version of the installer allows for installation to a loopback partition on any drive, as well as allowing the use of a local iso image, or any other data source.  It allows extra customisation to the boot.ini (default boot, timeout etc.), and has advanced options to fix the boot.ini if another application/windows reinstall removes ubuntu's entry.  It also allows moving of the grldr, kernel, initrd, and a few other files in case they are fragmented, or otherwise running windows defragment.  The read-only problem on the boot.ini file has now been fixed.
Perfect!

> but most of the bits that will need to go into the preseed.cfg file aren't done yet
locale and keyboard in the new version might need to be passed to menu.lst as well but I do not think that is going to be a problem for you.

> as some parts I'm not sure how to do in the preseed file (eg. NTFS shrink)
I strongly doubt we will be able to do NTFS shrink in time for feisty. I would live it for feisty+1. All the partitioning for now should be without parameters to avoid problems. The issue is to make partman digest loop devices.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-22
> **Hello1024 said:**
> One other thing, I think in the end it would be better to have a fully unattended installation from the moment the user clicks the install button in windows until the desktop in ubuntu, so it would be best to avoid asking all but the most crital questions during the d-i install. 
That's the idea, I still set priority=critical in case the user somehow enters into manual mode or if we skip some of the required parameters. At the moment you are asked questions because I cannot complete the d-i process automatically (yet).

> it might be best to hash them with MD5 in windows, with the required salt, and then save those hashes into the d-i preseed file (or another config file), as hashed with a salt, they're not too much of a security risk.
Sure, security wise I still prefer to ask for the password at first boot but that can be changed later on. 

> One other thing, I presume it be supported to have two ubuntu loopback installations on the same windows partition
Yes there are several ways to do this, the easiest one is to add an optional kernel_img argument to menu.lst, specifying the name of the root.img (I will take care of it from there). If it is empty I will default to 'root.img'. Also, since the folder is identified by a key file, you can have several folders on different partitions and/or more folders on the same partition and/or more root images within the same folder. Lots of flexibility. The only restrictions are 1) only alphanumeric chars + '-_' are allowed in the path (or it is not possible to pass the argument as a kernel parameter, no whitespaces), 2)  to put all the required files under the same folder. 

> but I'm guessing you've used /ubuntu/ somewhere in the initrd for autodetection of the location, or are you using the key= parameter for the path as well?
The folder is the dirname of the keyfile. Therefore if the keyfile is '/hello/ago/1234.key' then the folder is going to be '/hello/ago'. The keyfile must be passed in 2 places within menu.lst, as a kernel parameter and within "find". Also note that different versions of grub may want --set-root before or after the filename, try them both.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-22
> Sure, security wise I still prefer to ask for the password at first boot but that can be changed later on. 

Why not just have the installer delete the file containing the md5 encrypted password immediately after it reads it? There would be no security risk like that, and it would be able to  boot without asking annoying questions in an ugly interface; 1st impression counts

I've noticed that your version mounts and scans all of the drives to find the iso image. Why not just have it scan the NTFS drives, since that'll be where it's found?

```
sudo fdisk -l | grep NTFS
```

that'll determine what device it should mount, then just pass the parameter to mount

I've also noticed that your version uses an empty premade disk image file (and also, they're not formatted as loopmounted filesytems ; you should fix that with the commands below). Why not just have it create it on first boot of the d-i installer initrd, using the filesizes specified in the file created by the windows installer? This creates a 5 GB ext3 disk image file in around 10 seconds, so it'll reduce the installation time compared to extracting an empty disk image in windows, and allow for flexible disk image sizes:

```
dd if=/dev/zero of=ubuntu.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[5000000]
mke2fs -j ubuntu.img
```

for a 1 GB swapfile to be created instantly:

```
dd if=/dev/zero of=swap.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[1000000]
mkswap swap.img
```

By the way, your installer is unable to find my setup.iso, even though I've placed it in C:\ubuntu; I've tried with both the feisty herd 2 and edgy alternate install iso, and it gives the same error. Is that supposed to happen?

### Post by ago on 2007-01-23
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Why not just have the installer delete the file containing the md5 encrypted password immediately after it reads it?
One of the problems is that the user might not necessarily want to reboot immediately and until he does so, his password is exposed. That is an issue since people tend to reuse the same password. Obviously an encrypted password is not as useful as a plain one, particularly if it is only available for a short time, therefore the procedure is probably more than good enough to begin with, and windows user will likely have far worse issues. But it will raise concerns, particularly if this is to become an official installation method. 

> There would be no security risk like that, and it would be able to  boot without asking annoying questions in an ugly interface; 1st impression counts
The interface can use gtk (there is a framebuffer-gtk udeb somewhere), the initrd will be larger and it will require more work, but it can be done. In the meantime we can use the approach above. We can also play with password expiration, but I am not sure how different login managers handle that.

> I've noticed that your version mounts and scans all of the drives to find the iso image. Why not just have it scan the NTFS drives, since that'll be where it's found?
We can certainly limit the search using fdisk as you suggested, but it is good practice to also have generic capabilities. What if the files are under fat/fat32 or even ext2 (via extfs)? Or on a USB device which shows up with a bit of delay? The new version can cope with such situations, but obviously it is slightly slower. A good approach would be to try the ntfs drivers first as your suggested and fallback to the more exhaustive search if that fails. I will work on that. By the way, fdisk is not in the standard initrd and must be added, do you know any other way to genarating a list of device|filesystem? Otherwise I will simply add the package.

> Why not just have it create it on first boot of the d-i installer initrd, using the filesizes specified in the file created by the windows installer?
Main reason was that I can use the same script for setup and normal boot, so that in both cases you use pre-made images. Since the initial images do not need have to have a FS, you can also create them from windows using any custom size (hopefully avoiding fragmentation). The makefs operation should take the same time, both when I generate the images with dd or when they come from windows. Also, writing within a file without changing its size is a safer FS operation. That said it is very much possible to change the script so that the images are generated on the fly. 

> By the way, your installer is unable to find my setup.iso, even though I've placed it in C:\ubuntu; I've tried with both the feisty herd 2 and edgy alternate install iso, and it gives the same error. Is that supposed to happen?
That's because your version of my script stops once the iso is mounted (you will find it under /iso_folder and you will find the windows device under /host_device, the images are accessible in /dev/loop7-5 using losetup /dev/loop7), but it does not register the iso with d-i. Therefore the standard d-i iso-scanner is started, but that cannot mount /host_device since it is already mounted and fails. The standard iso-scanner cannot be used as it is anyway, since it just looks for the first iso image it can find, which might not be what the user selected. Using a named iso file as kernel parameter within a specified folder (identified by a unique key), is safer and gives more flexibility. In the new version I am writing, I modify the d-i iso-scanner directly and the script is started later on in the boot process (thus using the same autodetection of the d-i scanner), therefore the iso image will be properly registered, disks are scanned only once and more devices are supported.

Done, I have uploaded the new files here: [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/)

Now d-i should go through until partman (where it drops into manual mode). Next stage is to either skip partman (but possibly run partman-target) or use partman-crypt which should be able to support loop devices. The first approach is safer and preferrable.

Ideally there should be a patch upstream so that if /target is already mounted, the partitioning bit is skept and we move to partman-target directly.

NOTE: The above files are for developers, they contain  an automatic (installer +) partitioning + experimental code. Those, as you can guess, do not mix well together, therefore handle with care. ### Post by ago on 2007-01-24
> **Hello1024 said:**
> One quick question - does it matter the contents of the .img files in your current version?  (ie. should I make the installer extract your current disk images, or create it's own?)

The way it will work is this:

1) I look for a folder with a key-file specified as kernel parameter
2) I mount (losetup to be precise) the images I can find in there

3A) IF you also specified the name of an ISO as a boot parameter and IF the ISO exists under the folder and IF it is the correct ISO and IF preseed.cfg exists and IF I cannot mount root.img (which means it has no FS), THEN it means we are in Setup-Mode, therefore I run mkfs on root.img and mount it under /target (and if I can find a good way to skip partman, we move from there).

3B) IF we are not in Setup-Mode it means we are in normal mode, therefore I simply mount the root.img (by the way you can specify its name within menu.lst) and chroot into it. If I cannot mount it, then the process fails. 

In short it means that you can put anything you want into root.img except a mountable FS, or I will refuse to overwrite it (this is to avoid having people accidentally overwriting existing images). Same thing for home.img.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-24
I was evaluating a different mechanism for finding the files. At the moment you specify a hots-key including full path, which identifies a folder, then the script works within the same folder to find the other files.

host_key=/ubuntu/1234.key
setup_iso=setup.iso
root_img=root1.img

A more flexible way is to use the following syntax

root_img=1234.key/root2.img
setup_iso=2521.key/feisty-i386.iso
home_img=7490.key/home.img
swap_img=1234.key/swap.img

The above is not a path but a combination of key and filename, the path is not provided but it is discovered using the key.  The advantages are: 

A) There is no restriction on the path name (but if it is deep it will take some more time to find it), in the current approach whitespaces aren't allowed for instance 
B) You can use image files stored in different places 
C) You can move/rename the folders without having to change menu.lst.

The disadvantages are:

1) It is slower, since I have to look for the key under each folder for each partition (breadth first search) up to n levels.
2) If the user is going to copy the folder with the key, he may boot from either of the 2 folders, this is also a possibility in the first case, but only if the user copies the folder to a different partition using the same path name (c:\ubuntu and e:\ubuntu)
3) You still need to have a fixed path for initrd and kernel, since the grub heuristic will not be able to find them otherwise 
4) If things can be moved around, writing the uninstaller is going to be fun

3 and 4 are show-stoppers, therefore I will stick to the current approach. Moreover B is probably a feature that only more advanced users may be interested in. As for C, it is not a big deal: there is no requirement that an application should work when you shuffle folders around, in fact almost all software out there will break under such circumstances. As for A we can simply check that the folder name is appropriate from NSIS. In short, no change.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-24
> **Hello1024 said:**
> When I get to the installer main menu, it won't let me execute a shell so I can't easily investigate and I haven't looked deeper into the cause.
Press alt+F2 and you should get a shell. Check that /cdrom and /hd-media are mounted (it will be unmounted if it fails to use an ISO) and if preseed.cfg is in /hdmedia. Check the parameters with cat /proc/cmdline and have a look at the log in /var/log/syslog (it is in verbose mode now).

> Also, on vmware, every time the installer redraws anything on the screen, it takes about 30 secs to redraw the entire screen, which makes everything very slow.
I am not sure about that, works fine here with kqemu and virtualbox, it may be due to some framebuffer issues (you can disable it with debian-installer/framebuffer=false)

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-24
> Hey im New to both Ubuntu and Linux in general. I am using Windows Vista RTM and a XP dual boot, Is there any Vista support? Or something I can do to try this new installer?

Yes, this WILL work on Vista, but only if you're dual-booting with XP; if you only have Vista, it won't boot (for now, at least, until GRLDR gets Vista support). Boot into XP, install in XP, and when you want to boot ubuntu, select Windows XP in the Vista bootloader, then select ubuntu; this way, it chainloads Windows XP's NTLDR and allows GRLDR to work.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-25
You have to edit c:\menu.lst and change the (hd0,0) and hda1 parameters to other values. 

first 0 in (hd0,0) is the HD number (starting at 0)
second 0 is the partition number (starting at 0)
the h in hda1 is the disk type can be s or h
the a in hda1 is the disk number (starting at a)
the 1 in hda1 is the partition number (starting at 1)

The two have to match therefore (hd0,0)=hda1 and must both be pointing to the window partition that contains the ubuntu folder.

The new version will spare you the trouble and it is the one we are working on, but it does not work yet.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-25
Remember linux based OS's don't use drive letters as such, but instead mount points, which is more intuitive.

We probably will provide options to make migration easier, such as for example providing an option to copy documnts across (or show the users windows "My Documents" folder on the desktop), and maybe include copying of the internet explorer/firefox bookmarks.  We don't want to transfer too much stuff, because most windows machines have a lot of "junk" on them, and simply copying everything would cause all kinds of incompatibilities.  As far as detecting and installing certain applications, I think it might be a good idea to provide an option to do that, but lots of people have programs installed that they have only used once.

All of the above are really extension projects, as every part will have to be coded seperately, and the main focus right now is getting it to work, and work really reliably and with as near as possible no-risk.

As far as Vista is concerned, if it's a dual boot system, you can install as long as you do the installation from within XP.  If you've only got Vista then at the moment it's not possible, but I think with a bit of work we should be able to get it working, because the vista bootloader has hundreds of options, and at least one of them must do the trick!  If anyone can get the vista bootloader to chainload grub in any way, please tell me how you did it and I can script it into the installer, because thats the only reason Vista isn't supported right now.

I'm geting the alternate feisty cd as we speak to test the current version....

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-25
Thats a bit of a disadvantage, as it takes away many of the reasons for going with a d-i based installer.  I still think it's worth trying to get d-i working, because when it works we should end up with a much more reliable installation than using directly downloaded disk images, although the extra installation time is a bit of a problem to offset that.

It might also be worth taking a look at how ubiquity works though, because it seems to basicly be copying files from the squashfs media to the hard disk, and then patching things to make it work as a hd install rather than a livecd.

I did manage to get the installer working up to the partitioning with the feisty-alternative cd iso, from a download using the windows installer (it's all working automated now so I needent tweak any files before rebooting).  Also, I found the API's for fast allocation, and under windows XP it can allocate a 5GB file in under a second, and since it allocates it all in one go, it will be in as few fragments as is possible without moving other files.  Under windows 2000, it's not possible to use those API's, and the file will have to be allocated the "old" way, which takes a few minutes and doesn't guarantee the fewest fragments possible, but fragmentation will still be better than extracting the file with a utility like bzip.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-26
Hi,

Had a look at q-emu img, but due to some horid complications with ntfs, if you allocate a file but don't fill it, the file is only partially allocated on the disk (file valid data size is not set to the same as the file size).  The actual allocation occurs when the file is READ (so by reading a file you cause a write operation).  My worry is that if q-emu allocates an empty file (with no contents), then ubuntu installs into that file (without marking the file as fully allocated or changing the file metadata at all, because it's not expanding the file), then when windows starts, everything will be fine until a few weeks later the user runs a system backup utility or similar, which READS the file, and causes windows to complete the allocation of the file, and while doing so overwrite all the Ubuntu data with nulls.  I thought it was safer to use the windows API directly and create the file and then mark it as fully allocated, and that way there's no risk of windows attempting to complete the zero-filling later.  I havn't tested out this theory with the linux ntfs-3g drivers, but I suspect there are no allocated but not zero-filled files on an average PC, so I doubt the feature has been fully implemented. (tried a google search, but it didn't turn up any info)

My opinion is that it would probably be best to carry on with d-i, because we seem to have made good progress there, and going to ubiquity would be a bit like starting again, although I'm probably not the person to ask since I don't have much experience with the workings of linux based OS's.


Another thing we could look at is (although simplified), if the ubiquity installer copies the files across, and then patches files, and then does hardware detection, if we could split the hardware detection part off from the rest, we could go back to tuxcantfly's original disk image, and just run the hardware detection on that.  My concern is that there seem to be a lot of bugs with his current disk image, but IF most of them could be fixed with good hardware detection, that'd be great.  Obviously, we'd have that old problem of then having large files to distribute, and not using an official release, but if we could make a script which could effectively install ubuntu into a VM, patch some files, and convert it to a disk image, that would mean for every new release we just run the script again.

EDIT:  Replied to SgtPepperKSU's information in it's thread

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-26
> we could go back to tuxcantfly's original disk image, and just run the hardware detection on that

The current state of hardware detection on my disk image is rather simple:

```
dpkg-reconfigure -fnoninteractive xserver-xorg
dpkg-reconfigure dhcp3-client
```

first one's for Xorg, 2nd one's for networking. Put these commands into a script, place the script into /etc/rcS.d/, have it remove itself after running the first time (rm /etc/rcS.d/S99filename), and you should have hardware detection working on the new version.

By the way, why do you want to use qemu-img to create the disk images? They format them in a different way from a standard loopmounted disk images, and take longer to generate than a simple command on first ubuntu bootup, which we can build into the installer initrd:

```
dd if=/dev/zero of=ubuntu.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[5000000]
```

which will instantly create a 5 GB file, which you can then format using the filesystem of choice with d-i. We can also adjust the filesize by making this a script which reads a value created by the windows installer for the desired size of the disk image file.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-26
> home.img is locked at 50 MB

how do i expand it without losing its content?

```
sudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/media/windows/ubuntu/newhome.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[5000000]
sudo mkreiserfs -f /media/windows/ubuntu/newhome.img
sudo mkdir /media/ext3
sudo mount /media/windows/ubuntu/newhome.img -o loop -t reiserfs /media/ext3
sudo cp -r /home/* /media/ext3
sudo umount /media/ext3
sudo rm -r /media/ext3
sudo mv /media/windows/ubuntu/home.img /media/windows/ubuntu/oldhome.img
sudo mv /media/windows/ubuntu/newhome.img /media/windows/ubuntu/home.img
```

then reboot, and it should give you a 5 GB /home; adjust the first line to change this size

if anything goes wrong, just boot windows, and rename C:\ubuntu\oldhome.img to C:\ubuntu\home.img

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-27
> **Hello1024 said:**
> 
My opinion is that it would probably be best to carry on with d-i, because we seem to have made good progress there, and going to ubiquity would be a bit like starting again
I agree

> Another thing we could look at is (although simplified), if the ubiquity installer copies the files across, and then patches files, and then does hardware detection, if we could split the hardware detection part off from the rest, we could go back to tuxcantfly's original disk image, and just run the hardware detection on that. 
I was thinking the same thing, I think d-i is better if we use standard images, with premade images we can literally split ubiquity in half and execute everything ubiquity does after having copied the files. I can help with that too.

> Obviously, we'd have that old problem of then having large files to distribute, and not using an official release
As mentioned, to go with official ISOs, we'd probably have to go with d-i, therefore the trade-off is: 

+officcial ISOs 
+ all ubuntu flavors are supported
+official installation method
+identical installation to a standard setup
+less bandwidth 
+less maintenance (only have to worry about 1 initrd) 
+custom sized images
+autodetection of windows device (no users messing with menu.lst)
+d-i is very modular it provides clean hooks and it is very flexible 
-d-i is very complex (it is a self-contained mini distribution, and most tasks are determined, loaded and executed at runtime)
-longer-installation-time 
-uglier-interface (can be improved using usplash as d-i interface)

Therefore I still think that d-i + official (alternate) ISOs is a better approach.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-27
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> The current state of hardware detection on my disk image is rather simple:
Tux, as mentioned above it should well be possible to use Ubiquity (second part). Add it to your image, and hack the main routine to skip partitioning and copying. Then run what is left on first boot (just set a flag somewhere so that if the flag is not there when you boot, ubiquity is run).

```
By the way, why do you want to use qemu-img to create the disk images?
```
You can create images from windows pretty quickly as well, and that is a nice approach for 3 reasons:

1) You do not have to worry about disk space drying out after the wizard is run and before the next reboot
2) You can use the same script for setup-mode and normal-mode boot
3) Writing a file without changing its size (i.e. creating the file in windows and modifying it in Linux without changing its size), is a safe FS operation, therefore even if there are unknown bugs in ntfs-3g that might compromise the file system, their impact would be minimized if not eliminated.

A simple question, is there any advantage in using reiserfs as opposed to ext3 in the images?

PS answering to a previous questions of yours, the proper way to generate a d-i based initrd is to use install the d-i package ( [https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/debian-installer](https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/debian-installer) ), modifying it (possibly packing our scripts within a udeb), creating a new target if necessary, and then building the target with `make targetname`. My current procedure, unpacks an existing initrd, modifies it, and packs it back. It is quicker, but not the right way of doing things. I will move soon to the procedure above.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-27
genXhippie:  This is due to the fact that your computer uses the old FAT file system, which has some quite serious limitations, and therefore this installer doesn't work with it (I will add a check so that it notifies the user of that before installation if thats the case).

Included with windows is a tool to convert an old FAT format disk to a new NTFS disk, which is what microsoft reccomends to everyone now.  There is a small risk in the conversion, but overall the performance, and especially the reliability of your computer should be much better after conversion - it's up to you if you want to convert though.  Here's a microsoft page about how to do it:  [http://www.microsoft.com/technet/prodtechnol/winxppro/maintain/convertfat.mspx](http://www.microsoft.com/technet/prodtechnol/winxppro/maintain/convertfat.mspx)
Ignore the "during setup" section, because thats referring to during windows setup, which is already done.

EDIT:  I've thought of a more likely cause - Your computer has a "recovery Partition" or similar, which is the first partition on your computer.  That means windows is the seccond partition.  At the moment, it doesn't support installation to anything other than the first partition, so it won't work - sorry :(.  The next version should support your setup though.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-01-27
> A simple question, is there any advantage in using reiserfs as opposed to ext3 in the images?

mainly, it's online resizing; if the user needs more space, with reiserfs, all it takes it:

sudo mount -o remount,resize=10000000 /

that'll expand your / to 10 GB, just a simple command, no reboot or unmounting needed; we might even write a daemon that can do it automatically when the user runs out of space

with ext3, though, you'd have to do all sorts of complex resizing, defragmenting, and filesystem stuff, heavy chance of data loss, lots of reboots and tinkering needed

### Post by ago on 2007-01-28
> **Hello1024 said:**
> Yes my installation failed with lilo installation (said it couldn't find a suitable disk).  I'll be on the lookout for some preseed settings to disable it.

If you specify "grub-installer/skip" then Lilo is used, but unfortunately it does not seem that there is a "lilo-installer/skip" preseed option (but try anyway). I can see there is a nobootloader package which is executed after lilo. I cannot find any info on a preseed parameter that can allow to run nobootloader directly. In the meantime I am hacking it so that nobootloader is executed before Lilo installer, if that does not work I will make it skip Lilo .

EDIT: I have fixed it by moving the priority of nobootloader (but not with preseed). Now the installation completes. I have also cleaned the code a bit and made it possible to specify the image filenames as boot parameters. Now what is left to do is to change the initrd so that it can also be used for normal boot mode and we should have a useful initrd. I will upload the files once that is done in the coming days.

### Post by genXhippie on 2007-01-29
> **Hello1024 said:**
> genXhippie:  This is due to the fact that your computer uses the old FAT file system, which has some quite serious limitations, and therefore this installer doesn't work with it (I will add a check so that it notifies the user of that before installation if thats the case).

Included with windows is a tool to convert an old FAT format disk to a new NTFS disk, which is what microsoft reccomends to everyone now.  There is a small risk in the conversion, but overall the performance, and especially the reliability of your computer should be much better after conversion - it's up to you if you want to convert though.  Here's a microsoft page about how to do it:  [http://www.microsoft.com/technet/prodtechnol/winxppro/maintain/convertfat.mspx](http://www.microsoft.com/technet/prodtechnol/winxppro/maintain/convertfat.mspx)
Ignore the "during setup" section, because thats referring to during windows setup, which is already done.

EDIT:  I've thought of a more likely cause - Your computer has a "recovery Partition" or similar, which is the first partition on your computer.  That means windows is the seccond partition.  At the moment, it doesn't support installation to anything other than the first partition, so it won't work - sorry :(.  The next version should support your setup though.

Well, that's too bad.  I was eager to give this thing(Linux) a shot.  I guess my jalopy of a pc & I will have to wait then until the next version. Good luck ppl.  Btw, thanks Hello1024 for responding and not leaving me in the dark, I appreciate it

---

### Post by ago on 2007-01-29
> **cseg said:**
> But my first thought was: could this be done with coLinux, which runs linux (including Ubuntu -- see here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=81444](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=81444)) concurrently with Windows (not a dual boot).

As discussed above, the aim is to create a full dual-boot system to be run exactly as a standard Ubuntu installation. There are several reasons for that, among others, colinux does have some limitations and people may blame Ubuntu for features (or lack thereof) that are due to the use of colinux. The procedure is not 100% safe as you can read in the thread you linked, where the guy ended up with a corrupted file system. Moreover the idea is to try to make people use, understand and appreciate a different OS, you do not want people to switch to windows as soon as they have to send an email. Last but not least, allowing for more than one way to boot into the new OS may be cool and even useful for a few people, but it will confuse quite a lot of others. 

That said, it is technically possible to use coLinux with the images created by the installer, but it sounds more like a separate project to me.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-30
> **Skizem said:**
> I select Ubuntu and then get a black screen with a white flashing underscore, the screen sits like this for about 3 seconds, then returns the fd0 error.

You can only press "C" if you get to the grub menu (it looks +/- like the windows boot menu). If you do not see that it is either because the menu is hidden (press esc and/or make sure there is no hiddenmenu option in menu.lst) or because grub cannot load at all. In order for grub to load, grldr and menu.lst should be on the root of the "first" _ntfs_ partition, a brute force approach is to copy them on the root of each of your partitions (C:\, D:\, E:\...) and check that ntfs is the file system used.

If grub fails and you have a home network and another PC already running Ubuntu, and you can connect the target machine via ethernet to your network, an alternative way to install is to use netboot. See [http://wiki.koeln.ccc.de/index.php/Ubuntu_PXE_Install](http://wiki.koeln.ccc.de/index.php/Ubuntu_PXE_Install) 

Don't be scared, it is only a bit of cut and paste. Once you set-up the existing Ubuntu machine (skip the mirror section), you have to disable dhcp from the router (since the ubuntu machine will act as a dhcp server now), then you boot the target machine making it boot from network (you might have to change the boot order in the bios). If everything works, you should see the Ubuntu installation menu. After the installation is completed, you can reactivate the dhcp server of the router and turn off the tftpd and dhcp server of the Ubuntu machine.

It is a bit more involved but having a network that installs Ubuntu to the machines that connect to it, is quite cool. This is what I use at home. :D

---

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-01-30
Hi,

I don't think the error is in NTLDR, because it doesn't use that naming convention for floppy disk drives.

You could isolate the problem to either grldr or grub by renaming/moving c:\grub.exe and then rebooting.  Select the ubuntu menu, and see if the problem still occurs or if you get another error.  If you get another error, the problem is most likely grub, otherwise the problem is definately grldr.  grldr is part of the wingrub project, so you might want to ask them if they've ever seen the issue.

If you couldn't get ubuntu to install by other methods either, was that the error you got every time with other installation methods as well?  If it was, it points the finger at a problem with grub.  Since the error mentions fd0, I suspect it's a floppy drive issue.  If you have an external drive, I might try unplugging it.  If you don't have a floppy drive at all, it's a possibility the computers BIOS is set up to believe the computer has a floppy disk drive, and therefore when grub tries to access it fails.  If thats the case, you should go into BIOS setup (by turning on the computer while tappoing Delete, Escape, F10, F2, or Ctrl - you can tell which key by looking for "Press X to enter setup").  Then look through the menus until you find floppy disk settings, and change both floppy drives (A: and B:)to "none".  Write down anything that you change so you can change it back in case you need to set it back.

As ago said, it would be helpfull to know the make/model of your computer and/or how many of what sort of drives and partitions you have.

### Post by ago on 2007-01-30
> **E-Jey said:**
> I didn't read the whole threat, but what is the status about the ubuntu support for this installer? I hope this will be an official way to install ubuntu! Off course their should be some user education about the fact that it's better to install the iso image.

The new version should create an installation which is (almost) identical to an official one, it will use the same installer (d-i) AND the same ISO. Moreover I have tried to minimize and encapsulate the changes to d-i (which will hopefully be merged upstream in the future). Therefore I do not see why this method should not become an official one. It follows that the only advantages from installing via CD is that you get a dedicated partition as opposed to a dedicated file and that you control the main bootloader. But those 2 points are also a reason of concern for many inexperienced users and to be quite frank, if you can boot ok and if the fs performance is acceptable, the average user could not care less about what bootloader is used and where the file reside.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-01
I have uploaded the latest initrd, this should hopefully finish the installation and be able to boot the created image in normal mode. There is no need to have 2 entries in menu.lst for setup and normal boot, and no need to specify the device, the initrd should be able to autodetect where the files are and take the appropriate action.

[http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-0.0.0.4.tgz](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-0.0.0.4.tgz)

There are a lot of changes since last time, and I did not have time to run all the tests. As usual, I did not try it on ntfs and I expect the image to be buggy. Verbose mode is on. But the main features should be there, now it is a matter of cleaning the code, squishing the bugs and deuglify the newt interface.

WARNING: for developers only. The file does NOT contain any wizard, it is only the backend.

NOTE: The boot paramater for preseed now must be `file=/preseed.cfg` (see attached menu.lst)

---

### Post by e_____e on 2007-02-01
So, uh, I'm really new to Ubuntu and in general all things Linux-related.. I have a few questions I'd like answered, please.

1. After I install Ubuntu, how do I get it to start?
2. After I install Ubuntu, how do I go back to using Windows? (Not that I'd prefer Windows over it, just for informational purposes ;))
3. I assume this can't harm my existing files.. I mean, it seems really obvious that it wouldn't, but just to make sure there isn't a possible glitch or something..

---

### Post by natedawg on 2007-02-01
> **e_____e said:**
> So, uh, I'm really new to Ubuntu and in general all things Linux-related.. I have a few questions I'd like answered, please.

1. After I install Ubuntu, how do I get it to start?
2. After I install Ubuntu, how do I go back to using Windows? (Not that I'd prefer Windows over it, just for informational purposes ;))
3. I assume this can't harm my existing files.. I mean, it seems really obvious that it wouldn't, but just to make sure there isn't a possible glitch or something..

1. If all goes well... when you boot your computer you will be given the option to boot Ubuntu or windows. 
2. You can choose the boot windows when you boot your computer. You can also remove Ubuntu by using Add/Remove Programs in the control panel.
3. Installing the windows installer version of Ubuntu should not alter your file. Although the installer is still in development  so by using it at this time you are taking a risk.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-02
New version: [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-livecd-v1.zip](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-livecd-v1.zip)

this one is based off the live cd; you need an edgy (6.10) livecd ISO

download the zipfile at [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-livecd-v1.zip](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-livecd-v1.zip)

extract it

place grldr, grub, and menu.lst into C:\

place initrd.gz, vmlinuz, and ubuntu.img into C:\ubuntu

rename your edgy desktop iso to ubuntu.iso, and place it in C:\ubuntu

edit your boot.ini to include this at the last line:

```
C:\grldr="Ubuntu"
```

please make sure that your timeout value is set above 0

reboot, then select "Ubuntu"

Hello1024 should have the wizard ready soon

because this is based off the livecd, and is essentially a persistent install, so modifications to hardware configurations will be reset on reboot, but newly installed applications and new files will be saved.

an option is available for permanent installation upon booting, however, this installs to a physical disk, not a disk image, therefore, it will require partitioning (but it will install a standard ubuntu, with no hacks whatsoever)

download at [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-livecd-v1.zip](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-livecd-v1.zip)

---

### Post by natedawg on 2007-02-02
> **DrDooM_FR said:**
> ok ok, but if I install Ubuntu with traditional method, it works. I'm lost :(


Hope this helps...

This thread explains how to install Ubuntu to a windows partition. The benefit to this method is that the user doesn't have to partition the hard drive (a task many inexperienced users dread). Instead Ubuntu is installed on the Windows partition(ntfs) like a program. Doing this allows Ubuntu to easily access your Windows files and partition. It also allows Ubuntu to be easily removed by using the Add/Remove Programs section in the Windows control panel.


EDIT: This method is also good for people that have a few bad sectors on their hard drive and currently can't partition the drive with open source programs. (I have one computer with this problem)

---

Instead Ubuntu is installed on the Windows partition(ntfs) like a program. Doing this allows Ubuntu to easily access your Windows files and partition. It also allows Ubuntu to be easily removed by using the Add/Remove Programs section in the Windows control panel.


EDIT: This method is also good for people that have a few bad sectors on their hard drive and currently can't partition the drive with open source programs. (I have one computer with this problem)

### Post by ago on 2007-02-02
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> because this is based off the livecd, and is essentially a persistent install, modifications such as those to the Xorg configuration will be reset on reboot

an option is available for permanent installation upon booting, however, this installs to a physical disk, not a disk image, therefore, it will require partitioning (but it will install a standard ubuntu, with no hacks whatsoever)


I do not think we should walk the frugal-install route at all. The image I have uploaded can create a proper installation, not just a "read-only" one, and the real installation does not require repartitiong or change of bootloader. At the moment, my version of the installer is based on the AlternateCD (d-i), and the interface is ugly (the verbose mode does not help), but it is a proper installation, based on a proper installer, the look compartment is less important and can be fixed later. 

I think I know what you are aiming at, but I believe you should avoid the temptation of just copying the content of squashfs to a new img and apply hacked scripts to configure it. The proper way of doing thing is to go through d-i, whether directly or via ubiquity or via maybe another interface. It is more complex, but I do not think you can take any shortcuts here. 

By the next version we can decide whether to invest more time on the d-i interface (possibly creating a usplash front-end) or whether to try and use Ubiquity and an ISOs based on the LiveCD targeting an image file. There are pros and cons for both approaches, and we started the discussion with Hello1024 some time ago. Whatever we decide, diverging from d-i is dangerous, and I do not think that releasing an installer which is inconsistent with what was done before and what will be done in the future will help matters.

By the way, your use of unionfs gave me an idea that should allow us to use both types of ISOs using the same initrd... ...with d-i/ubiquity obviously... I still need to run a few tests but the idea looks promising. On my side I will try to converge, v3 of my initrd was based on a d-i initrd, v4 is an hybrid of the d-i initrd and the regular Ubuntu's initrd, v5 will be based only on Ubuntu's initrd (but the behaviour will be identical to v4 and tehrefore d-i will be used). In v6 we can try to make it work with ubiquity/LiveCD and merge your code with mine.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-02
> I do not think we should walk the frugal-install route at all. The image I have uploaded can create a proper installation, not just a "read-only" one

Try it... it saves changes; only stuff like Xorg configuration which are reconfigured upon reboot due to the livecd autoconfiguration are reset; everything else (new applications, data, files) are saved; this isn't just a "read-only" install

> The proper way of doing thing is to go through d-i, whether directly or via ubiquity or via maybe another interface

The aim is to do it through ubiquity; 1) it'll be like a livecd and installation, only there will be no cd needed for the livecd, and 2) no repartitioning needed for the installation. Part 1 is done, but part 2 isn't; we still need to get it to install to a disk image file

> whether to try and use Ubiquity and an ISOs based on the LiveCD targeting an image file

The ubiquity approach is better; after all, most users will be downloading the desktop iso; they shouldn't need to use a different one to install on windows. Also, with my new version, all we need is a modification to ubiquity that causes it to install to a disk image file instead of a real hard drive (losetup /ntfs/ubuntu/ubuntu.img; then install to /dev/loop0); we can just put that into the persistent disk image (ubuntu.img) and it will show up on the user's desktop (like 2 options; install to hard drive, install to hard drive disk image), then he can select either and run the ubiquity installer, and it will install to either a dedicated partition or to the disk image file.

> Whatever we decide, diverging from d-i is dangerous

According to statistics, most users are installing with ubiquity, not d-i (more downloads for desktop isos, not alternate) so I'd say ubiquity is quite safe; just a small modification or parameter will be needed to get ubiquity to target a disk image file, instead of a hard drive. As for the rest of the work, it's done; we already have an initrd that can boot off the disk image file, and ubiquity will simply be installing to that disk image file

### Post by ago on 2007-02-02
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Try it... it saves changes;
I have seen your code. It is basically a loopmounted ISO which is made rw via unionfs. This is why I used the quotes, it is half way between rw and ro. Using unionfs as root fs is dangerous at best. There are other good ways of taking advantage of unionfs, but certainly not using it as the base of an "installation". It is fair to say that your initrd, at the moment, is very little different from instlux, in the sense that its main role is to spare users the trouble of burning an ISO. On top of it, users will be quite confused, since now they will see not 1 but 2 "installers", and if they want the real thing they have to repartition and change the bootloader, which goes against the objectives of this project. So I think your current image should be dropped for the time being.

> The ubiquity approach is better; after all, most users will be downloading the desktop iso;
Not quite. The NSIS wizard is in charge of downloading the ISO, the user could not care less whether the wizard fetches the Alternate ISO or a Live one. The only disadavantage of the Alternate ISO is that it would not be as interesting if burnt, but burning a CD has never been an objective of this project...

> Also, with my new version, all we need is a modification to ubiquity that causes it to install to a disk image file instead of a real hard drive
Not quite. You need to pass all the parameters collected by NSIS, and hack away the questions. Ubiquity does not support preseed at the moment. D-I was designed from the ground up to be used in automated installations (which is what we neeed), but Ubiquity was designed from the ground up to be interactive. (Properly) transforming Ubiquity into an automated installer can be done, but it is not as simple as you make it, I have already looked at the code. 

> Then you generate a second image and when you boot you need to select dynamically which one to use.
Which is what my initrd does already... On top of having a more robust algorithm to fetch the right folder and the right ISO, with tests and error handlers... On top of creating a real installation, using a real installer. Today, not tomorrow. I am not saying it is bullet proof, but it is a good base on which to work. Therefore I suggest we move to my initrd as the main one used in the project. In the meantime I will work on merging my code with yours. The new version will be based on the standard Ubuntu initrd (like yours) as opposed to a d-i one (my original one), and I it will be made in such a way that it will be easy to move from AlternateISOs to LiveISOs once we are ready (or more likely support both). If you want to you can start working on the ubiquity code. I can join you once I finish the above mentioned initrd. 

> then he can select either and run the ubiquity installer, and it will install to either a dedicated partition or to the disk image file.
My initrd can support a real target as opposed to a loopmounted file. You only have to pass it as a boot parameter, the question can be asked by NSIS. 

> According to statistics, most users are installing with ubiquity, not d-i
Quite obviously so, since that is the default downolad, while the alternate ISO is for advanced use(r)s. That has absolutely nothing to do with our case, since we already have a user interface (NSIS) and since our code takes care of handling the rest, so whether the ISO used in the background is userfriendly or not is completely irrelevant. You should see the ISO only as a source of packages, for what matters, you could pre-load all the required packages directly from the repos as opposed to use an ISO. Its main role is to have all required "stuff" handy BEFORE running the real installer within Linux, so that the installation can complete even if the network does not work under Linux.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-02
> I have seen your code. It is basically a loopmounted ISO which is made rw via unionfs. This is why I used the quotes, it is half way between rw and ro. Using unionfs as root fs is dangerous at best. There are other good ways of taking advantage of unionfs, but certainly not using it as the base of an "installation"

Of course... the one I uploaded is NOT a full-fledged installer, but rather a cd-less live cd... for a full-fledged installer, the tweaked ubiquity that can install to a disk image will need to be finished... until then, we can use your version

> On top of it, users will be quite confused, since now they will see not 1 but 2 "installers", and if they want the real thing they have to repartition and change the bootloader, which goes against the objectives of this project. .

True... but that's only in its current state. With a few tweaks to ubiquity, we can have it install to a disk image.

> Not quite. You need to pass all the parameters collected by NSIS, and hack away the questions. Ubiquity does not support preseed at the moment. D-I was designed from the ground up to be used in automated installations (which is what we neeed), but Ubiquity was designed from the ground up to be interactive. (Properly) transforming Ubiquity into an automated installer can be done, but it is not as simple as you make it, I have already looked at the code.

The idea is that my version should NOT ask the questions in Windows, but in ubiquity, so no, it won't be automated, but will do the question-asking and configuring within ubiquity. After all, the main reason why we wanted the installer to ask the questions from within windows was to give users a better comfort level, as they'd be intimidated by the alternate iso installer user interface, but the ubiquity livecd installer is already very user-friendly, and looks and feels just like a windows installer, and shouldn't intimidate users.

> So I think your current image should be dropped for the time being

For now, it's meant to simply be a livecd, without the cd; it addresses the issue of the cd burning and BIOS tweaking; it doesn't currently address the repartitioning issue. After all, there are many people that are interested in making a standard installation without burning a cd or tweaking the BIOS. So, no, I don't believe it should be "dropped", but simply re-branded as a livecd without the cd for the time being, until I figure out how to get ubiquity installing to a disk image. My idea is that the next version (v4) use your installer (d-i and alternate iso), and after that (v5) should use my livecd with a modified ubiquity. The code from v4 will be able to be reused, as the hard part isn't getting the system itself installed into a disk image, but having it properly boot from it; that part can be reused from your version (v4) in my livecd/ubiquity based version (v5).

> If you want to you can start working on the ubiquity code

Exactly what I'll be doing

### Post by ago on 2007-02-02
Hello,

Maybe I should turn off some of the debugging code before you include it. You should try it first with debugging on, let me know if it works. Tux you should try it too. 

Tux,

Maybe I was not clear enough. I am merging my code with yours, there is no point in diverging, since I am converging. Your code gave me this idea: I can use a standard initrd (as opposed to a d-i one) and patch the the installer image on the fly using unionfs, then run-init into the installer. This has several advantages. But in short it means that the same standard initrd can be used with both Alternate ISOs and Ubiquity ISOs, you simply have to put the patches in one tgz. The same initrd will take care of other duties (fetching the folder, running preliminary checks, decide whether to install or boot, and a bunch of other stuff that is already in my code...). Not least, being a standard initrd I can use mkinitramfs and automate the building process.

Therefore you can keep working on Ubiquity and I work on the initrd, and once your installer works it will be trivial to add Live ISOs capabilities to the initrd. Once I have finished with that, I can help with Ubiquity (I am much more confident with python than with bash). At the moment though, I do not think that your image is a good candidate, for the reasons exposed above. 

As for asking questions from Ubuntu, I have mixed feelings about that. 

1) You still need a wizard  to download the files and selecting where to install/uninstall them, once you use that, it does not make much sense to me to ask half of the questions in the windows wizard, and half in Ubuntu (and with 2 different interfaces). I think that would be somewhat confusing for the users. 

2) Also they will be confused by the intermediate stage. I.E. they run the windows wizard, reboot and find themselves in a working system. Therefore they may think that the installation is over, when they have not even started. Working on a system booted as a LiveCD (with or without unionfs), thinking that it is a real installation, might lead to nasty surprises... 

3) The windows wizard can collect some useful information, since it can "observe" a working system. Therefore you still may want a mechanism to send and use preseed. There is a spec to import settings and files, but some such things are far easier from within windows.

4) You have to defer the generation of the target image files, but it would be better to generate them from windows, based on the principle that the guest FS/OS should not modify the host FS/OS.  

5) I do not see what is the downside of using d-i directly. Once the user runs the windows wizard, he reboots, sees a progressbar and than he is in a real Linux installation. It seems pretty neat to me. Maybe the scrollbar does not look that good now, but that can be fixed.

On the other hand there are quite obvious advantages, particularly that only minimal changes are required. 

That said I would wait to ask official devs at least until the code stabilizes and we follow proper procedures, which basically means: 

1) building process of the initrd has been migrated to the proper tools (mkinitramfs)
2) using proper hooks whenever possible as opposed to modify files directly, 
3) making sure that any required change is backward compatible, so that it can be merged upstream. 
4) setting up all usual tools (bug tracking, vcs, mailing list...)
5) regressions/testing suite
...

This will make it far easier for them to accept the new installation.

### Post by D McK on 2007-02-07
I had some earlier problems getting this working on a couple of my laptops, but now have it working. I have a Dell Latitude D610 and an old HP Pavilion ZE4115. For both laptops, all it took was to modify C:\menu.lst such that this was my first choice for booting:
title		Ubuntu, HDA2, kernel 2.6.17-10-generic
root		(hd0,1)
kernel		/ubuntu/kernel root=/dev/hda2 ro quiet splash
initrd		/ubuntu/initrd

note the changes from (hd0,0) to (hd0,1) and from /ubuntu/kernel root=/dev/hda1 ro quiet splash to /ubuntu/kernel root=/dev/hda2 ro quiet splash


I'm a happy camper now!
Thanks for this,
Doug

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-09
Aliarse, the installer is still alpha software, you cannot expect it to be smooth (see the title of this thread). As for the driver you probably have to edit manually the xorg config file:

sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

And change the video driver and/or the resolution settings. The old xorg.conf might have been backed up in the folder, you can try with that. For the next time, copy xorg.conf before you make any changes. In fact I usually recommend to backup all of /etc with the command: cp -a /etc /etc.backup

### Post by Ferk on 2007-02-09

 This way downloads could go faster, we would not need to seed 3 different files

### Post by ago on 2007-02-09
Ferk, yes that is a possibility. There are only a few considerations.

1) All the files need to be pre-downloaded, because you cannot assume that the internet connection is going to work, and you do not want to drop users in console mode to try and fix that. Thus you move complexity to the windows/nsis layer. Also you will have to use pre-compiled lists of dependencies. You may download them form a central server, but, again, you increase complexity on the windows side (and you need to have some script on the server side to keep such lists updated). 

2) We do not really seed different images with the new method. Since all the alternate ISOs are basically identical, therefore there is no inherent advantage in starting with a Server ISO as  opposed to an Alternate ISO from a technical standpoint. The only advantage for the end user, is if he want to install multiple flavors, but the best way to do that is to start with one distro and install the XYZ-desktop-packages from within that distro, this makes it possible to switch desktop environment at login and not at reboot. 

3) The new initrd I am writing is very flexible and can support such installation method. But I still think that d-i + usplash + alternate ISOs is the way to go. In fact even using a Server ISO all the first steps would be almost identical, and the last step if anything would be more difficult to implement in the Server ISO since you need to use two local repositories.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-10
[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=91455](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=91455)

controlla inoltre con df quale e' lo spazio disponibile. Se non hai spazio su /, crea una nuova device di 7GB

sudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/media/windows/ubuntu/usr.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=7000000
sudo mkreiserfs -f /media/windows/ubuntu/usr.img
sudo mkdir /usr.new
sudo mount -o loop /media/windows/ubuntu/usr.img /usr2
sudo cp -a /usr /usr.new
sudo mv /usr /usr.backup
sudo mv /usr.new /usr

Aggiungi una linea in /etc/fstab copiando la linea di /home, ma puntando a /usr 

/media/windows/ubuntu/usr.img    /usr    reiserfs  ....
 
Prova a fare il reboot e se tutto va bene puoi cancellare /usr.backup (sudo rm -rf /usr.backup).

### Post by Aliarse on 2007-02-10
Necesitas entrar tu la C: conducir y corregir el archivo de menu.lst (con la libreta) y hacer tu raíz los ajustes de la impulsión dura correctos en el fondo de menu.lst. El defecto para el mío era /dev/sda1 - si instalaste en la C: impulsión de tu disco de las ventanas, que tiene solamente una partición, necesitas corregir las opciones inferiores del menú para ser /dev/hda1, entonces excepto el archivo y el reboot y él deben patear para arriba. Apesadumbrado para el español horrible más probable, isnt del traductor del google probablemente muy bueno.


[quote=aliarse][SIZE="0"]You need to go into your the C: drive and edit the menu.lst file (with notepad) and make your root hard drive settings correct at the bottom of menu.lst. 

Default for mine was /dev/sda1 - If you installed on the C: drive of your windows disk, which only has one partition, you need to edit the bottom options of the menu to be /dev/hda1, then save the file and reboot and it should boot up. 

Sorry for the most likely horrible spanish, google translator probably isnt very good.[/SIZE][/quote]

### Post by ago on 2007-02-10
New version is here (back-end only) !

**[http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-0.0.0.5.tgz](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-0.0.0.5.tgz)**
(source files to build the images are here: [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-src-0.0.0.5.tgz](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-src-0.0.0.5.tgz) )

WARNING:

This is EXPERIMENTAL SOFTWARE and it comes with NO GUARANTEE whatsoever. It is intended for developers only. See license  (GPL2) for details.

CURRENT STATUS:

The d-i installer should go through and install the system onto an image file and then be able to boot from that image. The same initrd is used both for booting and for installation. The correct action is determined at runtime. This avoids having to edit menu.lst after installation and it is safer since it prevents overwriting existing partitions. The image was only tested under Linux, not under windows.

INSTRUCTIONS:

0) In order to extract the packages you can use [http://www.7-zip.org/](http://www.7-zip.org/)
1) Extract the contents of the archive into C:\ubuntu
2) Extract the contents of disks.tgz in C:\ubuntu
3) Put grld, grub.exe and menu.lst into C:\
4) Download Ubuntu Feisty HERD-3 ALTERNATE ISO and put it in C:\ubuntu. The link is: [http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/feisty/herd-3/feisty-alternate-i386.iso](http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/feisty/herd-3/feisty-alternate-i386.iso)
5) Add the line C:\grld="Ubuntu" to C:\boot.ini
6) Reboot and select "Ubuntu"

NOTES:

* This is only the 'back-end' and it is not yet bundled to the windows wizard
* Read the contens of menu.lst for advanced boot options
* You can use any root.img file you want, just create a file of the desired size and call it "root.img".
* You can place the \ubuntu folder in any partition you want, you can change the name too provided you only use alphanumeric chars and adjust menu.lst accordingly
* You can have multiple root images and select them at boot by using the root_img parameter in menu.lst.
* You can have multiple ISOs and select them at boot by using the setup_iso parameter in menu.lst.
* There is no need anymore to specify HD parameters in menu.lst, they are autodetected.
* You can use any herd3 alternate ISO.
* The locale/keyboard is set to UK (this is where I am). You can change it in preseed.cfg before installing, or after installing
* If the installer stops half-way for any reason, you must delete and extract root.img again from disks.tgz. If the file is not vergin the installer will refuse to proceed with the installation (for obvious security reasons).
* The screen may appear a bit strange at a certain stage of the installation, but then everything should resume back to normal

DEBUGGING:

If you run into trouble, and want to debug, follow these instructions

1) Delete root.img and extract a new one, the installer will refuse to install on a non-empty image
2) Reboot in debug mode.
3) In debug mode you will be dropped into a shell after the first stage of the installation process (if you reach that point). This is normal and it is done so that more experienced users can run some checks.
4) To resume from the shell and go on with the installation, press CTRL+D.
5) If you are in the second stage of the installer you can use ALT+F4 to see the logs. Or ALT+F2 to get a shell.
6) The log is in /var/log/syslog
7) You can use SHIFT+PGUP/PGDOWN to scroll the output

WHAT ISO TO USE:

This one: [http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/feisty/herd-3/feisty-alternate-i386.iso](http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/feisty/herd-3/feisty-alternate-i386.iso)

* You have to use an ISO built on the same kernel of the installer, at the moment this means 2.6.20-6-generic kernel, which is found in Herd3 ISOs
* You can install only with an Alternate ISO, you can also use a Live CD ISO and you can only boot it.
* You should be able to use the Alternate CD ISO of any Ubuntu flavor, provided it is based on the same kernel as this installer.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-11
The partition detection code from my version, which I placed in init:

```
NTFSPART=$(fdisk -l | grep NTFS | sed  -e 's/\s/\n/g' | grep dev)
FATPART=$(fdisk -l | grep FAT | sed  -e 's/\s/\n/g' | grep dev)
mkdir /media
mkdir /media/windows
modprobe fuse
ntfs-3g $NTFSPART /media/windows
if [ -e "/media/windows/ubuntu/ubuntu.iso" ]; then
mountstuff
else
mount $FATPART -t vfat /media/windows && mountstuff
fi
```

As for the function mountstuff, which I placed in scripts/functions:

```
mountstuff() {
mkdir /cdrom
modprobe loop
mount -o loop -t iso9660 /media/windows/ubuntu/ubuntu.iso /cdrom
mkdir /squashfs
mount -o loop -t squashfs /cdrom/casper/filesystem.squashfs /squashfs
mkdir /loopfs
mount -o loop -t ext2 /media/windows/ubuntu/ubuntu.img /loopfs
modprobe unionfs
mount -t unionfs -o dirs=/loopfs:/squashfs none /root
}
```

This should fix the partition detection issues, so that it mounts ntfs/fat partitions, instead of ext3 ones. The mountstuff function here assumes that we are using a livecd iso, but it can be extended so that it can detect whether or not an alternate install iso is present, and do the mounting accordingly.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-11
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Ago: slight error with your installer

it mounts my ubuntu ext3 partition under /media/host, instead of the ntfs partition. It looks like some error with the partition detection code. Why don't you use the ntfs/fat detection code from my livecd-based version?

Because the new version is not windows specific. It can work on a mac (provided it is compiled with the right kernel) or on Linux. In fact I do not even have windows (which also means that I did not test it on windows). It is not an error, it is intentional. 

> anyhow, I've slapped together a self-extractor, so that the download and installation of 7-zip is not necessary
Thanks,

> 
NTFSPART=$(fdisk -l | grep NTFS | sed  -e 's/\s/\n/g' | grep dev)
FATPART=$(fdisk -l | grep FAT | sed  -e 's/\s/\n/g' | grep dev)


This will find the first NTFS/FAT partition. It will fail if the ubuntu folder is not into the first partition or if the partition is not NTFS/FAT, (in fact I think it may fail anytime there are multiple NTFS partitions). My implementation was inspired by the d-i iso-scan one, it is faster than the d-i one, but less extensive. It first loads the most commonly needed modules, it runs udev commands to make sure that all block nodes are there, then it filters out all partitions without a filesystem, then, for each of the partitions, it looks for the "right" folder as specified in the boot parameters. Once it finds the folder, it looks for the "right" files and runs several checks: it checks that the ISO is actually an Ubuntu ISO, it detects whether it is a LiveISO or an AlternateISO, it checks the status of the image files (whether there is a file system on them) then it decides what to do. Some of the advantages are that it works whatever the FS, even if the Ubuntu folder is inside the 8th partition, and it can resolve multiple installations on the same partition or on different partitions. You will find the implementation under src/initramfs/scripts/extra-functions. The original  init file is untouched (and it should not be modified), the scripts are run via a hook in init-bottom.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-11
> **javore said:**
> 
As I said, I got into the same error: can't access tty; job control turned off
That is not the error, you always get that when you are dropped to a console and there is no tty, The error is in the previous lines and it is what caused you to be dropped to console in the first place, You can scroll with shift+pgup. 

> I will download the iso again just in case the file was corrupted (but Winrar didn't find any errors).
No need. If you redownloaded ubuntu-setup...tgz it's fine. I am more interested in the logs above. Particularly mount/ntfs errors. Next to the error message you suld see some return values with return > 0. If you run in debug mode (select that in the boot menu) you should see a more verbose output. 

Note that if you run in debug mode, you will always be dropped to console, even if everything is fine. In this case you need to press CTRL+D. 

Thanks

### Post by ago on 2007-02-11
Also note that the 2.6.20-6-generic kernel (the one used in Herd3 and the installer) does not go along too well with emulators. See [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux-source-2.6.20/+bug/84191](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux-source-2.6.20/+bug/84191) This means that it is not possible at the moment to try the installer in a VM (which makes the debugging process slower). As soon as the kernel is fixed I will upload a new version.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-11
> **Naralas said:**
> Impressed but confused.

How does one harmlessly install Ubuntu without a second partition?

It is installed within a file that sits in an host file system. So you can have an ntfs or an ext3 partition containg a large file that itself contains a reiserfs file system. The instructions are here [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-0.0.0.5.tgz](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-0.0.0.5.tgz) 

The above works also for Linux, and it is a good way to try Feisty if you have no spare partition. Just copy the files in /ubuntu and append the menu.lst items to /boot/grub/menu.lst.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-11
> I was playing with that 5 mins ago. But using native linux commands. Do you use 7zS.sfx + config file? If so what is the config file?

But if you REALLY want to use commands:

download this file, unzip it, place the 7z.sfx file in the directory with the files
cd to the directory with the files
install p7zip-full (from universe repos), then:
```

7z -sfx./7z.sfx a ubuntu.exe ubuntu
```

this will create a ubuntu.exe self-extractor file with the contents of the ubuntu directory
