### Post by ago on 2007-02-11
> **timmay said:**
> An interesting Idea but what's not simple about the current installation of Ubuntu?
1) You need to burn a CD from an ISO, and most people out there have no clue how to do it, and even IF they decide to do it, often they do it incorrectly
2) Sometimes you have to change the Bios settings 
3) Requires changing the partitions and bootloader

Therefore the current process is still perceived as complex and dangerous by average joe. The idea of the installer is to address all of the above, so that the user has to run a single executable and the installation is much closer to that of an ordinary application than to that of an OS.

> This does however mean you can browse the web while you wait. 
It's going to take 15 mins, no big deal if you cannot browse.

> The text install available on the Ubuntu alternative CD's is also not difficult but could do with some eye candy to keep some people happy.
Next version will probably use a usplash frontend. The backend may stay with d-i (alternateISO) or be moved to ubiquity (liveISO). The new initramfs supports both.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-11
if interested, here is the sfx and config file, the others can be obtained by extracting the archive:

```
7z e ubuntu-setup-di-0.0.0.5.exe
```

to change the behavior of the installer, just modify the setup.bat file within the archive

to create the installer, just add all the files into a 7z archive:

```
7z a archive.7z ./*
```

then do:

```
cat 7zS.sfx config.txt archive.7z > ubuntu-setup-di.exe
```

### Post by ago on 2007-02-12
Thx, I went to sleep yesterday. I hope though that having the self extracting files on top of the nsis builds does not create too much confusion. By the way tux, the new initrd, more than an installer is a way to patch ISOs on the fly and "chainload" into the modified initrds/squashfs. 

In order to build a customized initrd for, say, a Live CD, place the files into ./src/live-iso/overlay. Then run make && make install at the project root. You will find the new initrd inside ./dist. In a Live-Iso the overlays are applied to the squash fs, in an alternate-iso they are applied to its initrd. But all this is automatic, you only have to place the files in overlay and run make.

See ./src/alternate-iso/overlay for an example. src/initramfs/ is a bit more complicated but it uses a similar approach (man initramfs-tools). You will be booted into the modified initrd and will find the root.img file mapped on /dev/loop7. If you copy the script in ./src/alternate-iso/overlay/sbin/loop-install into ./src/live-iso/overlay/sbin/ and run it at the beginning of the installation, it will  run some checks, format the disk images, create fstab and mount everything into /target (you have to disable the partman override though since in the Live CD it works differently).

### Post by ehst on 2007-02-12
Insert in the start of the bat file this line...

@echo off

It removes the commands in the prompt and show only results e "echo text" commands.

Ps.: I'm testing the version 0.0.6. Downloading in this moment...


My version of the bat script...


```
@echo off

echo Setting boot.ini atributes...
attrib -h C:\boot.ini
attrib -r C:\boot.ini
echo Done!
echo

set /p DRIVAR=Which drive would you like to install to (If in doubt, type in "C:") ? 
echo Creating install destination...
mkdir %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
echo Done! Moving files...

move linux %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move license.txt %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move README.txt %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move preseed.cfg %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move initrd.gz %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move home.img %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move root.img %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move swap.img %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move grldr %DRIVAR%\
move grub %DRIVAR%\
move menu.lst %DRIVAR%\
echo Done!

echo Making back-up of boot.ini and setting boot options...
copy C:\boot.ini C:\boot.bak
echo %DRIVAR%\grldr="Ubuntu" >> C:\boot.ini
attrib +r C:\boot.ini
attrib +h C:\boot.ini
echo Done!

echo Press enter to begin downloading the Ubuntu 7.04 alternate iso file. If you already have it, just close this window and place it in C:\ubuntu.

pause

wget http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/feisty/herd-3/feisty-alternate-i386.iso
move feisty-alternate-i386.iso %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
echo Installation complete, please reboot and select "Ubuntu".

pause
```

Sorry people, my english is very sux.. but I try post with simples words for a best read.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-12
Thinking about it, is there any need for the above installer? Isn't that supposed to be an NSIS task? Isn't this second installer going to confuse users? I think we are stretching 7z a bit too much and reinventing the wheel. Sticking with the nsis installer for main releases and tgz for the experimental builds is a good approach. And if we wanted to make things simpler for beta tests wanting to try experimental builds, we should simply create another NSIS app with appropriate versioning/name. In fact the NSIS frontend is fairly independent from the back-end and it can probably be used with different versions without too much extra work. Also because there is little point now to have more than one initrd, since the new one can work with any ISO.

Speaking of versions and file names, it is probably time to make file names and version numbers a bit more consistent, they look like projects coming from different individuals (which they are), but now it is time to merge stuff.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-12
> Thinking about it, is there any need for the above installer? Isn't that supposed to be an NSIS task? Isn't this second installer going to confuse users? I think we are stretching 7z a bit too much and reinventing the wheel.

What I was thinking of was splitting up the NSIS installer into several small installers for each interactive step and using the setup.bat file to call them. We could strip each of the NSIS installers into just dialogs by removing the initial prompts, and have the setup.bat do the noninteractive stuff in the background (in a hidden command line @echo off so that users don't freak out). This way, the user doesn't notice that there are several "installers"; they'll just see one.

So, the iso download dialog should have its own NSIS script, the install to drive dialog should have its own NSIS script, and additional features should have their own NSIS scripts; this way we can keep our NSIS script nice, short, and manageable.

The advantage to doing it this way is that the setup.bat script can do many things that NSIS can't, like attrib -h C:\boot.ini attrib -r C:\boot.ini attrib +h echo %DRIVAR%\grldr="Ubuntu" >> C:\boot.ini C:\boot.ini attrib +r C:\boot.ini which removes read-only attributes from boot.ini, edits it, and puts them back later

Also, by using my method, everything in the process of creating the installer can become automated, so we can instantly create new releases instead of having to modify the installer for each version; all we do is add the NSIS executables into what the buildsfx.sh downloads, and add them into setup.bat, and everything will work automatically.

Also, it will make this installer much more extensible, as if we need features NSIS can't provide, we just create the additional executable however we want to, add it into what the buildsfx.sh downloads, and add it into setup.bat, and it will be seamlessly integrated into the installer.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-12
> I could only boot it once, the second time around it had all sorts of complaints about filesystems etc.

Yep, just as I feared, it isn't unmounting the loopmounted filesystems properly, that's why it didn't reboot for me, same issue as I had in v1 and v2, but when I swapped to reiserfs for v3 everything magically worked.

Ago: perhaps you should switch to reiserfs? Or if it isn't the filesystem itself, see my livecd-v2 installer for the unmounting scripts? This issue was fixed in my versions a long time ago.

> I tried formatting the images as reiserfs but for some reason it was locking the installer

swap out the line in the initrd that says mount -t ext3 with mount -t reiserfs and adjust your fstab, it should work fine afterwards

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-12
> It might be due to the umount script being unable to "unwind" the peculiar ext3 mounts, but I cannot check right now. If anyone can pinpoint the issue let me know,

Of course that's the issue; it's trying to unmount /media/windows before /, but because / is a loopmounted file in /media/windows, it can't unmount /media/windows because / needs it to be mounted, and it can't unmount / before /media/windows because /media/windows has its mountpoint within the /

The only fix I can think of is moving /media/windows out of / then unmounting / then unmounting /media/windows

to move a mountpoint:

```
mount -o move /mnt/point/1 /mnt/point/2
```

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-12
Or, we could try to create a ramfs, copy some essential files and libraries over to the ramfs, move the mountpoints of / into $ramfs/root, move the mountpoints of /media/windows to $ramfs/windows, chroot into the ramfs, unmount /root, unmount /windows, and force powerdown (thus clearing out the ramfs), kinda elaborate, but it will work no matter what

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-12
Anyhow, Ago, any thoughts/ideas about my proposed 7z-NSIS hybrid installer autogeneration solution?

> **tuxcantfly said:**
> What I was thinking of was splitting up the NSIS installer into several small installers for each interactive step and using the setup.bat file to call them. We could strip each of the NSIS installers into just dialogs by removing the initial prompts, and have the setup.bat do the noninteractive stuff in the background (in a hidden command line @echo off so that users don't freak out). This way, the user doesn't notice that there are several "installers"; they'll just see one.

So, the iso download dialog should have its own NSIS script, the install to drive dialog should have its own NSIS script, and additional features should have their own NSIS scripts; this way we can keep our NSIS script nice, short, and manageable.

The advantage to doing it this way is that the setup.bat script can do many things that NSIS can't, like attrib -h C:\boot.ini attrib -r C:\boot.ini attrib +h echo %DRIVAR%\grldr="Ubuntu" >> C:\boot.ini C:\boot.ini attrib +r C:\boot.ini which removes read-only attributes from boot.ini, edits it, and puts them back later

Also, by using my method, everything in the process of creating the installer can become automated, so we can instantly create new releases instead of having to modify the installer for each version; all we do is add the NSIS executables into what the buildsfx.sh downloads, and add them into setup.bat, and everything will work automatically.

Also, it will make this installer much more extensible, as if we need features NSIS can't provide, we just create the additional executable however we want to, add it into what the buildsfx.sh downloads, and add it into setup.bat, and it will be seamlessly integrated into the installer.

---

It is much better to preload all files from windows, i.e. base the installer on HD-Media (or ubiquity). This is exactly our current installer. The only reason you cannot partition, is that I have disabled this functionality, for security reasons, but  I might move it back so that it is activated if special boot parameters are used. Now the priority is to get the installation working on a loopmounted file. Doing it the "old way" is not one of the objectives.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-12
Ago: also, another thing you may want to do is add a boot.img disk image, WITH a 512 byte MBR, and mount it with a 512 byte offset as /boot:

```
losetup -o 512 /media/windows/boot.img
mount /dev/loop5 -t ext2 /boot
```

then have GRLDR chainload it as an actual hard drive:

```
title Ubuntu
find --set-root /ubuntu/linux
map --read-only /ubuntu/boot.img (hd0)
chainloader /ubuntu/boot.img
rootnoverify (hd0)
boot
```

Why? In order to allow for kernel updates; currently, anybody who updates the kernel will be left with a broken system, but if we do this, and modify the mkinitramfs scripts properly, we will have a fully functioning, upgradeable system.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-12
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Ago: also, another thing you may want to do is add a boot.img disk image, WITH a 512 byte MBR, and mount it with a 512 byte offset as /boot

This seems like a good idea. I will try it tonight.

By the way, you can also build the images yourself, it is a good thing for you to familiarize with the new building process. Use the src release, edit the appropriate files (src/initramfs/...). Then go to the project root folder and run make && make install and you will find the image under ./dist. You will find the instructions inside the archive, but it should be fairly straightforward. You need to be running the same kernel as the target image and have all required files installed (ntfs-3g) and with 755 attributes. Next release I will move back to extracting packages directly, I am using copy-exec from initramfs-tools, but it is unsatisfactory since it pulls in glibc which almost doubles the initrd size. The alternative is to install locally the required binaries compiled aginst klibc.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-12
Hi,

Sorry I havn't been around for a while, but I have done some testing lately, and have come up with a few problems:

Install seems to take a very long time.  Installing on real hardware (not top of the line but still reasonable), I left the install going for over 5 hours before going to bed.  By the morning it was done, although it had rebooted into windows (any way to avoid doing a reboot after installation would be good, especially since some users will want to leave windows as the default bootloader entry).

When I got it all going everything went ok, logged in etc.  and worked great the first time (a few app crashes, but I guess thats because all the other apps aren't final).  On the next startup, lots of things went wrong though, although it did eventually get to the desktop, due to the fact lots of config files couldn't be opened I had a plain (basic theme) desktop and pretty much nothing would work.  I copied the entire system log to a file and I've attached it so you can browse through it.  I can make another while in debug more if thats helpfull.  You can also see the timings in the log, and I thought that might help to identify where the delays are.  To my untrained eye, it looks like the unclean unmount has corrupted a few things and it now can't cope with it.




As far as the NSIS installer goes with batch files, I think thats the wrong approach, because in windows batch files are nowhere near as powerfull as bash scripts.  NSIS scripts on the other hand do offer most of the niceitys of modern compilers, like for example includes, defines, conditional compilation, etc. allowing us to make a modular design which is easier to maintain.

As far as batch being more up for the job of the actual installation, I'm not sure I agree with you there, because nsis scripts can call any windows API, they can effectively do anything a windows program can do if you have enough patience to research all the api's involved (exception there seems to be effective multi-threading, but anyway).  As one example, in your installer you append another line to the boot.ini, but have you thought how hard it would be to remove that same line again with a batch file?  (I can see some horribly messy code with "find /V ... > c:\boot.ini" coming up).  Another pitfall of using a batch file is it's hard to have a progress bar without having a very inconsistant ui, as the single threaded batch file has no way to pass status to the nsis ui while a process is occuring, unless for example piping stdout to the nsis ui, which would be hard because all the possable outputs (errors, etc.) would have to be catered for seperately.

As far as adding functionality we might need to NSIS, I think it would be best to do it with C++, since nsis allows for loading dlls and executing exported functions from in them, effectively allowing anything to be done.  It can use any library compiled for win32, even if not modified for nsis with the "System" plugin, which I'm already using.

Going back to the modularization point, I think thats a very good idea, and I'll try to add it with multiple nsis files for different sections and include them all from a base file in the next major version of the installer, although I'll keep on developing the current version in parallel for the moment to support ago's updates to the initrd.

As far as what nsis script can do, changing file atributes, copying\extracting files, adding and deleteing registrty entries, and ini file entries etc. are all one line built in commands, and calling any windows api to do anything (eg. to hide/show a window, to check free disk space, connect an internet connection, or get the current username) is a single line with the system plugin.  The harder things are lack of decent data types (no floating point, no proper arrays, everything is global scope), although they can be worked around because it can handle memory allocation and access, allowing you to effectively create your own structures, and batch files can't exactly do arrays either.



I've created a new installer with those new files, and it's up on the cutlersoftware site([http://www.cutletsoftware.com/ubuntusetup/releases/setup-ubuntu.exe](http://www.cutletsoftware.com/ubuntusetup/releases/setup-ubuntu.exe)) although I wouldn't recomend it over the v3 release yet due to the above problems I had.

If anyone has any feedback on installation time or startup time, I'd be interested because I'm trying to find out if it's some oddity in my hardware setup or more generic.

---

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-12
it was mainly the installing packages that took a long time, as it seemed to take a few minutes for every package, with the HD light on solidly throughout, so I suspect some PIO/DMA problem as ago suggested.  I think the same issue might be continuing after installation, because it still takes 3 min 50 sec to get from the boot menu to the desktop.  The creation of the image files by nsis takes next to no time now (< 1 sec per file), although I don't know about formatting them, although I suspect thats quick as well.

I'm still unclear about which way you're planning on modularizing it - are you planning to have the nsis setup exe extract the batch file and execute it hidden, which in turn executes the nsis setup.exe once for every dialog, with different command line parameters so it knows which dialog to show?  Or are you using nsis as the base, which then executes different batch files (hidden) to do parts of the hardware detection/installation?

Either way, I still think it's best to modularize it at the source code level (different script files linked with includes etc.), but not at the end user side (because using batch files are really fairly inflexable when it comes to doing a lot of complex tasks, and it would be better to have different plugin dlls, if we're seperating it to allow parts to be compiled seperately).

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-12
I don't know how much of a priority everyone thinks hardware detection is, but it would be quite easy to get windows to spit out a list of all the hardware id's of every piece of PnP hardware.  That then needs to be checked against some kind of online database to check for compatibility out of the box, with special drivers/tweaks, partially, or not at all.  However there don't seem to be any good hardware databases around (I don't mean wiki lists, but rather searchable databases so a script can search it and highlight to the user any potential issues before installation).  To start with, it should just highlight potential flaws (eg. your disk controller isn't supported at all), as most driver issues are best sorted from within ubuntu itself, unless of course it's a vital driver.

We either need to find an ubuntu hardware database and customise it to do the searches we need, or create our own (which wouldn't be as much work as it seems if users submit the data).  It would be a very good way to see which hardware needs supporting, because on every installation, with the users permission, unsupported hardware can be logged and then a centralized system counts the most common problem hardware.  Every PC does have a LOT of hardware, usually many hundreds of devices, so we certainly need an auto-search.

All that sounds great, but the problem is while I can make and support the hardware detection code, I don't think I have time to make a server-side searchable user friendly hardware database.  (php and mysql projects always seem to take longer than I expect), so the real question is does any web developer out there like the idea of this as a project?  It would probably encompass all the other main linux distros as well, with the possible creation of a compatibility checker that can run on windows/linux to show which linux distros are supported on any given hardware.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-12
> I'm still unclear about which way you're planning on modularizing it - are you planning to have the nsis setup exe extract the batch file and execute it hidden, which in turn executes the nsis setup.exe once for every dialog, with different command line parameters so it knows which dialog to show? Or are you using nsis as the base, which then executes different batch files (hidden) to do parts of the hardware detection/installation?

The way I plan on having it happen is to have a 7-zip auto-self-extractor run first (takes roughly 10 sec here) then that will launch a batch script in the background (with the echo off option, so it isn't seen) and the batch script calls the individual NSIS dialogs that were compressed within the self-extractor. In other words, the user sees it extracting for 10 sec, then a nice nsis dialog pops up, asks a few questions, another one pops up, etc. The main advantage would be that we can easily modularize the installer; just add in another entry into the setup.bat file in the 7z self-extractor, add the new nsisdialog.exe file into the self-extractor, and it'll run. Also, we can have extra functionality outside of NSIS; just add it into the batch file, add the .exe file into self-extractor, and it'll work. Then, whenever you need to build the installer, you just this script: [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/buildsfx.sh](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/buildsfx.sh) and it will do it all for you, with a few modifications it can just download the source itself and build automatically

something like this: [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-exe.html](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-exe.html) only without the command line ugliness; instead, we'll have NSIS dialogs

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-12
It's an idea, but I'm still not sure I like the idea of batch files.  How about replacing the batch files in your design with say VBScript files (there is a vbscript interpreter in windows, just like the batch interpreter in windows).  That gives a lot more power, while still keeping the open un-compiledness of a batch file.

IMO, it would be better to seperate all the dialogs like you suggest to seperate source files, but then have them all compiled together by the nsis compiler, because that still allows data to be easily passed between windows\forms.  It also allows for files to be extracted sequentially to speed up the responsiveness of the installer.  There is also the problem that some things the current nsis installer does that are basicly impossible to accomplish with batch files, like calling Kernel32::SetFileValidData to stop the kernel zeroing out a newly created file.  One other thing is the batch file must do it's own window management in that case to hide the black dialog box, unless the 7z extractor can do that.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-12
I'm just thinking the batch files can't be as simple as you envision, because they've got to make the decisions as to which forms should be displayed, and the installer now isn't as linear as it used to be, since there are lots of routes through the program:

Normal  > edition select > username
Expert > Loopback > data source select > destination select > advanced options
Expert > Burn CD > data source select > cd burn options
Expert > Partition install > partition select (as yet not made)

Before deciding which routes to take, or allow the user to take, the code will have to manage, for example handling of resuming partial installations, detecting a broken installation, detecting if bootloader repair is required, detecting if the torrent download is complete etc.

The main issue is that in that case, it has to be the batch file that detects those things, since they have an effect on which forms need to be shown in which order to the user, and in my opinion quite a lot of those things would be easier to do with a more powerfull scripting language (vbscript, nsis, maybe even .net, java, python etc.)

Also, I didn't manage to figure a way without external apps to hide the batch file, because even with @echo off, the black box with flashing cursor is still shown, even if it stays blank.


EDIT:  re ago's comment, yep, agreed keep the two seperate, but we still want a building system for the frontend, unless it's a single command as it is now. (just run makensisw)

> **Hello1024 said:**
> I'm considering a pre-installation hardware detection to detect hardware thats incompatible with ubuntu (everyone must have tried to get ubuntu going on some old machine just to find it simply won't work, at all, unless they become an advanced user and trweak some config file from a boot disk)

As the author of this spec  [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/SupportedHardwareListProposal?highlight=%28supportedhardwarelist%29](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/SupportedHardwareListProposal?highlight=%28supportedhardwarelist%29) I could not agree more. 

The implementation though should be kept fairly simple, it should only be based on a black-list (creating a white-list as in the spec above is far more complex). Each blacklisted hardware should be associated to a localized warning message. Since the blacklist depends on the kernel, it can be built-in within the installer, there is no need to download it (you may miss some warning, no big deal).

I think though that this should be pushed to the next release.

### Post by ehst on 2007-02-13
A new version of bat install script... but I don't tested now, only more later I can test this version in Windows XP. I think this script version work great.

Now, the script offers a choice to download or not the Ubuntu CD image. Comming  soon a "default value system" to previne user don't enter a value in initial questions. If don't entered, a default value is used.

Sorry if this modification don't work.

```
@echo off

set /p DRIVAR=Which drive would you like to install Ubuntu to (If in doubt, type in "C:") ? 
set /p BOOTVAR=Which drive would you like to install the bootloader to (If in doubt, type in "C:") ? 
set /p ROOTSIZE=What size, in MB, do you want your root partition to be (If in doubt, type in "4000") ? 
set /p HOMESIZE=What size, in MB, do you want your home partition to be (If in doubt, type in "4000") ? 
set /p SWAPSIZE=What size, in MB, do you want your swap partition to be (If in doubt, type in "1000") ? 

echo Moving files...
mkdir %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move linux %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move license.txt %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move README.txt %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move preseed.cfg %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move initrd.gz %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
move menu.lst %DRIVAR%\
move grldr %BOOTVAR%\
move grub %BOOTVAR%\

echo Creating disk images...
qemu-img create -f raw root.img %ROOTSIZE%M
move root.img %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
qemu-img create -f raw home.img %HOMESIZE%M
move home.img %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
qemu-img create -f raw swap.img %SWAPSIZE%M
move swap.img %DRIVAR%\ubuntu

echo Making back-up of boot.ini and setting boot options...
attrib -s -h -r C:\boot.ini
copy C:\boot.ini C:\boot.bak
echo %BOOTVAR%\grldr="Ubuntu" >> C:\boot.ini
attrib +r +h +s C:\boot.ini

echo Press D to begin downloading the Ubuntu 7.04 alternate iso file. If you already have it, just press S to skip this step and place it in %DRIVAR%\ubuntu, then reboot.
echo.
CHOICE /N /C:DS Enter your choice (D or S): %1

IF ERRORLEVEL ==D GOTO DOWNLOAD
IF ERRORLEVEL ==S GOTO COMPLETE

:DOWNLOAD
wget http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/releases/feisty/herd-3/feisty-alternate-i386.iso
echo Download complete, moving Ubuntu image...
move feisty-alternate-i386.iso %DRIVAR%\ubuntu
GOTO COMPLETE

:COMPLETE
echo Congratulations! =)
echo Install complete, please reboot and select "Ubuntu".
GOTO END

:END
pause
```

### Post by ago on 2007-02-13
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> but when we get to the portion where we want to start autoconfiguring hardware and importing settings, I'm pretty sure we'll run into something NSIS can't do...

Are you sure about that? From what Hello mentioned, it really surprises me that DOS can do things not achievable with NSIS via direct windows API calls, C++ interface and so forth... Also because it looks like you can call DOS from NSIS ([http://forums.winamp.com/showthread.php?postid=2018532](http://forums.winamp.com/showthread.php?postid=2018532) ) ... I do not know how good NSIS really is, but the windows shell certainly does _not_ have a stellar reputation... Moreover NSIS was engineered for doing installations, which is exactly what we need, + it is being used by major projects. It looks to me that you guys are reinventing the wheel, trying to build an installer out of DOS which is something I have not seen done in the past 15 years.

My only real critique to NSIS is that it is not portable (yet), but DOS has no advantage when it comes to portability. I think that on the front-end the most important separation should be: 

1) info fetching, 
2) bootloader modifier, 
3) GUI + download manager   

I have put the download manager within the GUI because it needs to exchange events with it (progress/cancel signals...). 1 and 2 are clearly platform specific, 3 can be made platform independent.

By the way NSIS can be complied on POSIX from what I have read. There is no need for wine.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-13
Ok, new idea then, this should solve the issue of keeping the NSIS installer up to date:

let's isolate the files themselves from the NSIS installer (initrd, kernel, grldr, grub, menu.lst) and simply have the NSIS installer extract them from the backend, in other words, we simply have the installer check the current directory for the ubutu-installer-backend.tgz and if it can't find it there, add a dialog:

> Do you have the ubuntu-installer backend? If not, you can download it:

== Browse Files ==                                 == Download Backend ==

If he selects browse files, he can just select the ubuntu-installer-backend.tgz file and continue without downloading from the internet

Then, for the feisty iso download prompt, we do the same thing, check for feisty-i386-alternate.iso in the directory, and if it can't find it:

> Do you have the Ubuntu 7.04 ISO? If not, you can download it:

== Browse Files ==                                 == Download ISO ==

This way, we eliminate the need for both an offline and online installer; if you want to install offline, just bring the feisty-i386-alternate.iso and ubuntu-installer-backend.tgz along with yourself and place it in the same directory, and it will all automatically work. Also, it eliminates the need to build a new installer for every new version; just use the old installer and download the new backend, we'll only need changes to the installer if there are changes in the locations of the files in the backend, or if we want to add new features to the installer itself.

Also, Ago, mind removing the diskimages.tgz file from the backend? Since we're autogenerating the disk images using qemu-img (at least in my autogenerated batchfile based installer, not the NSIS installer just yet), there's no point of having them; it'll just make the file size larger and increase extraction time. As for non-windows users, just add into the readme:
```

cd ubuntu
dd if=/dev/zero of=root.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[1000*1000*4]
dd if=/dev/zero of=home.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[1000*1000*4]
dd if=/dev/zero of=swap.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[1000*1000*1]
```

Shouldn't be too hard to do, and will be way faster than extracting them, and will allow for better flexibility in determining disk image sizes.

So? Any ideas on my proposal to split the backend from the installer and use it separately to prevent having to recreate the installer and have 1 unified offline/online version?

goemon OR mubi (mac ubuntu installer frontend)
fujiko OR lubi (linux ubuntu installer frontend)

### Post by ago on 2007-02-14
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> 
Let's isolate the files themselves from the NSIS installer (initrd, kernel, grldr, grub, menu.lst) and simply have the NSIS installer extract them from the backend
Tux, I agree that files should be separated in the sense that the .tgz or .7z should be generated by the backend make command and the frontend should mostly unpack. But I think they should be bundled with the installer. 

Things are fairly fluid at the moment, but hopefully they will stabilize, so there will be 1 release of the installer for each Ubuntu release and that should be tightly integrated with the initrd, since each version of the installer will target an Ubuntu version, therefore a specific kernel, therefore a specific initrd. Therefore there is far less need to split the download. 

This will also avoid asking confusing questions (you really want to avoid explaining users what an initrd is) and have people mixing old versions and new versions. Of course we can check that, but then we will have to through an error message and yet another dialog. Let's simplify things and bundle everything. 

The image can be cut down to about 5-6MB by avoiding using glibc which at  the moment is imported by copy_exec. I will take care of that later on.

It is far more important to check whether there are existing installations, and take appropriate actions. We certainly do not want to overwrite existing files since that would be equivalent to formatting the Linux disks. We should minimize the number of questions assuming that the user has almost no experience. So in the case of an existing installation, the installer should automatically: 

1) check the initrd version and if it is compatible, overwrite the initrd, otherwise create a new one, 

2) create a root.img with a different name from the existing one (by the way root-img should be called using the Ubuntu/Debian version, so it will be Ubuntu-7.04-386.img), it should be difficult for users to install  Ubuntu-7.04-386 twice with the same name. To do that they will have to change the name manually (since the default will be something like Ubuntu-7.04-386-B) AND confirm their action after a big warning message.

3) use the existing home.img and swap.img, 

4) create appropriate entries in menu.lst. The initrd can support multiple installations by using the root_img, home_img and setp_iso parameters.

> Then, for the feisty iso download prompt, we do the same thing, check for feisty-i386-alternate.iso in the directory, and if it can't find it

I like the idea. The installer should check, without asking, whether a compatible ISO is already on the disk or if it needs to be downloaded. If it can find a usable ISO it should default to that (you can change that in the wizard). I am not sure what is the best way to look for an ISO. A quick solution is to register the location of the ISO when it is downloaded, and to look for the folders we normally use, for folders mentioned in existing menu.lst, and for ISO files 2 levels on each partition. And of course physical CD-Roms.

As you say, if we cannot find an ISO, that the user should be able to select the ISO manually, but we should not ask him/her anything at all. We should simply preselect as an installation option that the installer will "Download and Install XYUbuntu". If the user wants to use an existing ISO he will have to select "use existing ISO" in the Distro selector. So the distro selector will have options like: 

.  Download and Install Xubuntu 7.04, 
.  Install Kubuntu 7.04 (this implies we autodetected such ISO) 
* Install Ubuntu 7.04 (this implies we autodetected such ISO)
.  Download and Install Fluxbuntu 7.04
.  Use an installation file/CD I already have [browse]

When the user passes an ISO manually we should check that it is a compatible one. 


The mantra is that we should ask as little as possible, autodetect as much as possible, and preselect the most appropriate option. If users want something different they will have to change the default settings in the wizard.

> Also, it eliminates the need to build a new installer for every new version
As mentioned, this is a problem in this stage, since there are a lot of changes, but proper releases should work differently. There should only be 1 every 6 months. By the way we should change the folder name from "releases" to "daily-builds" or "experimental-builds". By "releases" I understand it is a "finished" product, while at the moment we are pre-alpha. 

> Also, Ago, mind removing the diskimages.tgz file from the backend?
Will do. That was only intended for the development stage.

> As for non-windows users, just add into the readme
I have done better than that, I have started creating Fujiko... [http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=359614](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=359614)

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-14
Hi,

Quite a lot of the things you suggested are already working in the nsis installer.  If the NSIS installer detects an iso with the correct file name in it's directory or other obvious locations (desktop, my documents etc.), It won't do the download but instead use that.  The current version of the installer also won't overwrite any image files, although we still need a user prompt to ask what to do in case the files exist (indicating a previous installation has already been done)

As far as multiple installations goes, I've been pondering over it and I suggest that allowing multiple installations might confuse users - most applications are either installed, or not installed.  They can't be installed twice.  I suggest if a user tries to run the installer again, they should be prompted to go to expert mode and install again, reboot to use the ubuntu installation, or uninstall the current installation.

Obviously, for the advanced mode it should be an option.  One thing to consider is if multiple installations share the same initrd, then what if one of the installations is upgraded to a later ubuntu version, then the initrd will no longer be compatible with the other installation using it.  For that reason I suggest that each installation uses it's own copy of all the files (with the possible exception of home).  To identify which files go with which installation, we should either give them all a file name prefix, or insist on each installation having it's own directory (although that does limit some other things, like sharing a home image).  The main thing is we need to ensure however it's set up it's not easy to accidentally overwrite any of it.  Let me know your thoughts on that.


As far as seperating the installer and the files it installs, I agree with both of you on those points - for final versions, they should all be bundled together in a single nsis installer, because that will slightly reduce size, and allow the installer to start quicker, however to speed builds up and allow anyone to build it without the nsis compiler, we can very easily have the installer get the files from the directory it's in, and then bundle the whole lot in a self extracting archive so the user never needs to know.   The code changes to do that are minimal so I'll try to make a version tonight.

Well done on setting up launchpad with all the projects.  I'm not one to comment on names, but any of the ones you suggested sound easy to remember.  I'm just looking around launchpad now.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-15
> Quite a lot of the things you suggested are already working in the nsis installer.
Excellent.

> although we still need a user prompt to ask what to do in case the files exist That might be avoided doing as described above

> I suggest that allowing multiple installations might confuse users - most applications are either installed, or not installed.
But here it is different, since we offer different versions of Ubuntu, i.e. our installer provides different products. It would be like a single Mozilla installer that let you get thunderbird or firefox, but only one at the time. It would not make much sense. Either we simply install Ubuntu Desktop and give no other choice (the user can always install other desktop environments from within Ubuntu) OR we let the user choose multiple Ubuntu flavors but we also allow multiple simultaneous installations. 

> I suggest if a user tries to run the installer again, they should be prompted to go to expert mode and install again
Technically there is no problem in having multiple installations. That simply equates to have multuple root images and correspective entries in menu.lst. With multiple flavors this is pretty neat (you will have one menu item for Ubuntu and one for Kubuntu), and it is neat if you install different versions of the same flavor (Kubuntu-7.04, Kubuntu-6.10). It becomes confusing if you install twice the same flavor with same kernel. In this case you will end up with menu items such as Kubuntu-7.04-A, Kubuntu-7.04-B.... Even if we only allow to install Ubuntu, we should decide what to do when the user runs the installer again. There are 3 cases here: he might want to upgrade distro, which should result in a new menu entries as described below, he might want to reinstall because previous installation went sour, he is running the installer by mistake. I think we should default to install with a different name for the root image, and if he changes to an existing name, we ask for confirmation. The user can also change the installation folder as one of the advanced options, but I would default to the existing folder, since avoiding wasting disk space is a priority. In any case, I would move the distro selector to the advanced settings.

> then what if one of the installations is upgraded to a later ubuntu version, then the initrd will no longer be compatible with the other installation using it
As explained above we should only overwrite an initrd when the new one is targeting the same kernel. This is a safe operation. Old initrds should simply be left in place. I do not see the problem in having 2 initrds or 2 kernels in the folder

title new installation
kernel /xyz/boot/linux.new
initrd /xyz/boot/initrd.new

title old installations
kernel /xyz/boot/linux.old
initrd /xyz/boot/initrd.old

By the way I would suggest to tide up the windows folder structure using

/wubi/boot/   -> contains initrd(s), kernel(s)
/wubi/boot/grub -> copy of menu.lst, copy of grldr copy of grub.exe
/wubi/virtual_disks/ -> root.img, home.img, swap.img
/wubi/docs/ -> README.txt
/wubi/uninstall/ -> uninstall.exe
/wubi/tools/ -> make an nsis app to handle typical tasks like creating disk images, resizing them (can be done from windows???)...

> however to speed builds up and allow anyone to build it without the nsis compiler, we can very easily have the installer get the files from the directory
I agree. That said once a user has a folder structure in place, he does not really need the installer anymore. All he has to do is replace the initrd and regenerate root.img. The last bit is tricky, since if you make it easy to regenerate root.img then users can also erase it simply by mistake.

> Well done on setting up launchpad with all the projects. I'm not one to comment on names, but any of the ones you suggested sound easy to remember. I'm just looking around launchpad now.
For now I have only set up the lupin product and the Lupin Team. Now we need to setup a product for each frontend, but before doing that we need to decide a naming convention. If there are no other candidates the choice is between Jigen+goemon+fujiko on one side and wubi+mubi+lubi on the other.
   
I would like to hear from users as well. I have a feeling that geeks will prefer jigen, and non-geeks will prefer wubi. Since our target is non-geeks we should probably go for wubi. Which is a real shame since the linux geeks will end up with lubi as opposed to fujiko...
   
Once we have a name for the windows frontend, I think it is up to you to register the windows gui product and upload your code. The code should be uploaded under the lupin team, so that each member can change it.
   
we should also ask the admin of ubuntuforums to open a dedicated product forum. I am against maintaining our own forum on the webiste since ubuntuforums is a great resource for beginners, and pointing a newcomer to that directly will spare him/her a lot of troubles and spare us a lot of general questions that are unrelated to the installer. Since the vast majority of the users will come from the windows frontend, the forum should be named after that product, we can add subforums in the future.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-15
> By the way I would suggest to tide up the windows folder structure using

/wubi/boot/ -> contains initrd(s), kernel(s)

Wait, we want to keep the initrd and kernel in a seperate disk image (boot.img), which will be mounted with a 512 byte offset as /boot and will contain an MBR, so that GRLDR can simply chainload into the GRUB installed on the MBR of the boot.img; that's the only way we can have the kernel and initrd upgrade properly.

Why the huge hierarchy, though? When you have long paths, GRLDR often has issues reading the files; I'd say just keep everything stored in C:\wubi to minimize the chances of error.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-15
in case you're interested in how to build the boot.img with a 512 byte MBR:

```
dd if=/dev/zero of=boot.img bs=1000 count=0 seek=$[1000*50]
sudo losetup -f boot.img
sudo fdisk /dev/loop0
Command (m for help): w
sudo fdisk /dev/loop0
Command (m for help): n
Command action
   e   extended
   p   primary partition (1-4)
p
Partition number (1-4): 1
First cylinder (1-6, default 1): 
Using default value 1
Last cylinder or +size or +sizeM or +sizeK (1-6, default 6): 
Using default value 6
Command (m for help): a
Partition number (1-4): 1
Command (m for help): t
Selected partition 1
Hex code (type L to list codes): 82
Command (m for help): w
sudo losetup -o 512 -f boot.img
sudo mke2fs /dev/loop1
```

I've attached mine if you don't want to do it manually

Then, during the installer phase, just have it setup the loop devices:

```
sudo losetup -f boot.img
sudo losetup -o 512 -f boot.img
```

Then have it install GRUB to the first /dev/loop# file, and have it treat the second /dev/loop# file as the partition

To mount it during bootup:
```

sudo mount -t ext2 -o loop,offset=512 boot.img /boot
```

To have GRLDR chainload it:

```
title Ubuntu
find --set-root /wubi/linux
map --mem /wubi/boot.img (hd0)
chainloader /wubi/boot.img
rootnoverify (hd0)
boot
```

Did you have the MBR properly set up, with GRUB installed to it, and the filesystem set up with a 512 byte offset containing the kernel and initrd, and did you have it mount properly (with the offset option), and did you have the bootable flag set up, and did you have the filesystem with the offset in the partition table with the correct partion hex code? Did the error come from GRUB or during the boot process?

from [http://grub4dos.sourceforge.net/grub4dos_examples.html#q4:](http://grub4dos.sourceforge.net/grub4dos_examples.html#q4:)

> Emulates an image file as the first hard disk and boots DOS from it (though it should work with Linux too; it's just chainloading):

```
map --read-only (hd0,0)/harddisk.img (hd0)
chainloader (hd0,0)/harddisk.img
rootnoverify (hd0)
boot
```

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-15
tux:   I have made a version of the installer that can be run from a batch file, and uses grub, grldr, menu.lst, linux, initrd.gz and preseed.cfg from the directory it's in.  By sticking that in a self extracting archive, and running it, it will behave exactly like the version with the files embedded.  It will allow anyone to rebuild the installer though without the proper compiler.  It's up on the cutlersoftware site, in the root dir because by itself it's not really a release.

In the source code, I've added a define so it can be built either with or without those files embedded very easily.  The size of it at the moment is just under 1MB, but 90% of that is the python binaries to run the torrent downloader, so I'm looking to changing to libtorrent to reduce size.


Ago:  My vote is for Wubi etc.  I havn't done any more testing on the reboots etc.

As far as using a boot.img and chainloading, doesn't that mean the menu.lst with the kernel parameters in must be inside the boot.img file?  That might cause problems for the nsis installer because it then can't edit the file if there are any parameters in there that need changing (for example the iso name).  If using a boot.img file is the way to go forward, then I expect I can "force" nsis to edit the menu.lst in the boot.img file simply by editing the image file directly, and searching for the offset of the menu.lst - oviously I can't change file size then and it's a bit of a hack, but it's doable.

EDIT:  I just had seccond thoughts about the boot.img idea.  I think it's not good because when using that method, the boot order is:  BIOS > Windows Loader > GRLDR > GRUB (on ntfs) > GRUB (in boot.img) > LINUX.    That means there are now 3 bootloaders in the chain, and 3 menus for the user to potentially get lost in, and 3 small delays while bootloaders load.  Add that to the above, and I think it's a compelling reason to investigate other ways to allow initrd updates.

One other thing, I mentioned a while ago the possible problems with two installations sharing a kernel/initrd, and I still think it's a problem, because consider the following:

Joe installs ubuntu version A from windows
Joe installs Kubuntu version A  from windows (and same kernel\initrd is used)
A new version of ubuntu\kubuntu version B is released 6 months later.
Joe starts ubuntu, and runs the auto-update from within ubuntu, which upgrades to ubuntu version B
Joe now wants to start Kubuntu, and he can't because the root.img is of kubuntu version A, whereas the kernel/initrd is version B since it was upgraded.

Is that a problem, or did I misinterpret your solution?



EDIT:  I'm looking for suggestions on how to keep track of ubuntu installations.  The normal way to do that in windows is through the registry, but I don't think thats suitable because for one reason or another an installation could be copied to another pc, or it's possible there are two windows installations and it would only be in the registry of one.  Also, using config files to store the locations and versions and types of various installations is troublesome, because a user may still manually rename/move an installation, and an installation can be upgraded (through dist-upgrade), and then the version number is no longer correct.  The installer can parse the menu.lst for installations, and then search for them to find installations, but it still has no way to know what edition or version it is.  The exact same problems occur for uninstallations when there are multiple installs.

Possible solutions I thought of are to use a specially formatted comment line in the menu.lst with all the information required to locate and identify an installation, and sufficient information to uninstall\repair it.  If the user moves/renames the installation, they loose the ability to automaticly uninstall, although they can be pointed to a help page to inform them of possible actions.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-15
> **Hello1024 said:**
> Ago:  My vote is for Wubi etc.
Since it looks like Tux also likes Wubi, as well as most users who bothered to reply, then Wubi will be. 

Can you please register the Wubi product in Launchpad? I'll send an email to the admins of Ubuntuforums to open a dedicated product forum. This thread has been overstretched. 


> I havn't done any more testing on the reboots etc.
You should I cannot test on windows right now. If the issues above are ok, I'll create a new  image with the name changes and we can bundle v4.

> As far as using a boot.img and chainloading, doesn't that mean the menu.lst with the kernel parameters in must be inside the boot.img file?
You need 2 menu.lst, 1 in windows and 1 in linux, the one in windows. But at the moment there are issues with the approach above since it does not work on Linux, because grub does not work like in Windows. We should probably go on as we are now, and try to lobby the grub devs. 

An alternative to this approach is to mount a folder within the host file system as /boot.That should be doable and should be working on other platforms as well.The only issue is that then we need direct write access to the host folder, which is something I am not very inclined to do.In this case 

The only advantage of the above is to allow to update the kernel from within Ubuntu, At the moment the user can upgrade the kernel once every 6 months by re-running the installer, which is not so bad, if that is not acceptable we could create a small utility that upgrades the kernel/initrd on the windows side and can be even run periodically.
  
> That might cause problems for the nsis installer because it then can't edit the file if there
There should be no need to do that. 

> Joe starts ubuntu, and runs the auto-update from within ubuntu, which upgrades to ubuntu version B
If the user updates the kernel, at the moment the changes will be within root.img. But that is not the kernel that is used. The kernel that is used is the one sitting in windows, which cannot be touched from within Linux. Therefore there is no issue. The only thing to watch is that /lib/modules/<old-kernel> is not removed, i.e that the old kernel is not uninstalled from Ubuntu.

> EDIT:  I'm looking for suggestions on how to keep track of ubuntu installations.  The normal way to do that in windows is through the registry.
Yes we use the registry (store only a list of FOLDERS not a list of installations) + parsing of menu.lst. We can create a list of folders and installations within each folder, which is presented to the users as a tree with checkboxes, the user checks what he wants to be uninstalled and then clicks ok. If there is a single installation we skip this step.

The installation name is given by the title of menu.lst or the image file name (if they differ we use both). The actual contents are irrelevant. Home.img is only deleted if the full folder is to be removed.

> but it still has no way to know what edition or version it is.
It does not really matter if the user upgraded. The name and kernel versions are more relevant.

> Possible solutions I thought of are to use a specially formatted comment line in the menu.lst with all the information required to locate and identify an installatio
You already have the path of the folder, the root image name within that folder, the title name, and the kernel version. Do we need much more?

> If the user moves/renames the installation, they loose the ability to automaticly uninstall,
That is true for almost any application under the sun, nobody expect us to be robust under such circumstances.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-16
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Not just during kernel upgrades; if the user, say, installs a new usplash theme, it will also break the initrd
The only initrd used during boot time is the one in the /wubi folder, which is not affected by linux-side upgrades. Upgrades will change the kernel and initrd in /root.img/boot, which are not used. You can even delete /root.img/boot as far as I am concerned, and you will still boot. The only requirement is that /root.img/lib/modules/kernel-xyz is kept in place, i.e. old kernel must not be uninstalled. 

> kernel upgrades occur more often than 6 months through the security repos...
I know but for the time being they will only be able to upgrade once every 6 months. Which is not that bad to be frank. Far better than what the windows guys get. Security releases that affect kernel issues related to typical desktop use are quite uncommon. We can always create a small utility to upgrade kernel/initrd on the windows side. Or go for a mounted /wubi/boot partition But I really do not think it is necessary.

> Of course, if you don't want to use the boot.img
I want wan to use it, it is a neat solution, but I think that grub should be fixed first. I cannot even test that without windows. And I do not like the idea to have different back-ends floating around. The grub inconsistencies should not spread and make our software also inconsistent. Also because grub legacy will probably be dropped soon.  

> But, the prerequisite to all of these is to have a proper mkinitramfs based initrd-generator;
We already have. The way our initrd is built is via mkinitramfs in a 100% standard way. I simply use the available hooks to insert lupin stuff. If you want to build the same lupin initrd inside the target system you should be able to do so simply copying lupin/src/initramfs on top of /root.img/etc/initramfs-tools, then when you update the initramfs you will find yourself with a lupin-initrd. I have not tried that (since at the moment there is no point), but it should work.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-16
> **Aquashark said:**
> by the way: stop wasting time on how Joe will install Ubuntu, Kubuntu and Xubuntu at the same time
and focus on releasing a reliable installer for one version at a time that'll be very simple to install/uninstall, so Joe could try them easily one by one.
I agree. As explained above I am in favor of either allowing only Ubuntu in single installation mode, or all the flavors in multi-installation mode, but I tend to prefer the first option.

In single installation mode you can only be in 1 of the 4 situations below: 

1) Reinstall (installation with current version is detected), 
2) Download and Reinstall (installation with current version is detected), 
3) Install (no previous installation, or installation with an older version)
4) Download and Install (no previous installation, or installation with an older version)

Since only one option at any time will be true, this is not something the user can select, only a label. The user will still be able to choose the ISO manually in the advanced settings, the manual download option will be shown only if we are in a "Download" situation. If you run the installer a second time, and change the installation folder in the advanced settings, this will first move the old folder, than proceed as usual with the reinstallation, so that there will always be a single folder, and we can track where it is. Which I like. In the advanced settings we might have a "kernel upgrade only" option to add a new kernel/initrd and add new menu.lst items, without reinstalling root.img (only shown in a Reinstall situation).

You will still end up with multiple installations, but only when the next major release comes out, we will have to face that in Feisty+1, which buys us 6 months to solve the issues, and it simplifies the installer for the time being. Users can always install desktops from within Ubuntu. In fact this is probably preferrable. For advanced users we can have a "Barebone Installation" option in the advanced settings, which skips the ubuntu-desktop packages.

See below for an proof-of-concept implementation.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-16
Find attached a very preliminary draft of the lubi interface (see the glade file at the bottom of the post), You need glade-2 to display it properly. You can run it by placing the code below in a lubi.py file within the same folder of the glade file and running it with "python lubi.py". By the way, a multi-installation could easily be included in the GUI below by changing the behavior of the CD button in advanced settings, so that you use that to select the distro as well as to manually choose the ISO. 

PS Tux this could be a good start for the Lubi interface. Hello, have a look, and see if you can get ideas for wubi. If you cannot see the icons, adjust the links manually. The icons should be pointing to /usr/share/icons/Tango/32x32/...

```

#!/usr/bin/env python
import sys
try:
    import pygtk
    pygtk.require("2.0")
except:
    pass
try:
    import gtk
    import gtk.glade
except:
    print "You need to install pyGTK or GTKv2 ",
    sys.exit(1)

class LubiGui:
    def __init__(self):
        self.lubi_file = "lubi.glade.gpl"
        self.wTree = gtk.glade.XML (self.lubi_file)
        self.window = self.wTree.get_widget("dialog1")
        if self.window:
            self.window.connect("destroy", gtk.main_quit)
    def main(self):
        self.window.show()
        gtk.main()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app=LubiGui()
    app.main()

```

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-16
> I doubt that 0.0.0.6.exe is based on the version which should have fixed the issues mentioned... 

Using 0.0.0.6 DOES fix that mounting issue, for both me and Aquashark; as I was saying, there was apparently a major NTFS mounting regression between 0.0.0.6 and 0.0.0.7; I think the error has something to do with some synax error for the command "find" in some shell script, as while booting, it displays this:

```
Usage: find [path...] [expression]

default path is the current directory; default expression is -print
expression may consist of: operators, options, tests, and actions:

operators (decreasing precedence; -and is implicit where no others are given):
      ( EXPR )   ! EXPR   -not EXPR   EXPR1 -a EXPR2   EXPR1 -and EXPR2
      EXPR1 -o EXPR2   EXPR1 -or EXPR2   EXPR1 , EXPR2

positional options (always true): -daystart -follow -regextype

normal options (always true, specified before other expressions):
      -depth --help -maxdepth LEVELS -mindepth LEVELS -mount -noleaf
      --version -xdev -ignore_readdir_race -noignore_readdir_race

tests (N can be +N or -N or N): -amin N -anewer FILE -atime N -cmin N
      -cnewer FILE -ctime N -empty -false -fstype TYPE -gid N -group NAME
      -ilname PATTERN -iname PATTERN -inum N -iwholename PATTERN -iregex PATTERN
      -links N -lname PATTERN -mmin N -mtime N -name PATTERN -newer FILE
      -nouser -nogroup -path PATTERN -perm [+-]MODE -regex PATTERN
      -wholename PATTERN -size N[bcwkMG] -true -type [bcdpflsD] -uid N
      -used N -user NAME -xtype [bcdpfls]

actions: -delete -print0 -printf FORMAT -fprintf FILE FORMAT -print 
      -fprint0 FILE -fprint FILE -ls -fls FILE -prune -quit
      -exec COMMAND ; -exec COMMAND {} + -ok COMMAND ;
      -execdir COMMAND ; -execdir COMMAND {} + -okdir COMMAND ;

Report (and track progress on fixing) bugs via the findutils bug-reporting
page at http://savannah.gnu.org/ or, if you have no web access, by sending
email to <bug-findutils@gnu.org>.
```

Then immediately afterwards:

```
fusermount: mount failed: operation not permitted NTFS mounting failed
```

Then it throws me into the minimal shell.

If I try mounting with ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /host it fails with that same error message (the fusermount one)

On 0.0.0.6, though, everything works perfectly on NTFS except for the rebooting issue (improper unmount), I don't know if the later versions fix it because they don't boot

On 0.0.0.7, I noticed that the /usr/bin/fusermount command was missing in the initrd, so I added it, thinking that it would fix the issue, and that became 0.0.0.8, but it still doesn't work, even though I checked and all the fuse and ntfs-3g related libraries and executables are there.

However, using the normal kernel read-only NTFS driver, I was able to mount the NTFS partition just fine... but that isn't any good, because we need write support to the loopmounted filesystem.

Thus, I can only assume that the changes between the disk detection shell scripts between 0.0.0.6 and 0.0.0.7 broke ntfs-3g mounting, and thus, if you are using NTFS, stick with 0.0.0.6 for now.

As for the mouse issue, I heard that the new feisty kernel has some issues with certain mice, just wait; newer feisty kernels will likely fix it

EDIT: Nevermind, I've fixed the issue with 0.0.0.8, download it at [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-exe.html](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/ubuntu-setup-di-exe.html)

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-18
there is no basic xorg.conf; they're all generated differently on different machines

to generate it:

```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure -fnoninteractive xserver-xorg
```

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-18
check these folders:

/media/host
/media/windows
/ntfs

your windows drive should be there, if not:
```

sudo -s
mkdir /media/windows
NTFSPART=$(fdisk -l | grep NTFS | sed -e 's/\s/\n/g' | grep dev)
mount $NTFSPART /media/windows -t ntfs -o nls=utf8,umask=0222
exit
```

then it should be in /media/windows

are you using the experimental wubi-0.0.0.8 or old v3 version?

### Post by ago on 2007-02-18
I have published the code on bazaar. Tux, can you please use bzr from now on? There is no need to upload tgz files now. devel is going to be the main branch for lupin (Hello1024 you should also publish your code under wubi). The simple rule is that we should not commit anything on the devel branch unless we tested the code and it is somehow usable (i.e. people should always be able to boot when using devel branch). Experiments should be kept in separate/personal branches. The launchpad http server to browse the code has a bit of latency, so at the moment it is not possible to browse the source with a browser, but it is possible to use bzr with it.

[https://code.launchpad.net/~lupin-team/+branch/lupin/devel](https://code.launchpad.net/~lupin-team/+branch/lupin/devel)

It seems to work in my test cases with Herd4. 

Note: I have changed the directory structure under wubi, organizing files in more meaningful directories, this means that the INSTALLATION SCRIPTS AND MENU.LST SHOULD BE MODIFIED. Run make;make install and have look under /lupin/dist/. The initrd expects such folders when looking for files. In particular all the disk images should be under 'disks', preseed and ISO should be under 'install', initrd and kernel should be under 'boot'. The new menu.lst is in /wubi/boot/wingrub/menu.lst

Known bugs:

#2: there is still an issue with vfat. Tux, I'll be busy in the coming days, see if you can pinpoint the issue, it is likely a missing module. You can add the modules in src/initramfs/modules. You can try to modprobe/mount/umount manually by starting in debug mode, which will drop you to shell. Of course you need to put the modules in the initrd before modprobing. In fact you can place the modules somewhere under /host and use insmod with the full path (haven't tried that), so you can try the modules without having to rebuild the initrd every time.

#3: I still have I/O errors when shutting down on an ntfs partition. Tux, same as above. It seems difficult to umount/remount / , see if you can do that manually, try to replicate the rc shutdown commands and isolate the problem. See also bug #9.

#4: if you see some grep errors at the beginning of the boot process it is likely due to the upstream initramfs script which somehow fails when fuse is added as a module (loadmodules). If you remove fuse from src/initramfs/modules the error goes away, but then obviously you cannot use ntfs-3g & co. This leads me to believe that it is an upstream problem. If the cause is confirmed we need to post a bug report upstream. I did not investigate, but the grep error does not seem to have any practical effect on the rest of the process. 

#5: At the moment the init > mountroot is not really used for anything useful. I am passing ROOT=/dev/ram0 which fails without killing everything else. Then the first script after that is the init-bottom>lupin which actually does the real root(loop)mounting. This in effect amounts to replace mountroot with lupin, but you will see some error messages referring to ram0. There might be a more elegant way to replace/deactivate mountroot without directly editing the init script (ideally we should only use standard hooks like scripts/init-top, scripts/local-top or appropriate boot parameters...). Editing init directly is trivial, just replace mountroot with /scripts/lupin and move the lupin script from scripts/init-bottom to /scripts, then place the modified init script in overlay (that last bit is not guaranteed to work, since the overlay is controlled by mkinitramfs, it will work only if the hook is executed after the original init has been inserted, I have not tried it yet). If init is directly edited we need to be careful when the main init script is changed upstream (which is the reason why I prefer to use standard hooks).

#6: The initramfs comes with bin/fstype to detect the filesystem type. Unfortunately it does not detect the ntfs/fat. So I had to integrated that with the 'file' command which is added via overlay. fdisk does not work on files and it does not detect the fs type by magic. Ideally fstype should be corrected upstream.

#7: I am still using copy_exec in lazy mode, which means that glibc is imported, which means that the initrd is 2MB bigger than it wuld be if klibc or libc.udeb were used.

#8: /etc/fstab is static, other partitions should be added, possibly using the partman scripts

#9: At the moment there is no easy way to access the host partition from the hosted OS, this can be done executing `mount -o move` before run-init, but then we end up with a mount circularity which becomes annoying when shutting down.

#10: The hacks to disable partman/grub installation are shameful (but they work), the ones in bashing mode should therefore start form src/alternate-iso/sbin/lupin. The checks for the content of loop devices should also be improved (eufemism).



Of the above, the only annoying bug is #3, which therefore should have high priority,  #2 is relevant only if /wubi is on a fat32 partition, the other bugs should not affect the overall process and users should still be able to get a working installation. I haven't fully tested on sata. Hello, give it a run. Once #3 is fixed, and provided there are no other show-stoppers, Hello can create an installer around it. I'll add the bugs to launchpad later on.

TIP: if you boot with 'debug' (not 'debug=2', just 'debug'), all the output/errors of the first stage are redirected to /tmp/initramfs.progress which you can then read with 'more' once the shell is activated.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-18
Ago, I don't think we should be complicating the directory structure, even for the sake of organization; grldr really has issues with long names and paths; it often can't boot or read the files on the NTFS partition when there's multiple folders and paths. I think we should just keep it simple and store everything in C:\wubi to ensure that grldr can have the highest likelihood of being able to read the files on an NTFS partition.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-18
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Oh no, bzr itself isn't the problem... I didn't have it working with the sources.tgz file either; I always used to modify the initrd in the ubuntu-setup-di.tgz directly, only now that you're using bzr, I don't have access to the .tgz file with the prebuilt initrd anymore
I guess it's for the better then ;P

Once you learn to use the source with the make script, it is far more convenient than editing the initrd.... You'll find the instructions inside.

By the way I have uploaded the code for moving initrd and kernel to the toplevel folder. The new code is in bazaar. So now all files are in their folder except initrd and kernel which are under /wubi/. Whatever file is used to identify the folder (usually linux) must also be directly under /wubi/ 

> Nope, I've got a better idea... I'll just use the experimental lubi... Developing and testing at the same time, 2 birds in 1 blow... :)
You should still keep only code in the lubi bzr branch. Lubi should be in a separate branch within its own Launchpad product. But before the initial commit to the team branch I would wait until we have something resembling an alpha. You can start you own personal branch and when its ready it can be pushed to the lupin-team branch. By the way you can also have a personal branch for lupin and use it to play with edgy specific code, using the following command:

bzr branch sftp://bazaar.launchpad.net/~lupin-team/lupin/devel geza-branch

then you can commit locally with `bzr commit` (this will not change the official branch, but it will enable you to "go back in time" and undo mistakes which is neat). to sync with the official branch you use `bzr merge`. If one day there is anything you want to put into the main branch you can use `bzr push`. To build the image: cd geza-branch;make;make install

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-18
In fact you can have this nice setup: Put the bzr branch under edgy and make the folder share with the VM (see VirtualBox UserManual for how to do a shared folder). In the VM you link all the source folders, except for the dist and build folders. Then you write a script on the VM to run make+make install, copy /lupin/dist/* to /wubi, reset the HD and reboot the VM. Thus you edit the code in Edgy, but you build and test it in Feisty under VM, So you can edit the code while Feisty is building/testing

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-18
Ago, mind providing a list of deb packages you included in the initrd? I want to create a different initrd autobuilder script that will use dpkg -x on the packages, instead of copying them directly from the system; this will allow for increased flexibility, as to use a different version as the base of the initrd, all we'd have to do is use different packages, and it would allow people using any version to build any version.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-19
As you know I was using packages in the past, I moved to copy_exec since it is the standard initramfs-tool way to add executables, and it is quite convenient, but because of the glibc problem, I was already considering of reverting to packages. If you are also so inclined, we'll go back to packages I guess. The basic script is already available in the old versions, you can reuse that. But now the script should be more sophisticated. This is how I was planning of building it:

1) Each subproject (initramfs, live-iso, alternate-iso) contains a flat file called "packages" that contains the list of packages
2) you create a /lupin/packages folder to contain all packages from all subprojects. This folder should NOT go into bazaar, since it contains binaries
3) you add a script "make debget" that reads all the package lists, fetches all the missing packages, and puts them into /lupin/packages. You run "make debget" from "make build" 
4) you modify the overlay hook in initrams to read the package list, and use dpkg -x using the packages from /lupin/packages to extract them into the building dir (NOTE: the initramfs building dir is NOT lupin/build or src/initramfs/overlay, it is somewhere in /tmp. There is a special variable to indicate it, ${DESTDIR} or something, see the overlay hook).  
5) delete docs and manuals

Same thing for live-iso and alternate-iso, but you can worry about them later on, since at the moment I am not using any extra package. The above allows to include packages in the building process, while leaving the src uncontaminated by binaries.

The list of packages is basically the absolute minimum set required, to get the binaries listed in src/initramfs/binaries working. Udebs should be used whenever available. In particular, it is convenient to use mount-aes.udeb but then the scripts need to be changed since that package comes with losetup-aes as opposed to losetup. Things should be easier than before since klibc is already included in the initramfs (it was not included in the d-i initrd on which the old version was based upon), and mkinitramfs already takes care of the modules. 

The list of packages should be:

[http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/utils/file](http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/utils/file) (file)
[http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/utils/cpio](http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/utils/cpio) (cpio)
[https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/feisty/+package/mount-aes-udeb](https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/feisty/+package/mount-aes-udeb) (losetup)
[http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/otherosfs/ntfs-3g](http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/otherosfs/ntfs-3g) (ntfs-3g)

Probably you also need the following.

[https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/feisty/+package/libc6-udeb](https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/feisty/+package/libc6-udeb) (libc6, try first without it)
[http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/libs/libmagic1](http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/libs/libmagic1) (libmagic, required by file)
[http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/utils/fuse-utils](http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/utils/fuse-utils) (fusermount, required by ntfs-3g)

There might be some more package required by dependencies. But add them only once confirmed.

Use this link to see what are the available udebs: [https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/feisty/+search?text=udeb](https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/feisty/+search?text=udeb)
Search the content package to find a package starting from a binary name: [http://packages.ubuntu.com/](http://packages.ubuntu.com/)

I can work on that in the coming days, as mentioned I will not have much time this week, in the meantime you can use the old script and simply fetch the packages manually and place them in /lupin/packages.
