### Post by ago on 2007-02-19
> **Hello1024 said:**
> 
I'm guessing I have to register/create the branch before adding things to it, but how exactly?

You need to create the branch locally first.

So go to the wubi folder containing your code say it is in /home/devel/wubi

cd /home/devel/wubi
bzr init
bzr add . (there is a dot in there, will add all files in the current folder)
bzr commit -m 'initial revision' (will commit to the local branch)
bzr push sftp://omattos@bazaar.launchpad.net/~lupin-team/wubi/devel (will publish your branch, use the command above)

You need to register an ssh key in launchpad (do not use a simple passphrase). When you create your branch make sure to exculde any folder that contains binary stuff. You can follow my convention below. Before publishing, make sure the wubi tree is clean. You can use bzr log, bzr status, bzr unknowns to check that everything is in order.

> Yep, thats what I was expecting, although there is one slight hickup with allowing the user to select an iso.
Have you seen my proposal to have a single-installation mode? What do you think? Below that there is also some python code with a glade file. This is another GUI which will probably be used as a base for lubi, see if you can get ideas.

[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2164225&postcount=642](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2164225&postcount=642)
[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2164568&postcount=643](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2164568&postcount=643)

Tux, for you too.

> If the user selects the iso, there's no way nsis can know what edition\version it is, since windows can't mount an iso without special (non open source) drivers.
The distro flavor is not the issue. I can scan the CD and set the preseed desktop package parameter from my code. 

The problem is finding out the kernel version used, which is not something within the name. But this is not too annoying since with final releases since the kernel version does not tend to change and the filename is a good indication. It becomes an issue in pre-releases.

I will add detection for kerenel version so that the user is alerted in the early stages. It is annoying to find out that it is not the right ISO after a reboot, but it is not the end of the world, particularly considering that this should be an advanced feature. I guess the only reliable solution on your side is to run an md5 checksum. 

That said I would not be overly concerned about it. As mentioned, official releases are fairly stable in terms of kernel. And this is an advanced option anyway. 

> As far as directory structure goes, was the structure suggested for the source in bazzar, or for the installed systems?
For bazar my structure is

/lupin/src (contains the actual sourcecode), 
/lupin/scripts (any script to build the stuff or dev utility tool which is not used at runtime) 
/lupin/build (the working directory used to build stuff) 
/lupin/dist (the final product ready for disrtibution), 
/lupin/doc (any documentation)
/lupin/src/dist (static files that will go into /lupin/dist)
/lupin/packages (folder containing binary files that will be inserted in the finished build)

The folders build, packages and dist, which are the ones containing binaries, are ignored by bazaar (do not add those folders or use bzr ignore). Any content in /lupin/build and /lupin/dist is deleted and regenerated every time I build the system.

The installed folder structure (generated under /lupin/dist/) I am using at the moment is

/wubi/ (now contains kernel and initrd + all the folders below)
/wubi/boot (nothing in there, used to contain kernel and initrd, see tux post above)
/wubi/wingrub (files to be copied on c:\, including menu.lst, which you may want to edit)
/wubi/install (ISO and preseed.cfg)
/wubi/uninstall (your uninstaller goes here)
/wubi/tools (any small tool, like mini apps to generate the image files)
/wubi/doc (docs)
/wubi/disks (root.img, home.img, swap.img, extra.img, note: root.img should be named after the actual distro, same thing goes for the kernel and initrd, the kernel should be called "linux-i386-2.6.20-8-generic" for educational purposes)

> I think it might be best to go for a flat directory structure like we have now, especially since the number of files probably won't grow any bigger as the project matures.
Well if you add tools and docs and disk images and more kernels and multiple ISOs, than it will soon become messy. But tyding up is only part of the story.

The real reason, is that I wanted to avoid that when the user cleans up, he deletes an .img file by mistake. Image files are not going to go into the recycle bin since they are too big, they will simply be deleted forever (unless you use undelete tools). Deleting the kernel or initrd by mistake is not a big deal, but an .img file is like an hard disk. It is more unlikely for people to delete accidentally a folder than a single file (many will confuse .iso with .img...). Once you create the disks folder, the others are a consistent choice.

But if you guys prefer it, I will revert to a flat structure. For the reason above, I do not think it is a good idea though. We can probably simplify the dir structure but I would not eliminate completely. Particularly the disks folder.  Thinking about it, it is probably a good idea to rename "disks" to "harddisks" and use ".hd" as an extension for the images, just to be extra clear.

> I've also sent PM's to a few of the ubuntu forum mods and admins requesting a dedicated forum, also with no replies
Good let's all ask the same thing: wubi forum.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-19
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> is there any way you could have the installer put your version of mkinitramfs into the guest OS?
Sure it can be done, essentially you have to copy src/initramfs on top of /host/etc/initramfs-tools, but you have to take care of the binaries as well.

> Then, we can just symlink /boot to the windows parition/wubi, and then we'll be able to fix the kernel/usplash/initrd upgrade problem.
There are 3 issues with that: 

A) bug #9 (which I was thinking of addressing using -o bind,ro or -o move,ro... )
B) the fact that writing to the host from the guest is generally considered bad practice and writing stuff using an fs driver still in RC status is also considered bad. Put the 2 together and you may be in for trouble. At the moment we only write within a file without changing the file size, which is a safe FS operation.
C) to include the binaries, we cannot use copy_exec from the guest system, since the guest system is not guaranteed to have the required binaries, we have to use overlay, but to populate the overlay folder, we need to use copy_exec from the host system. It can be done but it is not trivial.

they have to be in a folder within the initrd (which increases the initrd size quite a lot) or in a folder within /wubi which exposes you to failure if an user deletes a file from there.

To be honest I think it is a lot of trouble just for a kernel update, and that will push back the schedule. I think we can fix that in the next release, there are more important issues at the moment.

> Oh yes, and I now finally have access to a feisty rig, so I can now use the copy-exec installer scripts; kubuntu feisty herd4 has been installed with my own wubi installer... Writing this message from kubuntu feisty...
1 happy camper 

"self-compilation"  is always considered an important milestones. I guess we should party.

:guitar:

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-19
> the fact that writing to the host from the guest is generally considered bad practice

We don't exactly have a "host" or a "guest", that logic is generally applied to virtual machines, but since, unlike in a VM, the "host" OS won't be running while the "guest" is, this logic doesn't apply; the usual problem is when the guest tries to write to something while the host is accessing it, but since only the guest is running, we won't run into this problem, so it's pretty safe to write to the host...

> and writing stuff using an fs driver still in RC status is also considered bad

From [http://www.ntfs-3g.org](http://www.ntfs-3g.org)

> The driver currently is in release candidate (RC) status, which means that no data corruption or loss has been reported during ordinary driver use, nor found in our extensive quality testing before release of the latest version and we're planning to release the RC version as stable in the near future.

ntfs-3g sounds pretty stable to me...

### Post by ago on 2007-02-19
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> But of course it will; we'll just have ntfs-3g, libntfs-3g, and fuse-utils be installed by deault (since we're going to need to have access to ntfs partitions anyhow), apart from that, all the libraries and binaries in the initrd are just the same standard ones included on every system
Not really. ntfs-3g is installed in the initrd not in the guest system. Of course you can install it on the guest as well using preseed, but that assumes that 1) there is a working internet connection, 2) or we put the deb somewhere and 3) that the user does not uninstall it. If you go for 2 you may just as well use overlays as opposed to copy_exec. Having the binaries in is not a huge issue, it is simply quite a bit of work. Is it really important? All I am saying is that I do not think it is high priority at the moment, we can do that in the next rlease.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-19
I think these cover the "corner cases": [http://www.ntfs-3g.org/quality.html#testmethods](http://www.ntfs-3g.org/quality.html#testmethods)

Also, a bunch of the major distros are already shipping with ntfs-3g by default, so those are probably already a couple thousand users

Anyhow, we already are using ntfs-3g, to mount the host partition, and are using it to write to the loopmounted file, and according to the tests, the creation/deletion of files works just fine...

As for the priority, I think this is next highest after the rebooting, as after all, the first thing users generally do after installing is a full system upgrade; it there's been a kernel/usplash/initrd security update since then, and if they're installing proprietary drivers or other modules (like just about every ATI and NVIDIA user does), they'll have major issues (complete xserver crash), because the system is using a different kernel than the modules and dpkg expect... And it's not exactly going to leave a good impression to tell them: edit the /etc/X11.xorg.conf file from the command line with vi, etc...

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-19
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> I think these cover the "corner cases": [http://www.ntfs-3g.org/quality.html#testmethods](http://www.ntfs-3g.org/quality.html#testmethods)
they'll have major issues, because the system is using a different kernel than the modules and dpkg expect
The "old" modules will still be there. So there will be no problem booting or using the modules. Simply put the user will not be able to use new drivers for 6 months. 6 months for drivers and kernel upgrades is absolutely nothing. Compare that to the windows world where users typically upgrade kernel and drivers once every 5 years when they buy a new machine...

Is this an issue for average joe? I doubt. 

And geeks will still be able to use new modules, by using a new release of wubi to upgrade the kernel on the windows side (see advanced tab). 

This is perfectly acceptable for this release. Next release we can fix that properly. Hopefully by then we'll have more developers on board.

Users will have problem ONLY IF If they intentionally UNINSTALL the old kernel , which in turns deletes /lib/modules/oldkernel, which is what we need to have in place. Installing a new kernel by itself is not a problem at all, and we will deselect the option to automatically uninstall old kernels. If the user then likes to poke into /etc/apt as root, and things break, it's his business. Even IF they manages to break stuff, they can easily fix it by running a new version of wubi to only upgrade the kernel.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-19
> upgrade the kernel on the windows side

Ouch... I really don't like the sound of that... shouldn't the process of installing new drivers/kernels/dist-upgrading be the same in our version and the official version?

> Users will have problem ONLY IF If they intentionally UNINSTALL the old kernel

Not exactly... Let's say you're following a guide to install nvidia or ati drivers (which are absolutely necessary because the vesa drivers that are used by default are unbearably slow), and a new security-fix version of the kernel has come out since you installed your system, and so when you install the nvidia/ati kernel modules, the linux-restricted-modules are only installed for the new, security-fix kernel, not the old one (that you're using) and since the xorg.conf is modified, but the old kernel will be loaded (therefore the nvidia/ati modules won't be present), you'll effectively be left with a broken xserver... of course, reverting the xorg.conf and downgrading the linux-restricted modules would fix this, but not many people know how to do this from the command line, especially those (beginners) we're targeting.

Same problem applies to just about anything that needs modules; vmware, kqemu, wireless drivers, funky hardware, etc.

> By the way I am more interesetd in bug #3. Do you have issues with I/O errors on reboots?

Yes, still the same old buffer I/O errors

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-19
as for sata drives, they've always worked perfectly for me; it's the pata drives I've never tested

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-19
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Ouch... I really don't like the sound of that... shouldn't the process of installing new drivers/kernels/dist-upgrading be the same in our version and the official version?
We have to match the current kernel version of the ISO. If that is upgraded we will have to upgrade Wubi. On top of that we can always provide kernel and initrd on the website for different versions, users who borked their system can use those as a drop-in replacement to quickly fix the issues.

> you'll effectively be left with a broken xserver... 
Not really. It's not us borking the system, it is the user playing with xorg.conf as root. If he ends up with a borked system it is hardly our responsibility. We deliver a working system. End of story. When you play as root with config files, there are endless ways to screw things up, it is not our role to prevent that. And if someone can edit xorg.conf (which cuts out the vast majority of our target audience) they can simply revert the changes OR download the new kernel/initrd.

Moreover the users that want 3d acceleration, can simply install the restricted drivers for our specific kernel version. All they have to do is fire up synaptic. That seems far more likely than editing xorg.conf to me.

> but not many people know how to do this from the command line
You can download the kernel/initrd from windows and place them on in /wubi. No need for X to do that. And even if you want to edit xorg.conf and do not like nano or cp, you should be able to count on [https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/bullet-proof-x](https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/bullet-proof-x)


Fixing #3 is far more urgent.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-19
did you put /devel at the end? "bzr push" by istelf should be enough

bzr push sftp://gezakovacs@bazaar.launchpad.net/~lupin-team/lupin/**devel**
Also it is good practice to:

1) make clean (just to be sure there are no binaries around)
2) bzr ignore ./build ./dist (only once)
3) bzr diff;bzr log;bzr status; bzr unknowns (check that stuff is ok)
4) bzr commit -m 'added required module for vfat support'
5) bzr push

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-20
Hi,

Although a lot of work has been put into the NSIS installer, I do think it would be worth looking at a cross platform front end, because if it all works smoothly, it's so much less maintanance looking after a single version rather than a version for every platform.

As far as updates of the kernel\initrd go, in my opinion all updates and changes should be done from the ubuntu side, because the user should never have to boot windows again after installation if they don't want to.  It sees like a bit of a washout to tell a user that ubuntu's so great, but you'll have to use windows again whenever you want to update it\change usplash and a few other things.

I think it might be best to say all updates can only be done from the ubuntu side, unless you want a "clean" install of a new version.  The only "repair" operations that can be done from windows are ensuring the bootloader is still installed, as it might be lost with a windows reinstall.

I think I agree that a single-installation mode is best, at least for an initial release.  Advanced users can always place the files manually.  If users want more than one desktop environment installed, it would be best for them to install one, and then use synaptic to add the other desktop meta-packages.  That way all updates/programs/settings etc. are common between them, which is again better for novice users.  It also saves massive amounts of disk space.

For the VFAT version, how was the 4GB file size limit worked around.  Since you can't get much of a system in 4GB, it seems like a fairly big limit.  Splitting up the different root folders to different images (var.img, etc.img tmp.img bin.img etc.) seems like a bad idea, because then any of them can overflow the 4GB limit.  Is there any support to attach a sequence of files to a single block device in linux, as that would seem like the best way forward to me.

As far as I can see, as soon as installation is done, the iso never needs to be used again, so it doesn't matter if the kernel version is updated and no longer matches.

Also as far as I can see ntfs-3g is stable enough for our uses, since the user won't be editing a lot of files frequently, the chance of anything going wrong is very low.  There are certainly bigger risks during installation, like a disk write error while updating the boot.ini or files in root.   Basicly, what I'm saying is that IMO it needs to be possible for mkinitramfs to build a new initrd.gz and save it to the host disk, just like it would rebuild the initrd.gz on a normal system after say a theme change.  Not as big a priority as some other bugs (buffer error on unmount), but still fairly important for a general release.

For stopping users deleteing disk images, I've implemented setting security settings on the disk images in such a way the user can't move/delete/view/edit etc. the disk images.  It is still possible to delete them using only the UI, but security permissions need to be changed before the files can be deleted.  I think that will be sufficient to stop the files being accidentally deleted, while advanced users can still manually delete them, and novice users can use the uninstaller.  The uninstaller can still delete the files by unsetting the permissions before deleting the file.

I've tested both SATA and PATA and both seem to work for me in the latest versions.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-20
> **Hello1024 said:**
> I do think it would be worth looking at a cross platform front end
The common routes for that are QT, XUL(runner), wxWindows, and to a lesser extent, GTK. I am a big fan of XULRunner, which will probably be the preferred route once FF3 is released (since most users will already have XULRunner installed and XULRunner has an infrastructure to support downloads and upgrades). But size does matter and I guess wxWindows might be the lightest of the bunch (didn't check to tell the truth). Have a go, see my code in the previous post (GTK). If GTK is the route, one option might be to port Ubiquity or a modified version of Ubiquity.

I think though that there is not enough time to change the frontend now. Therefore we should probably stick to NSIS for this release, make it work properly and then we can concetrate on the crossplatform interface for the next release. 

> As far as updates of the kernel\initrd go, in my opinion all updates and changes should be done from the ubuntu side
We all agree on that, it is a bit like the above. It is a a lot of work and it is probably unrealistic to hope it can be accomplished AND tested before Feisty is released. Moreover bug #9 is blocking this feature, there is no point in working on it at all until #9 is dealt with. Once that is addressed, we can certainly start an experimental branch. If the branch proves stable we can always merge it. For now users will have to come to terms with the fact that usplash cannot be changed. 

> I think I agree that a single-installation mode is best, at least for an initial release.
Agreed. That is probably far less confusing for new users and it makes the initial release much more feasible. 

> For the VFAT version, how was the 4GB file size limit worked around.  Since you can't get much of a system in 4GB, it seems like a fairly big limit.
People can use the extra.img and change fstab to mount /usr in there. We might do a little script them to migrate stuff to extra.img. But that too is not too high priority. You should enforce the 4Gb limit in the wizard though if the filesystem is vfat. 4GB for / + 4GB for /usr + 4GB for home will accomodate most users 

> Is there any support to attach a sequence of files to a single block device in linux, as that would seem like the best way forward to me.
Sure you can do a raid0/evms. But that again complicates things. Raid0 in fact is probably not a good idea, since it is fast on multiple disks, but doing raid0 on the same disk implies a LOT of seek times. Looks to me like another next release feature.  

> As far as I can see, as soon as installation is done, the iso never needs to be used again, so it doesn't matter if the kernel version is updated and no longer matches.
That might matter if you keep the ISO and run a new version of Wubi which has a new kernel on it. In this case you cannot use the old ISO.

> Also as far as I can see ntfs-3g is stable enough for our uses, since the user won't be editing a lot of files frequently, the chance of anything going wrong is very low.
Except that here you would be writing inside a circular mount: / is mounted on ntfs and ntfs/wubi/boot is mounted on /. That was preventing people from rebooting for one thing. And even without the circularity, we still have unresolved I/O errors. Therefore I would not assume too much.

> It is still possible to delete them using only the UI, but security permissions need to be changed before the files can be deleted.
I like that

> I've tested both SATA and PATA and both seem to work for me in the latest versions.
Good

---

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-21
Has anyone had any success tinkering with grldr and vista to get it to boot.  I've found, that contrary to some microsoft docs, the vista bootmgr CAN chainload 16 bit real mode code, without any special headers or anything.  To do that I did:

Bcdedit /create /application BOOTSECTOR /d “Test Entry”
Bcdedit /set {GUID} device boot
Bcdedit /set {GUID} path \testbootfile
Bcdedit /displayorder {GUID} /addlast

where "GUID" is the identifier returned by the first command, "Test Entry" is the entry name in the boot menu, and "testbootfile" is the file on the boot disk thats loaded and executed.

I've tried loading different things, but it seems the boot manager is quite picky about what it will execute.  I can write 15 bytes of my own assembly code in a file, and it will execute it, and I've tested different file sizes up to 200KB, however for the larger file sizes it seems to get unreliable.  If it doesn't execute the code it returns:

File:  \testbootfile
Status:  0xc000007b
Info:  The selected entry could not be loaded because the entry is missing or corrupt.

I have a feeling it may want the file size to be a multiple of the sector size for larger file sizes, or possibly it's checking certain bits in larger files to see that they're bootable.  small (<4kb) files always seem to be executed reliably, so prehaps the behaviour is different for different file sizes.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-21
> **ecology2007 said:**
> Wonderfull ! It works.:popcorn: 
Should i edit the menu.lst of the c: drive ?
yes, the one in C:\menu.lst. The "quiet splash" will add some eyecandy during boot

> I m not sure if i can access it from ubuntu. I think you did protect this drive on purpose with some magic, right ?
More like black magic... Anyway at the moment you cannot access C: from Ubuntu.

> Should i go back in windows and add some more boot option ? In order to select some specific kernel ? Is it worth it ?
The only kernel you can access is the one in C:\wubi, you can add all the options you want but it will still be that kernel, and it is not wise to change that (since it has to match the initrd). You can add the "quiet splash" option though to the main entry (not the debug one). You can also uncomment the "hidden true" option, so you do not see the grub menu at all (you can activate it with esc), and set the timeout to 3. Maybe make a copy of menu.lst first.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-21
> Should i go back in windows and add some more boot option ? In order to select some specific kernel ? Is it worth it ?

Don't bother with the more specific kernels; they don't exist. All the processor-specific kernels were phased out in 6.10 (and aren't present in 7.04, which we use) and all the optimizations were put into the generic kernel; installing linux-686 will not change anything; as you can see at [http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/base/linux-686](http://packages.ubuntu.com/feisty/base/linux-686) the 686 kernel packages are simply transitional packages to the generic kernel.

(thats while windows loads a high memory driver and the remainder of io.sys).

It's going to be hard coding a reliable installer/uninstaller for that though, because there are lots of corner cases for what could be in the config.sys (some programs create menu entries there, and put custom drivers in there, which we wouldn't want to load when booting ubuntu, but would want to load for the windows menu option)

Provided i make a backup before and i have enough space, is there a command to resize the root.img file ?

### Post by ago on 2007-02-22

### Post by ago on 2007-02-22
> **ecology2007 said:**
> Provided i make a backup before and i have enough space, is there a command to resize the root.img file ?
I'm pretty sure i saw something like that but i can't find where. (I think it was posted on this thread)
Any clue ?
Thanks forward.

There is a new way:

1) Create an extra.img of the desired size* and place it under C:\wubi\disks. 
2) Boot into Ubuntu and run the following code (you can cut and paste it into a terminal):
```

sudo mkfs.ext3 /dev/loop4
sudo mount /dev/loop4 /media/extra
sudo rsync -avx --exclude '/sys/*' --exclude '/proc/*' / /media/extra

```
3) Boot into windows and rename extra.img to root.img

Watch the spaces in the code sniplet. You can use the same procedure to expand home. For home.img just replace the last command with:

sudo rsync -avx /home/ /media/extra

then from windows rename extra.img to home.img. 

* You can use qemu-img for that, there will be a tool in /wubi/tools 
 
NOTE TO DEVS we could write a small script to automate the code above and run some sanity checks from within ubuntu + add a qemu-img tool in windows. We could just add /usr/sbin/copy_rootimg_to_extraimg and /usr/sbin/copy_homeimg_to_extraimg

### Post by ago on 2007-02-22
You can run qemu-img to create a file of the desired file. Every time an installation fails (once you get to stage where you see the scrollbars) root.img has to be regenerated since if it is not empty the installer will refuse to overwrite it, and try to mount that instead. You can run the installer in debug mode and have more information.

### Post by ecology2007 on 2007-02-22
> **ago said:**
> There is a new way:

1) Create an extra.img of the desired size* and place it under C:/wubi. 
2) Boot into Ubuntu and run the following code (you can cut and paste it into a terminal):
```

sudo mkfs.ext3 /dev/loop4
sudo mount /dev/loop4 /media/extra
sudo rsync -avx --exclude '/sys/*' --exclude '/proc/*' / /media/extra

```
3) Boot into windows and rename extra.img to root.img

Watch the spaces in the code sniplet. You can use the same procedure to expand home. For home.img just replace the last command with:

sudo rsync -avx /home/ /media/extra

then from windows rename extra.img to home.img. 

* You can use qemu-img for that, there will be a tool in /wubi/tools 
 

> 
Well, i have made an extra.img file in windows with qemu, formated it and mounted it succesfully in ubuntu using the exact commands you provided
Now i am copying the whole content of the / to the /extra/ folder using rsync, but it seems really really slow. The console pauses every minute on some file then restart and copy a bunch of files till next hang. It also uses 100 % cpu and copied so far 1 gb in more than 1 hour, which usually takes 1 minute or less.


I found what went wrong.

The ntfs-3g was taking 100% cpu when doing the rsync. 
I think i know why. The extra.img was somehow not correctly written on the c: ntfs drive.
I made it using the command ```

qemu-img create -f raw extra.img 10000M

```
It seems qemu-img does not really write the file on the drive and does not allocate the space for it. When make images with it, the free disk space does not change. When i delete a file made with it, the free disk space does not change either. When i run chkdsk after, it finds that the disk space is reported incorrectly.
That's why the ntfs-3g had so much trouble writing to /media/extra.
edit : I can reproduce it 100 % sure with any file size on primary and extended partition and on 2 different drives.

Here is the workaround i found. I made the extra.img on another partition then moved it the wubi folder. Then the free disk space was reported correctly.

After that the 3 following commands took 10 minutes and completed properly in a ubuntu terminal.
```

sudo mkfs.ext3 /dev/loop4
sudo mount /dev/loop4 /media/extra
sudo rsync -avx --exclude '/sys/*' --exclude '/proc/*' / /media/extra

```

Last step is to backup the root.img and rename the extra.img to root.img in windows.

I'll see if it works in a few minutes.

Perfect ! It works nice and i have now plenty of space to try every soft i can find in synaptic.:popcorn:
Thank you !

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-22
> It seems qemu-img does not really write the file on the drive and does not allocate the space for it.

That confirms what I had suspected for a while.  Qemu-img used on NTFS creates the file, and sets the file size, but doesn't actually allocate the file (thats why it's so quick).  When you then use the file from ubuntu, ntfs-3g is trying to allocate parts of the file (as a sparse file), making it use a lot of cpu and disk thrashing.  The copying solution works because the windows copy api reads the data to copy it, causing windows to allocate the file before copying it.

The nsis installer file allocation doesn't suffer from that (it uses a trick to make windows think the files been zeroed without the time of zeroing it - google SetFileValidData for details), although there is another bug which should be noted to frequent testers:

When an image file is deleted with the uninstaller, and then immediatly recreated by running the installer, it's possible the file will get allocated in exactly the same place on the disk, and as the file isn't wiped, you'll end up with a mountable image file, which will cause installation to fail.  A work around is to re-run the installer, as the chance of the file being allocated in exactly the same place a third time is very low.

The next version zeros the first 128KB of each image file to ensure it isn't mountable.

Unfortunatly I don't think it will solve bug #3...


EDIT:  for anyone using qemu-img, the trick to get windows to zero the file should be simply to open the file in any way.  either use a hex edtor and read the end of the file, or simply copy the image file to anywhere.  You could even open the file in notepad and see what happens, although it'll probably lock up your pc due to low memory before it's zeroed it all out.  You may also be able to echo the entire file to the console to zero it out, although I don't know how long that might take.  As soon as the file (or part of it) has been zeroed, the free disk space count will be updated.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-23
When we initially started, concerns about security descriptors were what headed us towards a loopmounted image - we need to make sure the security descriptors work from the ubuntu side otherwise we might end up with a very insecure system.  Other things that may cause issues are allowable file and directory name characters, alternate data streams, attributes, compressed\encrypted\sparse files, and symbolic and hard links, although I expect the ntfs team have fixed most of those.  to whoever mentioned sector size being a problem, NTFS is optimized for very small files because it can store those files entirely in the MFT, not requireing a sector for them.  I have tried using NTFS with a lot of files once (using cgywin to cross compile kernels), and when you have >100,000 files on the system things do get sluggish, although I didn't do any benchmarks at the time so I can't say how much.

As far as the windows virus risk goes, we can easily set file permissions in such a way that from windows nothing can access the directory (this is already done for some things like the "system volume information" directory in windows xp) - obviously this still can't stop a virus specially designed to meddle with the ubuntu data.

I'm a little worried about the risk of corruption of the ntfs drive, if it's being used for thousands of files with a not absolutely stable driver, it might cause problems.  The driver also has to be able to cope with the odd error that might occur from inproper shutdowns etc.   I have just spent today fixing a friends laptop where the filesystem (NTFS on XP) "forgot" about 1000 files randomly spread around the filesystem - after running a disk ckeck, all the file data turned up, but all the file names and locations were lost.  It's a bit of a lost cause to give it back and say heres all your data in one big file, but I've no idea where each file starts and ends, or where that file data came from.

On the other hand, there are quite a lot of advantages.  There is no issue of resizing image files if the user needs more space on windows or ubuntu.  There is no (or less) problem with kernel and initrd updates.  Performance may be better.

Ago, If you think it will be a fairly simple task to get a test version going, then we can see how things work out, but if it's a lot of work I think it might be best to stick to our current setup as we seem to be progressing quite well with that.

---

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-23
EDIT:  about using dd to convert image file to a real partition:

We can't really use that, or a system based on that, as a final solution, because consider this:

A user has a 60GB disk
The user has 25GB of files on a windows NTFS partition, which fills the disk.
The user has a 25GB ubuntu image file, on the same disk.
So overall, the user has 10GB free on their ntfs partition.

Whatever combination of resizing and copying they do, they can't convert their system to have two partitions - one for windows one for ubuntu, because they can only shrink the ntfs partition by 10GB, allowing them to create a 10GB ext3 partition, and then they can't copy over the whole of their ubuntu install.  The only solution in this case is to do multiple copy/resize/shrink operations to move all the data.

A much better longer term solution would be an offline conversion tool, which can defragment the ntfs partition in such a way the image file is contiguous and at the end of the disk, and all the windows files and allocation table are at the start.  The tool then shrinks the ntfs partition, leaving the ubuntu image outside the partition (now in free space).  It then creates a new entry in the partition table for the image, and you're left with the setup you want.   Thats a lot of work, so I think it's a job for someone else, IF there's the demand.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-23
> **Hello1024 said:**
> When we initially started, concerns about security descriptors were what headed us towards a loopmounted image - we need to make sure the security descriptors work from the ubuntu side otherwise we might end up with a very insecure system.  Other things that may cause issues are allowable file and directory name characters, alternate data streams, attributes, compressed\encrypted\sparse files, and symbolic and hard links, although I expect the ntfs team have fixed most of those.  to whoever mentioned sector size being a problem, NTFS is optimized for very small files because it can store those files entirely in the MFT, not requireing a sector for them.  I have tried using NTFS with a lot of files once (using cgywin to cross compile kernels), and when you have >100,000 files on the system things do get sluggish, although I didn't do any benchmarks at the time so I can't say how much.

As far as the windows virus risk goes, we can easily set file permissions in such a way that from windows nothing can access the directory (this is already done for some things like the "system volume information" directory in windows xp) - obviously this still can't stop a virus specially designed to meddle with the ubuntu data.

I'm a little worried about the risk of corruption of the ntfs drive, if it's being used for thousands of files with a not absolutely stable driver, it might cause problems.  The driver also has to be able to cope with the odd error that might occur from inproper shutdowns etc.   I have just spent today fixing a friends laptop where the filesystem (NTFS on XP) "forgot" about 1000 files randomly spread around the filesystem - after running a disk ckeck, all the file data turned up, but all the file names and locations were lost.  It's a bit of a lost cause to give it back and say heres all your data in one big file, but I've no idea where each file starts and ends, or where that file data came from.

On the other hand, there are quite a lot of advantages.  There is no issue of resizing image files if the user needs more space on windows or ubuntu.  There is no (or less) problem with kernel and initrd updates.  Performance may be better.

Ago, If you think it will be a fairly simple task to get a test version going, then we can see how things work out, but if it's a lot of work I think it might be best to stick to our current setup as we seem to be progressing quite well with that.

Yes, I've always thought that using ntfs as the root would be the best solution; see v1 as an example of a system that uses ntfs as /, only it has the usual issues of permissions errors. BUT I think I know how to solve this issue to get NTFS-as-root working with proper filesystem permissions support...

As I posted somewhere early in my original thread, we could use a UMSDOS-like system to get all the permissions support working under NTFS as /. This would be the best solution, as it would not have the performance overhead of using 2 filesytems on each other, would have a decreased chance of corruption (because the current system will make files unreadable if either the NTFS or EXT3 parition contained within it are corrupted) and would solve the quirks like the inability to shutdown (v1 shuts down fine, it seems like an issue that only affects loopmounted, not real, ntfs partitions), and it would fix the issue of being unable to access the NTFS partition, and the inability to update the initrd, and it would allow for conveniences such as being able to read and write your ubuntu documents (just /home, though; we don't want them messing with the root partition)without tinkering with stuff like Explore2fs, and it would be a heck of a lot easier to maintain; we'd just have to keep our kernel module or fuse driver updated, instead of tinkering with the initrd, startup/shutdown scripts, and everything else that we'd have to redo every release

Since we already have FUSE enabled in our initrd, we might as well use FUSE to create the UMSDOS-for-NTFS driver (UMSNTFS). I've googled around, and it doesn't exist; nobody has yet tried using NTFS as a root filesystem (except Microsoft Windows, of course). Using FUSE would be a lot better than a kernel module, as we wouldn't have to rewrite it every time the kernel changes something, so we can avoid the problem UMSDOS faced; the kernel changed, and it no longer compiles with the new kernels, because it would have taken too much work to port the system over to the new kernels, but if we use FUSE, this won't happen; FUSE is actively maintained, and given all the FUSE drivers out there, there won't be a sudden major regression that breaks everything. Also, if we plan to use this system on other distros (like Debian) we'd only have to add FUSE, NTFS-3G and UMSNTFS to the initrd and all will work; we won't have to tinker with compiling additional modules for their kernel or messing with the startup scripts. Essentially, if we use FUSE, once we write the UMSNTFS driver, we won't have to worry about it again.

Like UMSDOS, UMSNTFS should store the filesystem permissions in a file, instead of the filesystem itself. So, that would mean that instead of tinkering with all the undocumented NTFS internals, we can simply create a file (C:\umsntfs or something) that stores all the permissions and other stuff we need that NTFS-3G doesn't support, we can just store it all in whatever format we please within a file. Another advantage to doing this is that this would allow for different permissions on Windows and Ubuntu; we can hide the Ubuntu files in Windows and keep them read-only, while keeping them read-write and visible in Ubuntu. I was thinking that instead of having the ubuntu / be a subfolder, we have all the ubuntu files be directly in the NTFS partition, so we can mount it directly like any other filesystem and not have to worry about chrooting in and out of it, which will require us to tinker with startup/shutdown scripts and make this project much harder to maintain.

As for the perceived security issues and all of having all the ubuntu files directly on the windows partition, this shouldn't be an issue. We can simply add hidden, system, read-only tags to all the ubuntu folders with the installer (attrib +r +h +s) and the user won't ever see the files or be able to touch them, though ubuntu will still boot fine. And, this shouldn't be any less secure than the current system; if a virus can modify/delete files that have hidden, read-only, and system attributes, your system is doomed no matter what; there's no security mechanism in Windows that can save anything if this happens. The only possible problem that could occur, that wouldn't occur with the loopmounted filesystem approach is if the user already has folders in C:\ with the exact same names as ubuntu has for directories in /, but given that the vast majority of our target, inexperienced Windows users rarely venture out of the My Documents and Program Files folders, and are even less likely to use names like C:\sbin, this shouldn't be an issue. If they do have folders named C:\sbin or the like (though I'd seriously doubt it), we could always just have the installer ask them to rename the folder to something else, and proceed afterwards. If the user is running on a compressed filesystem, we can just disable the compression attributes on the ubuntu-specific folders and files.

So, I think this FUSE-based UMSNTFS + NTFS-3G + NTFS root filesystem approach would be way better in the long run than our current loopmounted filesystem approach, in terms of inter-version/distro portability, maintainability, functionality, stability, and performance. Given that NTFS-3G has just reached 1.0 (that is it's considered stable) and has not had a single issue yet with reading/writing files (other than compressed files, which we can fix by disabling compression of the ubuntu-specific folders) I think it's ready to be used. Unfortunately, I don't exactly know all that much about creating filesystem drivers using FUSE, so I think someone else is going have to create this UMSNTFS driver... Developers wanted...

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-23
Before we diverge. I made some progress on #3

The error is due to 

/etc/rc6.d/S20sendsigs stop (executed during reboot)

In particular the command that does not go down well is

killall5 -15

This kills the ntfs-3g process, therefore any subsequent r/w operation will fail and thus you see I/O errors. This only happens with ntfs-3g and not other filesystems because ntfs-3g is in userspace and therefore vulnerable to killall. S20sendisgs is executed just before umountfs which umount the file system. You can reproduce the problem by issuing at any time the command

killall ntfs-3g -15

You will see the usual I/O errors. I fear this will be an issue even if we mount directly on top of ntfs.

Then there is a separate error in /etc/rc6.d/S40umountfs, this script basically reads the mounts from /proc/mounts and then executes `umount -f -r -d $REG_MTPTS` on each of them. What happens is that /host is listed in /proc/mounts even if there is no /host. This is due to the fact that /host was mounted before chrooting and the old proc (which saved the info on that mount) was used as the basis for the new /proc, thus preserving old info on old mounts. The mountpoint though is not there (or we create a circularity, see #9) and therefore when running the command above it fails.

Ideas welcome.

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-23
> Ideas welcome.

When about to unmount filesystems and kill processes, create a ramdisk, copy over a couple of crucial files needed for rebooting (umount, init, mount, etc.), chroot into the ramdisk, and execute the remainder of the umount/reboot commands from within the ramdisk. This will also fix bug #9 as well; no longer will the / filesystem be in a circular mount; we can just do another mount -o move and that will move all the filesystems around so that they can be individually unmounted, loopmounted filesystems first, and finally the ntfs partition. Then once the filesystems are unmounted, we can just do a forced powerdown; the ramfs will be wiped clean and recreated anyhow.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-23
In fact it seems sufficient to get rid of 

killall5 in /etc/rc6.d/S20sendsigs

and replacing the 2 killall5 commands with a kill command which ignores ntfs-3g such as

ps axwww | grep -v ntfs-3g | grep -v PID| grep -v rc| grep -v init| awk '{print $1}' | xargs -n1 kill -15
ps axwww | grep -v ntfs-3g | grep -v PID| grep -v rc| grep -v init| awk '{print $1}' | xargs -n1 kill -9

Can you pls try that and see if the reboot improves?
I am not sure whether ntfs-3g is closed cleanly though since I doubt it can be umounted properly.

Here is the hacked /etc/rc6.d/S20sendsigs

```

#! /bin/sh
### BEGIN INIT INFO
# Provides:          sendsigs
# Required-Start:
# Required-Stop:
# Default-Start:     6
# Default-Stop:
# Short-Description: Kill all remaining processes.
# Description:
### END INIT INFO

PATH=/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

. /lib/lsb/init-functions

do_stop () {
	# Kill all processes.
	log_action_begin_msg "Terminating all remaining processes"
	ps axwww | grep -v ntfs-3g | grep -v PID| grep -v rc| grep -v init| awk '{print $1}' | xargs -n1 kill -15
	log_action_end_msg 0
	sleep 5
	log_action_begin_msg "Sending all processes the KILL signal"
	ps axwww | grep -v ntfs-3g | grep -v PID| grep -v rc| grep -v init| awk '{print $1}' | xargs -n1 kill -9
	log_action_end_msg 0
}

case "$1" in
  start)
	# No-op
	;;
  restart|reload|force-reload)
	echo "Error: argument '$1' not supported" >&2
	exit 3
	;;
  stop)
	do_stop
	usplash_down
	;;
  *)
	echo "Usage: $0 start|stop" >&2
	exit 3
	;;
esac

:

```


See if it works.
NOTE: if you use gedit, watch for the backupfile. gedit (and other editors) leave a backup copy: S20sendsigs~. You need to remove that or you will see an error message about it.
NOTE2: is still an issue with /host not found (see previous post for explanation)

Tux, I'll play with your solution but it is not obvious because of file dependencies... Looks like another copy_exec job,,,

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-24
Hi,

I read tux's long post about using ntfs aas the root.  While I agree with most of it, here are a few points I picked up on:

1.  using the root of the ntfs as the root of ubuntu isn't really to be reccomended.  Effectively that means each OS can interfere with the other, and from the users point of view it makes a lot of mess (Hidden or not I don't think it's a good idea for it to be there).  One c:\wubi folder is much better, although as a user I prefer everything in "Program files", although since this an entire OS we're talking about I'm happy where it is.

Your plans for UMSNTFS sound workable, although the problem of having nobody with experience of FUSE driver development is a big problem - I'm not sure it's worth starting to go down that route until we find someone, because it sounds like a big job to replicate all the functions of a kernel module, adding support for another file system, in a user mode driver from scratch.

For the current version:
As far as the ntfs unmounting goes, I don't think it's too much of a problem, because we're not changing the size of any files, so the risk of corruption is very low, although it's obviously an issue that should still be fixed sometime.


About the problems with updating the kernel and initrd from ubuntu:
Rather than having a system of circular mounts and symlinks, couldn't we have a shutdown script which updates the kernel and initrd on the host IF the local versions (the ones on the loopmounted image) have changed.  That way there's a lot more flexibility (the copy can be done after unmounting troublesome things), and there's no loss in functionality as the kernel and initrd will never be needed till the next reboot anyway.  It still requires a way to build the initrd on the target system though.

---

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-24
dpcfMander:  For Tux's version, yep.  If it's my (older) NSIS version, run uninstall from Add/Remove Programs.

I'm away at the moment (for the next ~48 hours), so I probably won't get a chance to recompile a new NSIS version (unless of course I can get some internet access thats not so costly on my phone - the 10MB installer would cost me £40 to download!)

The current NSIS version has partial localisation support - ie. generic bits of the installer are translated to most languages under the sun, such as the buttons, however non-generic bits, like the instructions and message boxes aren't yet.  I'll need to find some quick way of adding translations support to the source code so it isn't a lot of work to maintain when somehings changed.  Other changes in the installer are basic hardware blacklisting support, Windows 95/98/98SE support, and a few utilities to create disk images and repair the bootloader etc.

I'm still working on Windows ME support (any developer with windows Me is very welcome to put suggestions forward or try to get it working - the problem is windows Me has no support for a boot menu, or config.sys or boot.ini, so no way to chainload GRUB)

Also, with more priority I'm working on vista support, as I think that'll become more and more important.  I think we need to be able to compile grub or a grub loader to <64kb to allow it to be chainloaded.

ago:  a few pages ago you mentioned using lilo as a bootloader - what's the reasoning for that if grub covers all our needs already? (ntfs support, chainloaded from ntldr and chainloaded from config.sys)

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-24
I've noticed something rather strange with mounting the host NTFS partition...

If you use ntfs-3g to do it (sudo ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/extra) it works, you can access and write to your partition and if you attempt to unmount it (sudo umount /media/extra) it still works, no I/O or unmount errors.

If you use mount to do it (to use the kernel ntfs driver, sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/extra) it doesn't work, and gives out an error: (/dev/sda1 busy or already mounted)

If you use /etc/fstab to do it (add the line):
```
/dev/sda1               /media/extra              ntfs-3g                defaults          0       0
```
it mounts on bootup, but spews out buffer I/O errors on shutdown

Since using /etc/fstab should simply automate the manual ntfs-3g mount/unmount process on bootup/shutdown, I find it strange that it doesn't work, though ntfs-3g does. Maybe if we change the order of some of the shutdown scripts we can get bug #3 fixed?

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-24
> a few pages ago you mentioned using lilo as a bootloader - what's the reasoning for that if grub covers all our needs already?

We had thought of using lilo because of loadlin.exe which allows us to support older pre-NT windows versions, but since grub.exe in grub4dos package serves the same function (can be started from ms-dos), we have no need for that.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-25
> **tuxcantfly said:**
> Maybe if we change the order of some of the shutdown scripts we can get bug #3 fixed?
There are no mounts of ntfs devices from within ubuntu/fstab at the moment, the only mount (/host) is done within the initrd and it uses ntfs-3g directly (I was about to change that to mount -t ntfs-3g to address #5 in fact...). 

Unless there are still I/O error reports, #3 should be considered fixed. The remaining issues there are:

1) umounting /host at the end is not possible (because of #9), but I now send a kill -15 to ntfs-3g after root has been remounted ro, which should probably trigger a clean umount anyway, So that can be considered half patched in an hackish way.

2) We need to check that the current kill command (which basically kills everything except ntfsg/init/rc processes) is equivalent to killall5 (I didn't have time to look inside killall5). In fact we should patch killall5 upstream, since killing fuse stuff in S20 is wrong. Edit: I have submitted this bug report: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/sysvinit/+bug/87763](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/sysvinit/+bug/87763) (we should watch that, since once is fixed we'll have to remove the current patch).

By the way, I have also submitted this other bug report [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/fuse/+bug/87767](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/fuse/+bug/87767) affecting #4. Again, once this is fixed, we will have to remove my current patch (I haven't pushed that patch to bzr yet).

### Post by ago on 2007-02-25
> **dbgeezer said:**
> 
However I'm still having a problem getting it to boot to the right partition.  I'm on a Dell Inspiron E1505 and there are two partitions (a 47MB FAT, a 4GB FAT32 [recovery]) in front of the NTFS main partition.  I get to the FAT32.  Still working on it.
Use the latest exe, put the /wubi files in NTFS and make sure you have no /wubi folder inside the fat or fat32 partitions. 

> It seems as if there have been two or three re-releases of 0.0.1, but without any new version numbers.
Yes, I agree, that is not good practice, every new version should have a different "number", In the past the development was very fluid, and we basically pushed out a new version for every little change. Those were really intended as daily experimental builds generated for convenience (to spare users the trouble to build the image themselves), rather than real releases. 

Now that things seem to work, starting with the new NSIS release, we will have a far stricter and more conventional regime. 

From now on releases will be less frequent, they will have a dedicated release series branch in bazar, with a proper numbering convention. Talking of which I think we should drop the current numbering convention at least for the Wubi frontend. You do not want end-users to map Wubi 0.0.3 to Feisty and 0.1.2 to Feisty+1. We should instead  match Ubuntu's scheme. So users will see a jump from 0.0.1 to 7.04 (not bad!). 7.04 should be the main release series (in launchpad terms), then there will be several release points within that series (herdn, alpha, beta, final). This will be much clearer. 

For lupin I will probably keep the 0.0.1 convention (but still based on a proper release series), mostly because it is an internal project and I do not want other debian devs to think that lupin is ubuntu-specific.    

All current releases should be moved to an "experimental" folder, and we should only have 7.04 series in the release folder. Each and every build in there should match an equivalent bzr branch. Development will still happen on the main devel branch, and relevant changes will be merged to the release branch. 

My only doubt is whether the kernel and initrd names within wubi should match the kernel version or the release point (linux-2.6.20-8-generic or linux-7.04-herd1), I think the second one is easier for newbies but geeks will revolt.

Note to Tux and Hello: If you agree with the proposal above, I will soon add a release series to lupin, branching off the trunk (after I merge the fixes for #4 and #5). I will be audacious and aim at 0.1. Same thing should happen for wubi. The next NSIS release should be Wubi-7.04 Herd1.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-25
> **dpcfMander said:**
> Then I guess you don't want to know that I still see I/O errors, do you? :)  I've only used it once since the update yesterday, though; may be subsequent uses will clear it up.
If you did not use tux latest version, you will see the errors. If you want to avoid a new installation, you have to modify /etc/init.d/sendisigs and /etc/init.d/umountroot. Assuminig you did not already change the files yourself, open a terminal, and paste the code below:

```

sudo sed -i 's/killall5/ps axwww \| grep -v ntfs-3g \| grep -v PID \| grep -v rc \| grep -v init \| awk "\{print \\\$1\}" \| xargs -n1 kill/g' /etc/init.d/sendsigs
sudo sed -i 's/ES\=\$?/ES\=\$?;\/usr\/bin\/killall -15 ntfs; \/bin\/sleep 3/' /etc/init.d/umountroot
echo finished

```

Then try to reboot.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-25
> **lean said:**
> What is the progress?
Once Hello is back, and bundle the wizard with the installer, there will be a new release which will be almost fully functional.

> The important part is of cause is that a user can install Ubuntu 7.04 in Windows
Yes you will be able to install Ubuntu 7.04 in windows. In fact if you do not mind not having the wizard, you can do that already, using Tux package.

> and use the normal update tool to update kernels.
Not at the moment. You can update the kernel but that will not change the kernel used, so it is basically useless. You can still install restricted modules, and binary drivers, but only the ones matching the version of the kernel used by wubi. Anything like: "sudo apt-get install XYZ-$(uname -r)" will work. Wubi kernel will be the default kernel shipped with Ubuntu Feisty CD.

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-25
New NSIS installer made.

It's single installation mode.  Supports FAT32 (only partially tested) as well as NTFS.  Supports Windows 95 (part tested), Windows 98 (untested), Windows 2000 (untested), and Windows XP (tested).

Win 9x support isn't full yet, because the uninstaller still cannot remove the menu item, so you'll have to do that manually:
  start > run > type:
     attrib -r -h -s c:\config.sys
  press ok - A dialog box will flash up and then disappear
  then go start > run > type:
    notepad c:\config.sys
  press ok
  delete everything after and including the last [menu] line, this may leave an empty file, thats OK.
  save the file and exit.

Winder windows 2000/XP the image files are set so they can't be tampered with accidentally.  If you want to intentionally tamper with them, right click the file, select properties, and click the security tab.  Select to give yourself "Full Control", and then you can do what you like with the file.

If you already have the required iso, and don't want to re-download it, either use the expert mode and select the file manually, or put the iso in the same folder as the installer, and it will be auto-detected in normal mode.

The uninstallers been modified a lot, because older versions could have been dangerous if the file was moved (they could delete the folder they were in, including the c:\ drive if wubi was installed to c:\!).  Let me know if the new version works.

Is there support for using http downloads as the default? (rather than torrents).  IMO, that would put a very large strain on the mirrors if this installer gets popular, but http is probably more reliable than torrents through some firewalls/ISP's.

Direct link:   [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/setup-wubi.exe](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/setup-wubi.exe)

---

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-25
Going with the new naming scheme, I've renamed Hello1024's NSIS installer to [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/releases/wubi-704-herd4-r1.exe](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/releases/wubi-704-herd4-r1.exe) and it should now be referred to as Wubi 7.04 Herd 4 Release 1

Naming scheme, based on Ago's:

Final releases (based on the stable ubuntu version) will be Wubi (ubuntu version) Release (wubi release for that particular version)
Beta releases (based on the upcoming ubuntu version, like this one) will be Wubi (ubuntu version) (ubuntu codename) (wubi release for that particular version)
Experimental releases (same as Beta or Final, only with Wubi-specific experimental tweaks) will have the same version, except with Experiment (experiment #) after the name

File naming scheme:

wubi-(ubuntu version without period to prevent confusing extensions)-(codename if beta release, omitted if final)-r(wubi release#)-e(experiment number if applicable, omitted if not)

---

### Post by ago on 2007-02-25
Thanks Tux. Can we also organize the folder structure so that we have:

/wubi (html pages)
/wubi/downloads/archive (all our previous builds)
/wubi/downloads/experimental (wubi-7.04-herd4-v1...)
/wubi/downloads/releases (only final ones, wubi-7.04, so nothing yet)

When we publish a release we should also have a matching tgz, including both wubi source AND lupin source. The tgz will not be mentioned on the main page but it should be there (launchpad can use releases). 

So there will be 
/wubi/downloads/experimental/wubi-7.04-herd4-v1
/wubi/downloads/experimental/wubi-7.04-herd4-v1-src.tgz

On the home page we only have one big button with a link pointing to the best shot for new users (which now is /wubi/experimental/wubi-7.04-herd4-v1).  The linked version should be mentioned in the button text "Download Wubi\n7.04-herd4-v1 10MB". The other folders will only be known to affeccionados reading this forum, but will not be publicized on the website, nor there will be mention of the source file. At most we can have a "other download options" link (small font) below the main download button pointing to /wubi/downloads. This way we can clean up the wubi page quite a lot: 1 big button, 1 link for other download options and a short explanation. That's it. No need for the "installation" and "download" pages. We will have only a FAQ page merging any other info. Maybe a screenshot page.

Lubi downloads should be moved on separate folders in a separate "website" (mirroring the structure above but starting with /lubi) . There is no need to mention lubi from within wubi (windows users could not care less). We will create a separate page once it's ready. There should be no separate lupin builds other than the ones bundled within the installer, the source will be bundled as explained above. We can simply point people to launchpad somewhere in the faq. 

We should create only major release series in bazar. So we should have a "wubi-7.04" release series. 


EDIT: I see we have posted simultaneously.  :D I also had the beta folder, then I decided to remove it and only leave experimental and releases to make it clearer

EDIT2: I think I like the dot in the name (7.04) since that is what ubuntu uses. Is there any issue with that on windows?

EDIT3: maybe we should use -v1 instead of -r1? Since r is for "release" which might confuse users. I would not use the word "releases" for anything else than the final releases.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-26
> I see grub> and thats it i can't to find the kernel. I mean i even cleared 40 GB off my hard drive to see if that would help but it didn't.

I've seen this error before too, GRLDR apparently can't read the complex directory structure, always spews out:

```
Error 18: Inconsistent Filesystem Structure
```

and yes, this was after chkdsk and defragmenting...

try moving linux and initrd in C:\wubi directly into C:\ and removing "wubi" from the kernel and initrd lines in the menu.lst, that fixed it for me... this is yet another reason why we should keep the directories and paths small; GRLDR has trouble with long ones.

> **Aquashark said:**

.7.04  should not be noticeably slower than 6.10, even with wubi style install.
Make sure that your img files are in one piece with running defrag ->analyse in windows.
> also there were a lot of errors during reboot
it said something about corrupted journal and I/O buffer error on loop6 and 7
See if this help
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2202805&postcount=781](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2202805&postcount=781)
> also after uninstall the content of the Wubi folder isn't deleted and the boot.ini file is not a system & hidden file anymore 
That will be fixed next version.
> also the time in WinXP got screwed up..
Can you try to find out if your bios time is UTC or your real time, by entering your bios at boot.
By default wubi now assume that your bios time is the same time you have in windows.
To fix your clock in windows simply left click on your clock and select synchronize from an internet server.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-27
> **ecology2007 said:**
> I have made some test that were not that bad.:popcorn: 
I have succeded in making a folder autodetected given its host folder name, and automounted.
There is a spec for that within lupin ([https://blueprints.launchpad.net/lupin/+spec/direct-mount](https://blueprints.launchpad.net/lupin/+spec/direct-mount)) , Feel free to expand on it. Keep me posted.

> Now i'm gonna ask something i should have asked first :
is all the magic of the boot located in the sources of the lupin and lupin-function files (initram folder) or is it also floating somewhere else ?
The rest is in alternate-iso overaly. Those are the patches which are applied dynamically to the alternate-iso to make it work with the loopmounted files and it does the actual installation (it basically replaces partman). You will need to change that too since now it assumes that the target devices are /dev/loop7 & co and it will format them if they are not empty. You basically have to change the target and avoid the formatting, also there is no point in having a separate /home. You will also have to change the section which edits /etc/fstab. The trickiest part though is to modify /target/etc/initramfs-tools so that new initrds are clones of lupin. You also have to make sure that ntfs-3g is installed in the guest system (you can use preseed.cfg but you cannot use any downloaded package). I hope it helps, 

I am fixing [https://bugs.launchpad.net/lupin/+bug/87833](https://bugs.launchpad.net/lupin/+bug/87833) (you can have a look at the other bugs too, maybe there is anyone you fancy...) then I will join you.

By the way #87833 involves splitting lupin in 2 scripts and it will require to use "mount -t ntfs" as opposed to "ntfs-3g dev mntpoint". Can you please test already whether "mount -t ntfs" creates problems when used in the initrd? Replace the ntfs-3g within safe-mount in lupin-functions and give it a spin. I was assuming it was the same thing, but after reading tux post I am not that convinced anymore.

### Post by tuxcantfly on 2007-02-27
> Will this installer work in Windows 2000 Professional Edition?

Yes it should

> But before doing that i would like to know if you had started something about it and if you don't mind sharing.

Yes I have done plenty of work with ntfs root, check for the versions prior to version 3 in my old thread at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=305109](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=305109) those all used ntfs as root, note that they're rather buggy though.

For the error with inconsistent filesystem, move initrd.gz and linux from C:\wubi into C:\ and change the menu.lst:

```
title Ubuntu - Dynamicly created by setup-wubi.exe
find /linux --set-root
kernel /linux find=/linux fb=false quiet priority=critical root=/dev/ram0
initrd /initrd.gz
boot
```

### Post by Brandonson112 on 2007-02-28
> **tuxcantfly said:**

Yes I have done plenty of work with ntfs root, check for the versions prior to version 3 in my old thread at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=305109](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=305109) those all used ntfs as root, note that they're rather buggy though.

For the error with inconsistent filesystem, move initrd.gz and linux from C:\wubi into C:\ and change the menu.lst:

```
title Ubuntu - Dynamicly created by setup-wubi.exe
find /linux --set-root
kernel /linux find=/linux fb=false quiet priority=critical root=/dev/ram0
initrd /initrd.gz
boot
```

### Post by ago on 2007-02-28
> **Brandonson112 said:**
> I still get an error 17: file not found

Once in the grub menu you can press 'c' and you should get a shell. Then you can type the commands above manually. The advantage of the shell is that if you type part of a command and hit the tab key, it will tell you all available combinations. You will be able to see with the eyes of grub. The find command should find the root for you (by root we mean the partition in which wubi lies), but it may be failing, therefore it might be wise to override it and specify the disk number and partition number manually. The only command you are interested in is:

kernel (hd0,0)/linux ...
or
kernel (hd0,0)/wubi/linux ...

Where "0,0" indicates the disk number and partition number (starting at 0). You will need to put some numbers appropriate for your setup. You will not need the "find" line anymore. You can hit tab at any part of the command above. For instance if you type "kernel (hd" and then hit tab, you will see a list your harddisks. "kernel (hd0,"+tab will list your partitions, "kernel (hd0,0)/" will list the content of main folder on the first partition of the first harddisk... See if you can find linux like that.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-28
> **ecology2007 said:**
> I will forward you the code or publish it on launchpad/bzr
If it is on bazaar, make sure that code with different behaviour (such as direct mounting) goes in a separate branch. 

> 1-Do we still need the line ntfs in src/initramfs/modules ?
Not that I can't think of.

> 2-The libntfs-3g0_0.0.0+20061031-5_i386.deb (in src/initramfs/packages)
src/initramfs/packages is not really used at the moment, that is a left over and you can safely remove it. Copy_exec is used instead. That said I might reactivate packages in the future. But if that happens /lupin/src/initramfs/packages will be a clean text file, and the actual packages will be in /lupin/packages. With copy_exec, the binaries are taken from the system that builds the image.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-28
> **ecology2007 said:**
> The update manager just informed me that he wants to make a bigger update than usual and i think i saw something like kernel headers 2.6.20-[COLOR="Black"]**9**[/COLOR]-generic ?
For now i have a kernel 2.6.20-8-generic which i think is one of herd 4.
When i'm gonna generate news initrd.gz and linux, will these headers be used ?
For those of you using feisty, did you also have this message today ?

Herd5 will be out very soon and it will likely be based on 2.6.20-9. So when you build the lupin initrd on an upgraded feisty machine, you will have to use herd5. If you booted with 20-8 and built the initrd with that, you will have to use herd4. If you upgrade a wubi installation based on herd4, even if 20-9 gets installed, you will still be booted with 20-8. It's that simple. I'll try to finish my patches in time for herd5, so the new build will get rid of most of the current error messages.

### Post by ago on 2007-02-28
> **ecology2007 said:**
> Can you for instance launch 2 virtual machines at once
If you have a computer powerful enough why not, I can't... Check if your processor supports KVM ortherwise go for VirtualBox. But I doubt it is needed (see below)

> and make them share files through some kind of magic ?
You can create a shared folder in the host machine. See the VirtualBox user manual. Then each guest can simply mount that folder (if you use VirtualBox you need to install the GuesExtensions).

> ld be for developing where i would let the kernel files frozen to the ones of the herd 4 and the other one would be where i would run the tests.

No need for that in this case. What you do is you keep your source in Edgy (the host) and edit it from edgy. You link the source so that it appears in the shared folder. Then in the virtual machine you link all the lupin folders and files EXCEPT /lupin/build and /lupin/dist. This way you edit the code from the host, then in the guest you build the image, copy the files to the /wubi folder and reboot the guest. If you use feisty on the host, you are better off building on the host and simply copying the files over on the guest.

---

If you used the new installer make sure that menu.lst points to the correct kernel and initrd files within your c:\wubi folder (=same name).

### Post by Hello1024 on 2007-02-28
Since a lot of people seem to have slow downloads, I think I might change the built in downloader to an http one, although I'll leave the torrent source code there, as when the installer gets big, I think the mirror providers might prefer us using torrents if popularity explodes.

By the way, if you want to download the file manually, you want to either download the standard ubuntu one, and put it in the same directory as the installer, and then install.  The installer will put the file in the right place.

Another alternative is to select the iso file manually, using expert mode.  If you leave all the other options untouched, it will do the same default install.

### Post by ecology2007 on 2007-03-01
> **ago said:**
> 

By the way #87833 involves splitting lupin in 2 scripts and it will require to use "mount -t ntfs" as opposed to "ntfs-3g dev mntpoint". Can you please test already whether [COLOR="black"]"mount -t ntfs"[/COLOR] creates problems when used in the initrd? Replace the ntfs-3g within safe-mount in lupin-functions and give it a spin. I was assuming it was the same thing, but after reading tux post I am not that convinced anymore.

The "mount -t ntfs-3g" and "ntfs3-g" commands do the same thing within the safe_mount function.
mount -t ntfs is not the same and use old read only model.

To automount at boot ntfs partitions with /etc/fstab, adding this this line works

/dev/sda2 	/media/extra2/		ntfs-3g	    defaults 0 0
but not this one
/dev/sda2 	/media/extra2/		ntfs	    defaults 0 0

Here the drive is sda (first ide), partition number is 2 and it is mounted on the existing /media/extra2 folder.


Here is my progress report.
I am now at the end of the 1st stage of the boot with a detected partition that i mount in /host, 
so far nothing different for regular wubi, 
after that, i have also some detected folders inside this host and stored in some variables
/wubi/files/root
/wubi/files/home
/wubi/files/extra
/wubi/files/swap

I make folders root home extra swap (using check_mountpoint of lupin) in the ramdisk and mount --bind them accordingly to the ntfs folders.

One nice thing would be if it was possible to bind them to some kind of block like the dev/loops  you do with mknod and losetup, but for ntfs folders.

I'm not worried too much for the swap disk as i intend to use for it the swap.img of wubi.

Is  there a way to use the mount --bind in fstab ? (i'm pretty sure the answer is no)
If not is there a file similar to fstab where i can make a list of bindings ?

Another way to go could be :
Is there a command that makes virtual blocks like the lopsetup does for images files, but for ntfs folders (let s call them usntfs)
Then i would use something like these in fstab
/dev/usntfs1 	/ ntfs-3g	    defaults 0 0
/dev/usntfs2 	/home	ntfs-3g	    defaults 0 0
/dev/usntfs3 	/swap	ntfs-3g	    defaults 0 0 (not swap actually i feel he is pretty good in swap img)

I would try a simpler approach first. You mount /host in the initrd (rw), set the target to /host/wubi/root and perform the installation in there modifying the alternateiso overlay (make sure you do NOT run mkfs). You can worry about booting into the folder later on.

### Post by ago on 2007-03-01
I have just checked the src package in the download section, the 10MB size made me suspicious. That file should contain:

\lupin\src
\lupin\build (empty)
\lupin\dist (empty)
...
\wubi\src
....

And no binary programs. It should be well below 300K, it should be equivalent to use bzr with both projects. I will replace the current src file later on. 

PS1: I do not think we have the right to redistrubute CreateCD. It's not GPL from what I can see and at the moment there is limited need for it, since we do not use Live ISOs.

PS2: The bittorrent downloader is 1.4MB I think we should take it out for now.

### Post by ago on 2007-03-01
mirobe

I need the logs relating to loop7.
Edit c:\menu.lst and make sure you have "debug" (NOT debug=2) in the kernel line. Then reboot. Once at the prompt you will find the new log under /tmp (it's the only file there). You can filter with 

grep loop7 /tmp/?????? (put the log file name in there)

also execute

losetup /dev/loop7

and also

ls /host/wubi/disks/

you can PM me the result

But before re-running the installer, you have to delete and recreate C:\wubi\disks. In particular you need a file called C:\wubi\disks\root.img of 2+GB and one called home.img. Make sure that C: is on an ntfs partition and that there are no other \wubi folders around.
