### Post by MethodOne on 2007-03-19
Instead of using boot.ini, Windows Vista uses the command-line program bcdedit to edit the boot loader, which is very similar to regedit.

### Post by ago on 2007-03-20
> **dbgeezer said:**
> Where do I get the 2.6.20.11 kernel upgrade and how do I accomplish the upgrade within minefield4?  

I can't get into xwindows with the current version.

You should simply make sure you have the kernel that matches wubi is actually installed, for herd5-minefield it is 2.6.20-9. Once that is installed you will be able to use restricted modules. Upgrading the kernel is only required if there are some drivers that you need and are not working in the current kernel. That might be an issue these days, since Feisty is still pre-beta, but it will be far less relevant once Feisty kernel is finalized. Tux is having a look at [https://bugs.launchpad.net/lupin/+bug/87849](https://bugs.launchpad.net/lupin/+bug/87849) .

### Post by Calculoid on 2007-03-21
I ripped a DVD to my HDD to get its free space out of the 60-80GB range and was able to get Wubi installed properly.  I booted into Ubuntu and updated the system, then eventually (2 days later) rebooted and tried to get into Ubuntu again only to have the boot fail at some point and get stuck in a Busy Box shell with an [FONT="Courier New"](initramfs)[/FONT] prompt.

I fixed it and I'm typing this post in Ubuntu now, but I don't know why what I did worked.  Here is what I did:

After several boot attempts failing I went into Windows and read through the logs; they mentioned checking out the* find* command for an error because a file couldn't be found.  This lead me to start playing around with my *c:\menu.lst* file, which was still in the original installed state.

I followed the comments at the top of the *menu.lst* file and changed the line *initrd wubi/initrd.gz* to *initrd wubi/linux* - this caused a kernel panic :).

Next try, I unpacked the *wubi/initrd.gz* file into the wubi directory, then I changed the initrd line to *initrd wubi/initrd* -  When I tried to boot Ubuntu next _it worked_, and here I am.

So I'm not sure why it worked (I am no expert - see kernel panic above), but I thought I'd throw it out here as an FYI.

---

The installation never go past the shell.  The Wubi concept is great but currently the implementation has some kinks.  Looking forward to Wubi when its out of Beta.

### Post by ago on 2007-03-21
> **Calculoid said:**
> I followed the comments at the top of the *menu.lst* file and changed the line *initrd wubi/initrd.gz* to *initrd wubi/linux* - this caused a kernel panic :).

Next try, I unpacked the *wubi/initrd.gz* file into the wubi directory, then I changed the initrd line to *initrd wubi/initrd* -  When I tried to boot Ubuntu next _it worked_, and here I am.

Menu.lst should simply point to an existing kernel AND initrd files, so unless the kernel/initrd was renamed or the menu.lst was changed that should work. In normal circumstances menu.lst should point to 

initrd /wubi/initrd.gz

and you should have a matching file called C:\wubi\initrd.gz. If you rename the file you need to edit menu.lst. The drive letter (C: in the example) is determined by the existance of the file named in the find command. 

For the others, you may want to try [http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/devel/minefield/Wubi-7.04-herd5-minefield4.exe](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/devel/minefield/Wubi-7.04-herd5-minefield4.exe) it is our best shot so far.

---

### Post by Calculoid on 2007-03-21
I used minefield4.

All I was saying was that to get it to work past the failed boot I had to:
[LIST=1]
[*]Unpack the initrd.gz file, 
[*]Change the appropriate line in menu.lst to point to the unpacked initrd file
[*]Reboot and try again.
[/LIST]
The boot loader didn't like the gzipped file for whatever reason.

I just wanted to outline everything I did though, so it took me 6 paragraphs to say it.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-03-21
> **Calculoid said:**
> The boot loader didn't like the gzipped file for whatever reason.

On top of my head there are 2 reasons why that may happen: 1) name mismatch between what is in menu.lst and the actual file name. 2) initrd.gz got fragmented, one side effect of unpacking is that you create a new file which is generally contiguous, moving to a different drive and back would have had the same effect.

### Post by unntrlaffinity on 2007-03-22
> **Keli said:**
> What a crap it is.
Why release somthing with such a shitload of bugs?  

It gave me the option to start up in windows or ubuntu and if I chous ubuntu I have 3 chouses an none of it working.

This would be a great if it was not releasted until it is done because giving people who would like to try Linux crap like this make it stick even faster on windows in the future and won't be interest to try Linux again.

Sad, but true.

It's a maybe-beta piece of software that's three months old using an alpha version of the newest Ubuntu, and they even tell you up front that it's a beta and there are no guarantees.  The release a few versions back worked for me, and I'll admit the recent ones haven't, but if you're using the minefield versions, you can't really get mad since those aren't even available from the main page, you have to look for them.

Anyway, considering it's free, they've been very helpful when it comes to troubleshooting your bugs, if you're patient and willing to ask.

I'm a Windows user who gave Ubuntu a try, and Wubi is the only way I even got it up and running in the first place.  Linux's learning curve is the huge barrier to us newbie Windows users, not alternative installers like this.  I did enjoy it once it was finally up and running, after a lot, a lot of troubleshooting.

### Post by ago on 2007-03-23
As mentioned, I agree with Keli, there have been 3 different problems: 

1) wubi is not yet a finished product, and we are trying to iron out the major bugs, the project is very young. As you can see from this thread, the intended audience at the moment is composed of "testers and deveolopers", not users. 

2) there used to be no distinction between highly experimental builds and "releases" so that the download button used to point to the latest experimental build. Again, this is maybe passable in early prototyping stages but not when a wider audience is involved. This in fact lead to a lot of problems for users. As we get closer to having a "product", the upload policy now has changed and will be far stricter than in the past, the aim is to guarantee that whatever is linked to the upload button on the website will work as well as possible, while experimental builds are be segregated to a "secret" minefield area. At the moment we have not redirected the main button to a new build since I am not satisfied with current builds yet, but it will be fixed very soon, we are quite close indeed, so hopefully people like Keli will have a far easier ride. 

3) The website is admittedly too optimistic, but that was designed with the "finished" product in mind. I decided to open it up before the launch so that other people could help out with the design/translation/etc, but also because the wubi release at the time was relatively stable, there were several regressions due to the proliferation of untested uploads after that. 

In short most of the issues were due to the transition phase from prototypes to product. Most problems will be ironed out this w/e or the next one.

---

### Post by LurkerLinux on 2007-03-23
> **johnwillyums said:**
> 
So I'm hanging in there but what this about Trolls from Frak? Is this some kind of Ubuntu "inspeak".
I don't know what it means but it looks like a dismissive put down and just the sort of thing that will confirm Keli's view for a lot of people. There should be no elitist crap like this on this kind of forum.
johnwillyums

No, troll refers to someone who is negative about something just for the sake of being negative about something. Usually for the purpose of starting an argument. It's not an ubuntu thing, or even a linux thing. It a general net thing having to do with forums and other public areas.

Like you I have had problems getting this to work, but I kept trying every time a new version came out. It finally started working for me with minefield 4. This is experimental software, when you try it, you become part of the experiment. If it does not work, the best thing you can do is try to explain the problem you are having to the best of your ability. Even if you are not very technical.

I'm not part of this project other than being a tester like you. As my name implies, I usually just lurk here. But I felt that I needed to respond to this so that you would not have a misunderstanding.

People who develop software like this do so because they like to do so. Many of them have "real" jobs and families and such, just like you, and they develop on weekend or their free time. Be patient and they will eventually get this where it will work for you.

---

### Post by miami on 2007-03-24
> **cr4zyd said:**
> im having the exact same issue, busybox appears instead of ubuntu, not sure what to look for in these logs but i did find a bunch of

```
Failure: Could not find any file...
    The bootlader is probably pointing to the wrong file/folder
    Please check the 'find' parameter
```

I had this problem and months of getting nowhere I looked through lupin.log and I saw something like the following...

```
...
looking for file /cdrom/dists/feisty
/Release
Warining: Can't find file.
...
```

So I mounted the ISO in Daemon tools and found a file /dists/feisty/Release.

I noticed what seemed to be a line break in the earlier line and after thinking about it a bit, I thought I should resave the preseed.cfg file as a Unix type file and not a DOS type file.

(Something about the $SUITE variable was not being set and was blank)

It worked, no more busybox prompt, which I have been getting for months now and was able to successfully install Wubi/Ubuntu.

Hope this helps someone, enjoy.

---

### Post by cr4zyd on 2007-03-24

```
...
looking for file /cdrom/dists/feisty
/Release
Warining: Can't find file.
...
```

So I mounted the ISO in Daemon tools and found a file /dists/feisty/Release.

I noticed what seemed to be a line break in the earlier line and after thinking about it a bit, I thought I should resave the preseed.cfg file as a Unix type file and not a DOS type file.

(Something about the $SUITE variable was not being set and was blank)

It worked, no more busybox prompt, which I have been getting for months now and was able to successfully install Wubi/Ubuntu.

### Post by Spegelius on 2007-03-25
Well it seems feisty isn't really working with my setup. Some experiences with different installers:
- minefield4: first time i installed it, i got only to initramfs prompt. See posts [http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2326860&postcount=1432](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2326860&postcount=1432) and [http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2328215&postcount=1437](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2328215&postcount=1437)
- minefield 4, second time: For kicks, i wiped everythin clean and re-installed. Can't remember in which order i created disk images and copied wubi-contents to C:\ (minefield installs to d:\, which is RAID 0 and cannot be used). Same access errors, but for my amasement, installation actually started and finished without problems! And it booted to login screen. But still, no good: no mouse and keyboard input, screen freezes completely after minute or so (they do work before graphic portion). I get a noise from inside my pc, which indicates my CPU is heavily loaded (coils resonating to high current flow). Dang.
- mindefield x (4 with initrd and kernel copied from herd4 installation). Same as minefield4 first time.
- beta-minefield1: can't even getr as far as with previous miefields, stops loading after some apic timer has been turned off. After waiting for long time, it finally gets to initramfs, but cannot do anything as keyboard isn't functioning. No access beyond limits-errors, though (maybe it should boot a little further for that).
- Wubi-7.04-herd4-v9.exe: wiped everything clean from previous attempts, installed, booted, installation works right away, but same freeze and high CPU load at login screen. Drat.
- the previously mentioned edgy installation? Works like a champ every time. I have the installation backed up in rar whenever i want play with it again.

Some other impressions:
1. minefield installers should have the exert options, at least i would like the option for selecting the destination and disk image sizes my self. Also cd-image selection would be nice. At the moment installation deduces that best place for wubi is in d:\, which is RAID 0 partition and thus cannot be used for wubi. So i have to move everything to c:\ and create disk images for my own.
2. disk images: they seem to be locked somehow. I understand that they need to be secure, but in a way that even Windows Administrator cannot touch them? I can delete them if i delete disks-folder, but copying or anything else... no dice. No big deal if 'see 1.'.

I did some searching of feisty bugs (mostly the 'can't acces tty...') and it seems that there have been some kernel problems. Maybe it would have been better to develop this with more mature release than feisty, to avoid the problem of distinguishing feisty-related problems from wubi-problems. Still, i can understand the want to delevelop something that works with the latest release...

Anyway, just my eperiences and impressions. It's a nice project and has a lot of potential. I've actually had a lot of fun figuring things out... :). I think i'll try the normal installation, to see if the freeze happens there also.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-03-25
That's possible if the update overwrites /etc/init.d/sendsigs. If you see killall5 in there it has been overwritten. That is a bug that should be corrected upstream. See [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/sysvinit/+bug/87763](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/sysvinit/+bug/87763) 

To fix it, see [http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2300074&postcount=1355](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=2300074&postcount=1355)

---

### Post by ago on 2007-03-26
See: losetup /dev/loop4 
and compare it to: losetup /dev/loop6
They should be the same except that loop4 should point to extra.img
If not look into zenigata.log and see if loop4 was attached to loop4 or not

---

### Post by Aliarse on 2007-03-26
losetup /dev/loop4 gives -
```
/dev/loop4: [0000]:-1208261920 () offset 0, xor encryption
loop: can't get info on device /dev/loop4: No such device or address
```losetup /dev/loop6 gives -
```
/dev/loop6: [0301]:110633 (/host/wubi/disks/home.img)
```And i used this command in windows to create the extra.img, which i then placed in C:\wubi.
```
qemu-img create -f raw extra.img 15000M
```Tried looking for a zenigata.log, but was un-able to find one...

[edit] Found the zenigata.log in the c:\wubi\logs folder, what am i looking for exactly?

---

### Post by ago on 2007-03-26
First see if loop4 is in: ls /dev/loop* 
Note that Qemu does not really allocate the file size and this might create problems. You can copy the file to another partition and back, this will force windows to allocate the disk space.
As for the logs, if you are using a recent version of wubi, you can add the menu.lst parameter "pause_for_debug" and at the prompt type "more /logs/zenigata.log".

PS can you try beta-minefield3 and see if it work? We need some feedback on it.

---

### Post by ago on 2007-03-26
In fact the problem is that extra.img must be in C:\wubi\disks
I missed it when I looked at your image the first time.

### Post by ecology2007 on 2007-03-26
> **dr_punk said:**
> Hey guys,
         I just finished downloading wubi and when i try to install, it says 
"The download was interrupted with the error. HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found"

any ideas? i'm really anxious to have ubuntu on my machine

This happens if something is blocking wubi from accessing the internet. If you know how to configure your firewall, give wubi access to http download.

Else you have to manually download the CD and wubi in the same folder.
[http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/devel/minefield/wubi-7.04-beta-minefield4.exe](http://cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/devel/minefield/wubi-7.04-beta-minefield4.exe)

I've checked and it looks like keyboard and language transfered just fine (but I'm really not sure.) 

Is minefield4 set to install on drive d: by default? (Or was that the drive with the most free space?) I'm guessing that the 'find --set-root /wubi/linux' line in menu.lst checks all the drives in some process I don't know the name of. Does this mean though that I could move the wubi folder to a different drive (via windows!!!), as long at it's at the root of that drive?

Really enjoying the system, and not having to compromise my windows install for it.

One thing I'd like is if anyone knows a way to open the .img files in windows? (Is it possible? i ask because I borked my xorg.conf file and thought just rebooting into windows and being able to get at it from there would be great... but I just googled and found my way through the shell pretty ok.)

I wouldn't be monkeying around in dual monitor heaven under ubuntu though if I didn't feel free to wipe everything out and start over. (Which I had to do a couple of times with earlier wubi versions that didn't quite make the install.) Y'all've opened the windows world up to using Ubuntu with that last release.
:KS :KS :KS

oh man, now I've got a million things to learn about linux. ;)


Oh hey, since this thread is pretty long, I'm adding this:

Get the latest version of Wubi here:
[http://www.cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/latest.html](http://www.cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/latest.html)

There is a built in downloader, but IF it fails, Then manually download the Ubuntu ISO here: (And you then need to run Wubi with the iso and wubi in the same folder.)

[http://releases.ubuntu.com/feisty/ubuntu-7.04-beta-alternate-i386.iso](http://releases.ubuntu.com/feisty/ubuntu-7.04-beta-alternate-i386.iso)

### Post by tomcat23a on 2007-03-27
Kemp: Try your windows username with no password -- perhaps you missed it the first time during install?

Also, install on a 2nd machine spits me out to the GRUB4DOS menu, and there is no way of scrolling up to see what happened. (FYI though, I installed it via a remote desktop connection... perhaps it didn't handle everything properly that way.) I will compare to my working install this afternoon. 

** Update, after booting into windows and editing my menu.lst and saving it again gldr picked it up and it worked just fine. Not sure what it was, and I am doubtful it was an edit that I did. ### Post by Kemp on 2007-03-27
Ok, found out what happened. It automatically picks up your windows login and enters that as the default for your username, and it ignores any changes you make during installation, that's fair enough for a beta I guess. It **does**, however, pick up the password you enter. Slightly inconsistent behaviour there.

---

It **does**, however, pick up the password you enter. Slightly inconsistent behaviour there.

### Post by ago on 2007-03-27

### Post by cro on 2007-03-27
Quite by accident I found an interesting side-effect with the current beta installer (wubi-7.04-beta.exe - 18:40 upload)

If you have ubuntu-7.04-beta-alternate-i386.iso in the same directory, it will detect and use it *unless* you have another ubuntu ISO (or, i guess, any file beginning with the string 'ubuntu') that is older or appears in the requested directory file list before the 7.04 file.

I had "ubuntu-6.10-desktop-i386.iso" in the same folder, and the installer wouldn't see 7.04 until I renamed 6.10 to "xubuntu-6.10-desktop-i386.iso".

---

### Post by ecology2007 on 2007-03-27
> **cro said:**
> Quite by accident I found an interesting side-effect with the current beta installer (wubi-7.04-beta.exe - 18:40 upload)

Fix commited, thanks for the report.

### Post by Oedipe on 2007-03-29
Hi,

> **dbgeezer said:**
> 
Now on to the NTFS side, so that I can read my drive.  


By the way, is ntfs-3g absolutely safe now, and included in the Wubi distribution/automatic installer? Or should we install that ?

I understand that in any case, we can't work with wubi/ubuntu on files in the same ntfs/windows partition where Wubi is installed... and that's a big problem for novice people who wants to try Linux... :(

Regards,

---

### Post by ago on 2007-03-29
ntfs-3g has gone through extensive tests and should be considered safe, it is used by wubi, but it is not installed by default in Ubuntu. You have to installa it separately (after you set up networking). See [http://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide](http://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide)

---

### Post by ago on 2007-03-29
> **bconver1 said:**
> I was able to move everything except the disks folder, with home.img, root.img and swap.img. I got an error saying they may be in use by another program.  But I decided to try and boot again just to see if anything else had changed, and this time, the wubi/linux folder was found, and the installation went a little father than previously, but then I was left with a prompt on a blank screen.

You need C:\wubi\disks with anything in there, trying to boot without that folder is equivalent to trying to but your PC with hard disks disconnected. If for any reason you cannot move it from its previous location, copy it over.

---

### Post by ecology2007 on 2007-03-29
> **bconver1 said:**
>   Any help would be greatly appreciated, however, keep in mind that I am pretty new at this, and I may be easily confused by complicated responses. :)



The best solution is :
Wait for a new revision. Wubi is supposed to work with zero configuration, if it does not you will not solve it without spending a lot of time.

Just tell us :
1-if you see a ubuntu-7.04-alternate-i386-beta.iso in g:/wubi/install/.
2-if your drive has anything special (usb, firewire, raid, vista, etc ...)
3-if you have several wubi folders spread among your drives.

---

### Post by chrisk11 on 2007-03-29
I've been trying to install wubi with a few different versions. I tried wubi herd 4, minefield4 and beta but I had no success. I'm trying beta again now. Last time I got these errors.

[   13.527611] Uhhuh NMI received for unknown reason 2d on CPU 0.

### Post by ecology2007 on 2007-03-29
> **dfajar2 said:**
> Ok. i delete c:\wubi and reinstall it.

now, i am having the same problem as before where i have the "Load installer components from CD " error.:confused: 
 which option should i select? iso-scan, load-iso, etc?
Thanks again


You don't have to select any option. You don't have to rename anything or do anything by yourself.
If you are sure you downloaded this iso, and if wubi tells you that it has detected it on the very first page, it will use it and copy it to c:\wubi\install. If it does not, you don't have the good files, download them again in the same folder.


[http://www.cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/latest.html](http://www.cutlersoftware.com/ubuntusetup/latest.html)
[http://releases.ubuntu.com/feisty/ubuntu-7.04-beta-alternate-i386.iso](http://releases.ubuntu.com/feisty/ubuntu-7.04-beta-alternate-i386.iso)


Then it will work on next reboot.
